**Sensor Calibration:** Vibration feedback in haptic devices must be scaled logarithmically to match human perception. - **Alarm Design:** Increasing urgency (e.g., beeping rate, pitch) logarithmically produces the strongest "alarming" perception. --- **(b) Numerical Problem — Stevens' Power Law Application:** **Given:** $I_1 = 40$ dB (baseline), $I_2 = 80$ dB (increased), $k = 0.5$, $n = 0.67$, $I_0 \approx 0$ dB (assumed negligible). **Step 1 — Calculate Perceived Loudness at $I_1 = 40$ dB:** $$S_1 = 0.5 \cdot (40)^{0.67}$$ Computing the power: $$40^{0.67} = e^{0.67 \cdot \ln 40} = e^{0.67 \cdot 3.689} = e^{2.472} \approx 11.85$$ Therefore: $$S_1 \approx 0.5 \cdot 11.85 = 5.93 \text{ units}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate Perceived Loudness at $I_2 = 80$ dB:** $$S_2 = 0.5 \cdot (80)^{0.67}$$ Computing the power: $$80^{0.67} = e^{0.67 \cdot \ln 80} = e^{0.67 \cdot 4.382} = e^{2.936} \approx 18.84$$ Therefore: $$S_2 \approx 0.5 \cdot 18.84 = 9.42 \text{ units}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the Perceptual Increase:** $$\Delta S = S_2 - S_1 = 9.42 - 5.93 = 3.49 \text{ units}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the Percentage Perceptual Increase:** $$\dfrac{\Delta S}{S_1} \times 100\% = \dfrac{3.49}{5.93} \times 100\% \approx 58.9\%$$ **Step 5 — Comments on Perceptual Implications:** - The physical intensity was **doubled** (40 dB → 80 dB), but the perceived loudness increased by only **~59%**. - This is consistent with $n = 0.67 < 1$, indicating **compressed perception** of loudness. - **HMI Implication:** Designers must increase physical alarm volume by a much larger factor than they want perceived urgency to rise. A linear mapping would make alarms feel disproportionately quiet at high intensities, defeating their warning purpose. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing Weber, Fechner, and Stevens:** Students often mix up the three laws. **Mnemonic:** *W*eber → *W*hole ratio (ΔI/I); *F*echner → *F*ragmented log; *S*tevens → *S*mooth power. > 2. **Forgetting to include $I_0$:** Stevens' Law requires $(I - I_0)$, not $I$. When $I_0$ is negligible (as in the worked examples), state the assumption clearly. > 3. **Wrong base for logarithm:** Fechner's Law is normally written in base 10 for engineering; base $e$ (natural log) is mathematically equivalent but uses a different constant. > 4. **Interpreting $n$ incorrectly:** $n > 1$ does NOT mean "more sensitive" universally — it means perception grows *faster* than the stimulus (relevant for pain/shock). > 5. **Mixing up iconic and echoic memory:** Iconic = visual (250 ms); Echoic = auditory (~3 s). Examiners frequently test this distinction. > 6. **Confusing recognition with perception:** Perception = organizing raw input; Recognition = identifying what it is. Both are needed for HMI success. > 7. **Skipping units in the formula sheet:** Always state the units of $S$ and $I$ in numerical answers (e.g., *sensation units*, *dB*). --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Human Perception and Recognition** - ☐ **Perception = organization of sensory input; Recognition = identification against memory.** - ☐ The information processing pipeline is: **Stimulus → Sensory Memory → Attention → Working Memory (7±2) → Pattern Recognition → LTM → Response.** - ☐ **Sensory Memory types:** Iconic (visual, ~250 ms), Echoic (auditory, ~3 s), Haptic (touch, ~1 s). - ☐ **Gestalt Laws** (must know all six): Proximity, Similarity, Closure, Continuity, Figure-Ground, Common Fate. - ☐ **Three pattern recognition models:** Template (rigid), Prototype (flexible-average), Feature Analysis (powerful, AI-based). - ☐ **Weber's Law:** $\dfrac{\Delta I}{I} = k$ — the JND is a constant fraction. - ☐ **Fechner's Law:** $S = k' \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ — logarithmic perception (used for audio taper potentiometers). - ☐ **Stevens' Power Law:** $S = k \cdot (I - I_0)^{n}$ — power-function perception. - ☐ **Stevens' exponent $n$** is modality-specific: $n < 1$ for brightness/loudness, $n \approx 1$ for length, $n > 1$ for pain/shock. - ☐ **Signal Detection Theory:** separates $d'$ (sensitivity) from $\beta$ (bias). - ☐ **Top-down processing** uses prior knowledge; **Bottom-up** uses raw sensory data. - ☐ **Miller's Law:** Working memory holds **7 ± 2 chunks** — limit display elements. - ☐ **Reaction times:** Visual ~250 ms, Auditory ~170 ms, Tactile ~155 ms. - ☐ **HMI Design Implications:** Use Gestalt closure for icon design; respect Weber/Fechner/Stevens for alarm scaling; respect 7±2 for menu design. - ☐ **Mental Models:** Users form internal conceptual models of how a system works — good HMI design aligns the system image with the user's mental model (Norman's principle).

topics.title: Psychology of Users
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT753 - HUMAN-MACHINE INTERFACE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Psychology of Users ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (HMI - Psychology of Users)** > *Psychology of Users* in Human-Machine Interaction (HMI) refers to the systematic study of human cognitive, perceptual, and behavioral characteristics that govern how a person perceives, processes, stores, retrieves, and acts upon information presented by an interactive system. It forms the **scientific foundation** of user-centered design (UCD) and ensures that interfaces match human mental capabilities rather than forcing humans to adapt to machine logic. ### 1.1 The Formal Academic Definition In the context of HMI, **user psychology** is an applied branch of cognitive psychology that investigates the **internal mental states** of users (beliefs, expectations, motivations, memory load) and maps them to **external interface behaviors** (keystrokes, gaze patterns, error rates, response times). The discipline explicitly models the user as an *information-processing system* with bounded cognitive resources such as: - A **limited short-term (working) memory** capacity of approximately **7 ± 2 chunks** (Miller, 1956). - An **unlimited long-term memory** organized through **schemas** and **mental models**. - A **selective attention filter** that bottlenecks sensory inflow. - A **perceptual apparatus** that organizes raw stimuli using innate **Gestalt grouping laws**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Every design decision in HMI — button placement, color, menu depth, feedback, error messages — is a *direct consequence* of a psychological principle. Examiners frequently ask: *"Justify this design rule with a psychological principle."* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen Imagine a **restaurant** where a customer (the user) places an order (input) with a **waiter** (the interface), who must communicate it to a **chef** (the processor), who prepares the dish (output) and returns it through the waiter to the customer. - The customer cannot "teleport" wishes directly into the kitchen — they must use the **menu** (interface vocabulary), speak in the **waiter's language** (input modality), and remember only a few items at a time (**short-term memory**). - If the menu is in French but the customer speaks English (**mismatched mental model**), orders fail — this is a *usability error* caused by *psychology of the user being ignored*. - If 200 dishes are listed on one page (**cognitive overload**), the customer freezes — this is the **Miller 7±2** violation. - If the chef remembers the customer's previous allergies (**long-term memory / user profile**), the experience improves — this is *personalization through user psychology*. The **HMI designer** is the architect who designs the menu, the waiter, and the kitchen flow **based on how customers actually think** — not how the chef wishes they would think. > [!TIP] > A useful mnemonic for exam writing: **PAMI** → **P**erception → **A**ttention → **M**emory → **I**ntelligence/Mental Models. These are the four pillars of user psychology in HMI. ### 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Constants to Memorize | Metric / Constant | Symbol | Standard Value | Design Implication | |---|---|---|---| | Short-term memory capacity | $\mu$ | **7 ± 2 chunks** | Limit menu items, group options | | Absolute judgment limit | $k$ | **~5–9 levels** | Don't show 50 shades of grey | | Hick's Law coefficient | $b$ | ~**100–250 ms/bit** | More choices = slower decisions | | Fitts' Law index of difficulty | $ID$ | bits | Target size vs. distance | | Eye fixation duration | $T_f$ | **200–300 ms** | Visual scanning budget | | Foveal vision angle | $\theta_f$ | **~2°** | Need for "above the fold" content | | Adult reading speed | $R$ | **~250 wpm** | Text chunking strategy | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > Module 1 of HMI is **theory-heavy**. The KTU 2024 scheme expects you to be able to *describe*, *contrast*, and *apply* each psychological concept to interface design scenarios. Memorize the formulas below — they are repeated every year. ### 2.1 The Three Pillars of User Psychology #### Pillar 1 — Perception Perception is the brain's *active construction* of reality from raw sensory data. It is **not** a passive recording. The HMI designer must exploit perceptual laws (Gestalt) so users *automatically* see the correct grouping. **The Gestalt Laws of Perceptual Organization (HCI-critical subset):** - **Law of Proximity** — Elements close together are perceived as a group. *Application:* Grouping form fields with consistent spacing. - **Law of Similarity** — Elements sharing visual features (color, shape) are perceived as related. *Application:* Highlighting all disabled buttons the same way. - **Law of Closure** — The mind fills in missing information. *Application:* Skeleton screens during loading. - **Law of Continuity** — Smooth, continuous patterns are preferred over abrupt changes. *Application:* Progress bars. - **Law of Figure-Ground** — Distinguishing object from background. *Application:* Modal dialogs that dim the background. - **Law of Common Region** — Elements within the same enclosed region are grouped. *Application:* Card-based UIs. #### Pillar 2 — Attention Attention is the selective concentration of cognitive resources on one aspect of the environment while ignoring others. **Key properties:** - **Selective** — Cannot attend to two simultaneous high-load streams (cocktail party effect). - **Limited** — Only ~±1 conscious item at a time. - **Effortful** — Sustained attention depletes mental energy. - **Bottom-up (exogenous)** — Driven by salient stimuli (motion, color, contrast). - **Top-down (endogenous)** — Driven by user's goals and expectations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Design rule derived from attention:** Use **signifiers** (arrows, highlights) to direct top-down attention, and **contrast / motion** sparingly for bottom-up attention. Over-stimulation causes *attention hijacking* and error. #### Pillar 3 — Memory The most heavily tested concept in KTU HMI exams. Memory is treated as a **three-stage store** (Atkinson-Shiffrin Model). **The Three Memory Stores:** | Store | Capacity | Duration | Coding | HMI Analogy | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Sensory Register** | Very large (all senses) | **0.25–2 seconds** | Raw sensory | A camera buffer | | **Short-Term Memory (STM) / Working Memory** | **7 ± 2 chunks** | **15–30 seconds** (without rehearsal) | Acoustic / visual | RAM of the brain | | **Long-Term Memory (LTM)** | Effectively unlimited | **Minutes to lifetime** | Semantic, episodic, procedural | Hard disk | **Chunking** — Miller's 1956 paper established that STM stores *chunks* (meaningful units), not raw items. A chunk could be a single letter, a word, or an entire phrase. The *magic number 7 ± 2* refers to chunks, not characters. Designers exploit this by: - Grouping digits in phone numbers: `98765-43210` (2 chunks vs. 10 digits). - Replacing "1,000,000,000" with "$1B". - Using icons (single chunks) instead of long labels. #### Pillar 4 — Mental Models A **mental model** is the user's internal, simplified representation of how a system works. It is built from experience, training, and analogy. > [!WARNING] > The user's mental model is **never** identical to the designer's conceptual model. The *goal* of HMI is to make the user's mental model **match** the designer's model through: > - **Visibility** of system state. > - **Good mappings** (Norman). > - **Affordances** (Norman). > - **Constraints** that prevent invalid actions. > - **Feedback** for every action. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!TIP] > Print this section. Every numerical HMI problem in KTU reduces to one of these four equations. | # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables Explained | Units | Used For | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Miller's Law (memory chunks)** | $C = 7 \pm 2$ | $C$ = chunks in STM | chunks | Menu sizing, list length | | 2 | **Hick's Law (choice reaction time)** | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ | $T$ = decision time, $n$ = choices, $a,b$ = constants | seconds | Predicting navigation time | | 3 | **Fitts' Law (pointing time)** | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | $D$ = distance, $W$ = target width | seconds | Button size & placement | | 4 | **Stevens' Power Law (perceived intensity)** | $\psi(I) = k \cdot I^{n}$ | $\psi$ = perceived magnitude, $I$ = stimulus intensity | varies | Slider/progress mapping | | 5 | **Memory decay (Ebbinghaus)** | $R = e^{-t/S}$ | $R$ = retention, $t$ = time, $S$ = memory strength | dimensionless | Recall after delay | | 6 | **Throughput (GOMS Model)** | $T_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$ | $T_i$ = time per operation | seconds | Task time prediction | **Critical boundary conditions to memorize:** - $n$ in Hick's Law must be $\geq 1$ (use $+1$ to avoid $\log_2(1) = 0$). - In Fitts' Law, $W > 0$ always. - $n$ in Stevens' Law is **< 1 for brightness** (compressed perception), **> 1 for electric shock** (expanded perception). - $ID = \log_2(D/W + 1)$ is the **Index of Difficulty** in bits. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Avionics & Medical Devices:** Miller's 7±2 dictates the maximum number of items on a single cockpit display — exceeding it has caused fatal accidents (e.g., Therac-25). - **Automotive HMI:** Fitts' Law justifies large, close-to-driver touch targets and voice control fallbacks. - **Web & Mobile UX:** Hick's Law explains why flat design (fewer choices) outperforms nested menus. - **Gaming & VR:** Stevens' Power Law calibrates haptic feedback intensity to feel "natural". - **Accessibility Engineering:** Gestalt laws inform screen-reader spatialization for blind users. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > The derivations below are the *exact* ones KTU examiners expect in 14-mark Part-B answers. Practice writing them on a single sheet of paper within 8 minutes. ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hick's Law (Choice Reaction Time) **Hick's Law** states that the time taken to make a decision is a logarithmic function of the number of available choices. It is derived from **Information Theory (Shannon, 1948)**. #### Step 1 — Define the Information Content of a Choice By Shannon's definition, the information (in bits) required to identify one item out of $n$ equally probable alternatives is: $$H = \log_2(n)$$ #### Step 2 — Adjust for the Case $n = 1$ When $n = 1$, the user has *no choice* (one path) and theoretically $H = 0$. However, real users still spend time confirming the single action. We therefore modify the formula to: $$H = \log_2(n + 1)$$ #### Step 3 — Map Information to Reaction Time Empirically, the time to process one bit of information is approximately constant (for a given user and modality). Let this constant be $b$ seconds/bit. Additionally, there is a fixed motor preparation time $a$ (the time to *initiate* a response, independent of choice). $$T = a + b \cdot H$$ #### Step 4 — Substitute to Obtain Hick's Law Substituting Step 2 into Step 3: $$T(n) = a + b \cdot \log_2(n + 1)$$ #### Step 5 — Practical Worked Example A mobile app menu has $n = 7$ items. Empirical constants are $a = 100$ ms and $b = 150$ ms/bit. Calculate the expected decision time. $$T(7) = 100 + 150 \cdot \log_2(7 + 1)$$ $$\log_2(8) = 3$$ $$T(7) = 100 + 150 \cdot 3 = 100 + 450 = 550 \text{ ms}$$ **Interpretation:** Reducing the menu to 4 items gives: $$\log_2(5) \approx 2.322$$ $$T(4) = 100 + 150 \cdot 2.322 \approx 448.3 \text{ ms}$$ A **~18% reduction** in decision time by simply halving the menu size — this is why HMI designers cap menus at 5–7 items. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fitts' Law (Pointing / Selection Time) Fitts' Law (1954) models the time to *rapidly move to a target area* — fundamental for mouse, touch, and stylus interaction. #### Step 1 — Define Index of Difficulty (ID) The difficulty of hitting a target depends on two spatial factors: how **far away** it is ($D$) and how **wide** it is ($W$). The dimensionless difficulty in bits is: $$ID = \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$$ The **+1** prevents division-by-zero and matches the empirical Shannon formulation (same form as Hick's Law). #### Step 2 — Linear Movement-Time Law Empirically, the movement time is **linear in information processed** (Fitts observed this with a two-tap disk-transfer task). With intercept $a$ and slope $b$: $$T = a + b \cdot ID$$ #### Step 3 — Combine to Get Fitts' Law $$T(D, W) = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$$ The constant $b$ is the *index of performance* (measured in **bits/second**); higher $b$ means a faster user. #### Step 4 — Numerical Example A button of width $W = 40$ px is located $D = 200$ px from the user's current cursor position. Constants: $a = 50$ ms, $b = 100$ ms/bit. $$ID = \log_2\!\left(\frac{200}{40} + 1\right) = \log_2(6) \approx 2.585 \text{ bits}$$ $$T = 50 + 100 \cdot 2.585 = 308.5 \text{ ms}$$ #### Step 5 — Design Rule Derivation Differentiating $T$ w.r.t. $W$ (treating $D$ fixed): $$\frac{\partial T}{\partial W} = -\frac{b}{W \cdot \ln 2}$$ A **larger $W$ always reduces $T$**, and the marginal benefit is *largest* for small buttons. **Design rule:** Doubling the width of a small button reduces movement time more than doubling the width of an already-large button. This is why macOS uses *Aqua* oversized scrollbars and why touch targets should be $\geq 44 \times 44$ px (Apple HIG). --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — GOMS Throughput Calculation The **GOMS model** (Goals, Operators, Methods, Selection rules — Card, Moran & Newell, 1983) predicts total task time as a sum of *primitive operator times*. #### Step 1 — Identify the Lowest-Level Operators Standard operators include: - $K$ = Keystroke (avg **0.12 s** for skilled typist, 0.28 s novice). - $P$ = Pointing with mouse (use Fitts' Law). - $H$ = Homing hand between devices (**0.40 s**). - $M$ = Mental preparation (**1.35 s**). - $R$ = System response (user must wait, often ignored in model). #### Step 2 — Decompose the Task *Task:* "Open a file `report.txt` in Notepad by typing its name." Methods: 1. Mental prep (think about filename) → $1 \times M$ 2. Click on filename input box → $1 \times P$ 3. Type "report.txt" (10 chars) → $10 \times K$ 4. Press Enter → $1 \times K$ #### Step 3 — Sum the Operator Times $$T_{total} = M + P + 10K + K = 1.35 + 0.30 + 10(0.12) + 0.12 = 1.35 + 0.30 + 1.20 + 0.12 = 2.97 \text{ s}$$ #### Step 4 — The Keystroke-Level Model (KLM) Variant Card, Moran & Newell simplified GOMS into the **Keystroke-Level Model (KLM)** where only $K$, $P$, $H$, $M$, $R$ are summed: $$T_{execute} = T_K + T_P + T_H + T_M + T_R$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > For KTU exams: If the question gives operator times in a table, simply sum them in the order of execution. **Marks are awarded for showing the substitution**, not for re-deriving the operator list. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Psychology ↔ Interface Design Mapping | Psychological Principle | Cognitive Limit Exploited | Interface Design Application | Risk if Ignored | |---|---|---|---| | Miller's 7 ± 2 | STM chunk capacity | Limit menu items, chunk phone numbers | Cognitive overload, decision paralysis | | Hick's Law | Logarithmic choice cost | Group items, use progressive disclosure | Slow task completion | | Fitts' Law | Movement vs. target size | Large touch targets near user's focus | Missed taps, motor fatigue | | Gestalt Laws | Perceptual grouping | Group related controls, use whitespace | Misunderstood UI structure | | Stevens' Power Law | Perceptual non-linearity | Use logarithmic sliders (volume, brightness) | Poor sense of control | | Ebbinghaus Decay | Forgetting curve | Re-show context, breadcrumbs, undo | Lost-in-navigation errors | | Mental Models | User's prior knowledge | Use real-world metaphors (folder, trash) | User forms wrong expectations | | Attention Bottleneck | Selective perception | Animate, highlight, signpost | Critical info missed | | Long-Term Memory Schemas | Recognition > Recall | Show options (icons) rather than ask (type) | Increased memory load | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Human Information Processor (HIP) — Card, Moran & Newell Model The foundational model of user psychology in HMI. It treats the user as a three-stage processor plus a **motor** and a **long-term memory** feed. ```mermaid flowchart LR envA[External Environment] -->|Sensory Input| senA[Sensory Registers
Visual / Auditory / Haptic] senA -->|Attention Filter| stmA[Working Memory
STM - 7 plus or minus 2 chunks] stmA -->|Encoding| ltmA[Long Term Memory
Unlimited - Schemas] ltmA -.->|Retrieval| stmA stmA -->|Decision| cogA[Cognitive Processor
Match - Compare - Decide] cogA -->|Motor Command| motA[Motor System
Hands - Eyes - Voice] motA -->|Action| envA senA -.->|Raw Buffer| stmA cogA -.->|Retrieval| ltmA ``` **How to read this diagram for a KTU answer:** 1. *Input flow* (solid arrows): Environment → Sensory → STM → Cognitive → Motor → Environment. 2. *Lateral feed* (dashed arrows): LTM constantly supplies schemas to STM and the cognitive processor — this is why **recognition beats recall**. 3. *Bottleneck* is at STM (the 7 ± 2 choke-point). ### 4.2 Memory Hierarchy & Information Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB subA[Sensory Memory
Capacity: Massive
Duration: 0.25 to 2 s
Coding: Raw] subB[Short Term Memory
Capacity: 7 plus or minus 2
Duration: 15 to 30 s
Coding: Acoustic and Visual] subC[Long Term Memory
Capacity: Unlimited
Duration: Years
Coding: Semantic and Procedural] subA -->|Attention| subB subB -->|Rehearsal| subB subB -->|Encoding| subC subC -.->|Retrieval Cue| subB ``` **Key flow properties:** - **Rehearsal loop** in STM prevents decay (this is why repeating a phone number helps you remember it). - **Encoding** into LTM requires *deep processing* (semantic, meaningful) — this is why meaningful icons are remembered better than arbitrary symbols. - **Retrieval** is *cue-dependent* — give the user a recognizable cue (a label, an icon) and LTM responds. ### 4.3 Fitts' Law — Design Implications Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB goalA[Design Goal: Minimize Pointing Time] --> paramA[Input Parameters: Distance D and Width W] paramA --> calcA[Compute ID equals log2 of D over W plus 1] calcA --> optA{D is fixed by layout?} optA -->|Yes| optB[Increase W - make button larger] optA -->|No| optC[Decrease D - move button closer to focus] optB --> outA[Reduced T - faster acquisition] optC --> outA outA --> guidelineA[KTU Design Guidelines: 44x44 px touch targets, 8 px edge clearance, infinite scroll edges, large close buttons] ``` ### 4.4 GOMS / KLM Execution Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR g1[User Goal] --> m1[Select Method via Selection Rules] m1 --> s1[Sub-Goal 1] s1 --> op1[Operator: Mental Prep M] op1 --> op2[Operator: Point P] op2 --> op3[Operator: Keystroke K] op3 --> op4[Operator: Homing H] op4 --> s2{Sub-Goal Done?} s2 -->|No| s1 s2 -->|Yes| sum1[Sum All Operator Times] sum1 --> out1[Total Task Time in seconds] ``` This is the *exact* block architecture that KTU questions on GOMS expect you to draw when asked to *"explain how a GOMS model estimates user task time."* ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > The questions below mirror the **actual KTU 2024 scheme assessment pattern**: Part A short-answer (3 marks) and Part B with internal choice (14 marks total, split into 7+7 sub-parts). Tag indicates simulated past-paper origin. ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define the term "Mental Model" in the context of Human-Machine Interaction. Why is it important for an HMI designer to align the system image with the user's mental model?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **mental model** is the user's internal, simplified understanding of how a system works, built from prior experience, training, and analogies (1 mark). In HMI, Don Norman's framework identifies three models: - The **designer's model** — what the designer intends. - The **system image** — what the interface actually conveys. - The **user's model** — what the user believes. If the system image does not clearly communicate the designer's model, the user's mental model will diverge, leading to **errors, frustration, and abandonment** (1 mark). Alignment is achieved through **visibility, feedback, good mappings, affordances, and constraints** (1 mark). --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **State Miller's Law. Explain with an example how the concept of "chunking" is used in interface design to overcome the limitations of short-term memory.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Miller's Law (1956) states that the capacity of short-term (working) memory is approximately **$7 \pm 2$ chunks** of information (1 mark). A *chunk* is a meaningful unit — it can be a digit, a word, or a familiar pattern (1 mark). **Example:** Indian mobile numbers have 10 digits. Displayed as `9876543210` they exceed STM. Displayed as `98765 43210` they form **2 chunks** of 5 digits each, well within STM capacity, and can be remembered for re-entry (1 mark). --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Discuss the **three-stage model of human memory** (Atkinson-Shiffrin). For each stage, state its **capacity**, **duration**, and **coding format**, and give **one HMI design implication** for that stage. (7 marks) **(b)** Explain **Fitts' Law** with its mathematical formulation. A button is placed **200 pixels** away from the user's current cursor and has a width of **40 pixels**. Using constants $a = 50$ ms and $b = 100$ ms/bit, calculate the movement time. Hence, derive **two design rules** from Fitts' Law for touch interfaces. (7 marks) --- #### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A(a) [7 marks] The **Atkinson-Shiffrin three-stage model** (1968) divides human memory into three structurally distinct stores (1 mark): **1. Sensory Register** - **Capacity:** Very large — captures *all* raw sensory data from vision, hearing, touch. (1 mark) - **Duration:** ~0.25 to 2 seconds. Without attention, the trace decays rapidly. (½ mark) - **Coding:** Raw sensory (iconic for vision, echoic for audition). (½ mark) - **HMI implication:** Designers must capture attention *within ~250 ms* of content rendering, or the user will miss the information. Examples: splash-screen brand marks, loading skeletons, brief flash messages. (1 mark) **2. Short-Term Memory (Working Memory)** - **Capacity:** **7 ± 2 chunks** (Miller, 1956). (1 mark) - **Duration:** 15–30 seconds without rehearsal. (½ mark) - **Coding:** Primarily *acoustic* (verbal) and *visual*; recent research shows it is a multi-modal workspace, not a single store. (½ mark) - **HMI implication:** Use **chunking** to pack information — display phone numbers in pairs, group menu items by category, replace numeric IDs with meaningful icons. (1 mark) **3. Long-Term Memory (LTM)** - **Capacity:** Effectively unlimited (lifetime). (½ mark) - **Duration:** Minutes to a lifetime. (½ mark) - **Coding:** *Semantic* (meaning), *episodic* (events), *procedural* (skills). (½ mark) - **HMI implication:** Exploit LTM's preference for **recognition over recall** — provide visible menus, icons, and dropdowns so users recognize options rather than generate them from memory. (1 mark) --- #### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A(b) [7 marks] **Fitts' Law** (Paul Fitts, 1954) is one of the most robust quantitative models in HMI. It predicts the **time to rapidly acquire (point to and select) a target** based on the target's distance and size. (1 mark) **Mathematical formulation:** $$T = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$$ where $T$ is the movement time in seconds, $D$ is the distance from the starting point to the target's center, $W$ is the width of the target along the axis of motion, and $a$ and $b$ are empirically determined constants (intercept and slope). The term $\log_2(D/W + 1)$ is the **Index of Difficulty (ID)** measured in bits, and $b$ is the **Index of Performance** measured in bits/second. (2 marks) **Calculation** with $D = 200$ px, $W = 40$ px, $a = 50$ ms, $b = 100$ ms/bit: $$ID = \log_2\!\left(\frac{200}{40} + 1\right) = \log_2(6)$$ $$\log_2(6) = \frac{\ln 6}{\ln 2} = \frac{1.7918}{0.6931} \approx 2.585 \text{ bits}$$ $$T = 50 + 100 \cdot 2.585 = 50 + 258.5 = 308.5 \text{ ms}$$ (2 marks) **Two design rules derived from Fitts' Law:** **Rule 1 — Maximize target size $W$:** Because $T$ decreases as $\log(W)$ increases, enlarging buttons reduces acquisition time. This justifies the Apple Human Interface Guideline that **touch targets must be at least 44 × 44 px**, and the iOS convention of placing the back/close button in an oversized corner where the thumb naturally rests. (1 mark) **Rule 2 — Minimize distance $D$, and bring targets to the user's focus point:** Place frequently used controls **close to the user's current cursor or thumb position** (e.g., bottom-center of mobile screens, near the current focus in a form). This is why *infinite scroll* pages place "Add to Cart" or "Like" buttons at the screen edge — the user is already near them. (1 mark) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - **Do NOT** write the formula as $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W)$. The "+1" is **mandatory** — losing it costs ½ mark. > - **Always state the units** of $T$ (seconds) and $ID$ (bits). Examiners deduct ½ mark if units are missing. > - **Mention Index of Difficulty and Index of Performance by name** — these are KTU-favorite keywords. --- **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** With a **neat diagram**, explain the **Human Information Processor (HIP) model** by Card, Moran and Newell. Identify the **bottleneck** stage and justify your answer with a psychological principle. (7 marks) **(b)** State and explain **Hick's Law**. A smartphone Settings menu has **8 options**. If the decision constants are $a = 120$ ms and $b = 180$ ms/bit, calculate the expected decision time. If the menu is reduced to **4 options**, what is the percentage reduction in time? Comment on the **design implication**. (7 marks) --- #### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B(a) [7 marks] The **Human Information Processor (HIP)** model, proposed by **Card, Moran, and Newell (1983)**, is a foundational cognitive architecture for HMI. It treats the user as an *information-processing system* composed of three interacting memory stores, a processor, and a motor system. (1 mark) **Stages of the model (with diagram):** 1. **Sensory Registers:** Receive raw input from the external environment via visual, auditory, and haptic channels. Hold it briefly (0.25–2 s) before either decay or transfer to working memory. (1 mark) 2. **Working Memory (Short-Term Memory):** A limited-capacity buffer of **7 ± 2 chunks** (Miller) holding information for **15–30 seconds** without rehearsal. (1 mark) 3. **Long-Term Memory:** An effectively unlimited store of schemas, procedures, and episodic knowledge, organized semantically. (1 mark) 4. **Cognitive Processor:** Performs matching, comparison, and decision-making operations on the contents of working memory, drawing upon LTM via retrieval. (1 mark) 5. **Motor System:** Executes physical actions (hand, eye, voice) that act on the environment, closing the loop. (1 mark) ``` [Environment] → [Sensory] → [Working Memory] → [Cognitive] → [Motor] → [Environment] ↑ ↑ └── Long-Term Memory ──┘ ``` **Bottleneck identification:** The bottleneck is the **Working Memory (STM)** stage. (1 mark) **Justification:** According to **Miller's Law (1956)**, working memory can hold only **$7 \pm 2$ chunks** of information at any moment. This structural limit — not a software issue, but a neurobiological constraint — is what restricts the rate of information flow through the entire cognitive system. Every design choice that minimizes STM load (chunking, recognition over recall, progressive disclosure) directly addresses this bottleneck. (1 mark) --- #### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B(b) [7 marks] **Hick's Law** (William Hick, 1952) states that the time taken for a person to make a decision is a **logarithmic function of the number of available choices**. (1 mark) **Mathematical formulation:** $$T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n + 1)$$ where $T$ is the decision time in seconds, $n$ is the number of equally probable choices, and $a$ and $b$ are empirically determined constants (the +1 ensures $\log_2(1+1) = 1$ for the two-choice baseline, avoiding $\log_2(0)$). (1 mark) **Derivation note:** Hick's Law is derived from **Shannon's Information Theory** — the number of bits of information required to identify one item out of $n$ equally likely alternatives is $\log_2(n)$; Hick showed reaction time is linear in this information content. (1 mark) **Calculation for $n = 8$, $a = 120$ ms, $b = 180$ ms/bit:** $$T(8) = 120 + 180 \cdot \log_2(8 + 1) = 120 + 180 \cdot \log_2(9)$$ $$\log_2(9) = \frac{\ln 9}{\ln 2} = \frac{2.1972}{0.6931} \approx 3.170$$ $$T(8) = 120 + 180 \cdot 3.170 = 120 + 570.6 = 690.6 \text{ ms}$$ (1 mark) **Calculation for $n = 4$:** $$T(4) = 120 + 180 \cdot \log_2(4 + 1) = 120 + 180 \cdot \log_2(5)$$ $$\log_2(5) \approx 2.322$$ $$T(4) = 120 + 180 \cdot 2.322 = 120 + 417.96 = 537.96 \text{ ms}$$ (1 mark) **Percentage reduction:** $$\Delta T = 690.6 - 537.96 = 152.64 \text{ ms}$$ $$\% \text{ reduction} = \frac{152.64}{690.6} \times 100 \approx 22.1\%$$ (1 mark) **Design implication:** Halving the number of menu options from 8 to 4 yields a **~22% reduction** in decision time. This is a *logarithmic* gain — not linear — which is why HMI designers aggressively cap menu items at 5–7. Recommended techniques: *progressive disclosure*, *navigation hubs*, *search-first* interfaces, and *grouping related items* under collapsible categories. (1 mark) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Question B-specific):** > - The **derivation origin** (Shannon / Information Theory) is worth ½ mark in many KTU papers — *do not skip* it. > - $\log_2(9) \neq 3$. Use $3.17$ precisely; writing $3$ loses ½ mark. > - When asked for "percentage reduction," always state the **formula** $\frac{T_{\text{old}} - T_{\text{new}}}{T_{\text{old}}} \times 100$ explicitly. --- ### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-minute revision checklist for Psychology of Users:** - ✅ **Mental Model** = user's internal representation. The designer's job is to align **system image** with **user's model** (Norman's three-model framework). - ✅ **Miller's Law:** $C = 7 \pm 2$ chunks in STM. *Chunks*, not characters. - ✅ **Atkinson-Shiffrin:** Sensory (0.25–2 s, raw) → STM (7±2, 15–30 s, acoustic) → LTM (unlimited, semantic). - ✅ **Hick's Law:** $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ — derived from **Shannon's Information Theory**. - ✅ **Fitts' Law:** $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W + 1)$ — derived from **Shannon's formulation of difficulty in bits**. - ✅ **Gestalt Laws:** Proximity, Similarity, Closure, Continuity, Figure-Ground, Common Region — *memorize all six with one example each*. - ✅ **Stevens' Power Law:** $\psi(I) = k I^n$. $n < 1$ for brightness, $n > 1$ for shock. - ✅ **HIP Model (Card-Moran-Newell):** Sensory → Working Memory → Cognitive Processor → Motor; LTM feeds laterally. - ✅ **GOMS/KLM:** Sum of $K, P, H, M, R$ operators to predict task time. - ✅ **Recognition over Recall** — fundamental design heuristic (Shneiderman). - ✅ **Standards for KTU:** Touch target $\geq$ 44 px, menu items $\leq$ 7, response time < 100 ms feels instant. - ✅ **Always write the "+1"** in Fitts' and Hick's formulas — examiners deduct ½ mark otherwise. - ✅ **Always state units** of time (ms or s) and ID (bits). - ✅ **Draw the HIP diagram** with arrows showing sensory → STM → Cognitive → Motor feedback loop to LTM. - ✅ **Connect every design rule to a psychological principle** in long answers — this is the *single highest-scoring move* in KTU HMI papers.

topics.title: Attention
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT753 - HUMAN-MACHINE INTERFACE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Attention # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Attention** is a cognitive process that involves the selective concentration of mental resources on specific aspects of the environment, task, or internal thought, while actively filtering out other perceivable stimuli. In Human-Machine Interface (HMI) engineering, attention is treated as a **limited-capacity information processing resource** that determines which sensory inputs, displayed information elements, or control signals are prioritized for higher-order cognitive processing (perception, decision-making, and motor response). In the context of HMI design (PEBRT753), attention is a **bottleneck resource** because the human sensory system receives far more information per second (~10⁷ bits) than the conscious cognitive system can process (~50 bits/second). Effective interface design must therefore align with the operator's attentional priorities to ensure critical information is perceived and acted upon. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (Module 1):** Attention is covered as a foundational concept of human information processing, preceding topics on perception, memory, and mental models. It forms the basis for evaluating display design, alarm systems, and workload management. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are sitting in a crowded airport terminal. Dozens of conversations, flight announcements, scrolling displays, and movement happen around you simultaneously. Yet, when you hear your *own* flight number called, your brain instantly zeroes in on that one announcement, suppressing the rest. This is **attention** in action — your brain's "spotlight" pivoting toward what is *relevant* and *meaningful*. In the same way, an aircraft pilot staring at six cockpit displays cannot consciously process every gauge, light, and warning at once. The interface designer must engineer the displays so that the *most critical information* (e.g., engine failure warning) automatically captures the pilot's spotlight. If the design is poor, the pilot's "spotlight" may be on the wrong display at the wrong moment — a phenomenon that has caused numerous aviation incidents. ## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in Attention Research - **Reaction Time (RT):** Typically **200–300 ms** for simple visual stimuli; can exceed **1000 ms** under divided attention. - **Foveal Vision (High Acuity):** Approximately **2° of visual angle** around the fixation point. - **Useful Field of View (UFOV):** About **30°** for static targets, shrinking to **≤15°** under high cognitive load. - **Donders' Subtractive Method:** Used to isolate the time cost of attentional selection (~**50–150 ms** for stimulus discrimination tasks). > [!NOTE] > These metrics are crucial for HMI designers to determine display sizes, font legibility, and alarm placement zones. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Attention Attention is not a single monolithic mechanism; it is a multi-layered cognitive control system. The KTU 2024 syllabus treats attention as a set of interacting sub-processes: ### 2.1.1 Types of Attention | Type | Definition | HMI Engineering Implication | |---|---|---| | **Selective Attention** | Focus on one stimulus while ignoring others | Alarm filtering; pop-up critical alerts | | **Sustained Attention (Vigilance)** | Maintaining focus over prolonged periods | Long-haul monitoring; nuclear control rooms; decreases after **~30 min** without stimulation | | **Divided Attention** | Processing multiple task streams simultaneously | Multitasking interfaces (e.g., driving + GPS); performance degrades with task similarity | | **Alternating Attention** | Switching focus between tasks | Menu navigation; tabbed interfaces; incurs **switch cost** (~200–500 ms) | | **Focused Attention** | Responding selectively to specific stimulus features | Color-coding; iconography; auditory beeps for specific events | ### 2.1.2 Stages of Attentional Processing 1. **Sensory Register / Pre-attentive Stage (≤ 250 ms):** Raw sensory data is buffered. Features like color, orientation, motion, and size are detected *automatically and in parallel*. 2. **Attentional Selection / Filtering (Broadbent's Bottleneck):** A filter (selective or attenuating) reduces the incoming data stream to a manageable capacity. 3. **Pattern Recognition & Decision:** Selected information is matched against long-term memory schemas. 4. **Response Selection & Execution:** Motor commands are generated. 5. **Feedback / Monitoring:** The system monitors the outcome to update attention. > [!NOTE] > The KTU syllabus emphasizes that **pre-attentive processing is "free"** (no attention cost), which is why good HMI design exploits pre-attentive features (red color, large size, motion) to highlight critical information. ### 2.1.3 Classical Models of Attention **A. Broadbent's Filter Model (1958) — Early Selection** - All sensory information enters a buffer. - A strict **"all-or-none" filter** selects one channel based on physical features (e.g., ear, pitch, location). - Only the selected channel proceeds to perceptual processing. - *Limitation:* Cannot explain the "Cocktail Party Effect" (your name being heard in unattended speech). **B. Treisman's Attenuation Model (1960)** - Filter is **not all-or-none**; unattended channels are *attenuated* (weakened) rather than blocked. - Highly relevant stimuli (e.g., one's name) can still pass through the attenuated channel. - Explains the Cocktail Party Effect. **C. Deutsch & Deutsch Late Selection Model (1963)** - All inputs are semantically processed fully, but only the *most relevant* reaches conscious awareness and response. - Selection occurs *after* semantic analysis. - *Limitation:* Cognitively expensive; cannot easily explain unattended deafness. **D. Kahneman's Capacity Model (1973)** - Attention is a **single pool of limited resources** (or multiple pools). - Performance = f(Attentional Capacity, Task Demands, Arousal). - Useful for HMI: explains dual-task interference. **E. Posner's Covert Orienting Model (1980)** - Attention can be shifted *without* eye movement (covert attention). - Has three components: **disengage → move → engage**. - Validated by Posner Cuing Paradigm (cue validity effect). ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept / Formula | Description | Engineering Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $RT = a + b \cdot I$ (Hick's Law) | Reaction time grows logarithmically with number of choices $I$ | Menu design; number of buttons on a panel | | 2 | $H = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2(p_i + \epsilon)$ (Information Theory, Shannon) | Information entropy; cognitive load measure | Display density optimization | | 3 | $RT = a + b \cdot ID$ (Fitts' Law) | Time to move to target of width $W$ at distance $D$: $ID = \log_2(D/W + 1)$ | Touchscreen button sizing; cursor targeting | | 4 | $T = \log_2(n) \cdot b$ (Hick-Hyman) | Decision time for $n$ equally probable choices | Dropdown menu items limit | | 5 | $P_{dual} \le P_1 + P_2 - P_{overlap}$ | Dual-task performance bound (Kahneman) | Workload assessment in multi-display cockpits | | 6 | Useful Field of View: $UFOV = f(age, \text{load})$ | Shrinks with age and cognitive load | Display layout for older operators | | 7 | $\text{Yerkes-Dodson Law: } P = k \cdot A \cdot (A_{opt} - A)$ | Performance peaks at optimal arousal $A_{opt}$ | Alarm design (avoid under- and over-stimulation) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** Hick's Law and Fitts' Law are the two most frequently tested equations in the attention / display-design module. Memorize the constants: typical $b \approx 100\text{–}200$ ms/bit for Hick's Law, and $a \approx 50$ ms, $b \approx 100$ ms/bit for Fitts' Law. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aviation Cockpits:** Attention management via *head-up displays (HUD)* and auditory warnings; mitigated *attentional tunneling* after pilot fatigue. - **Medical Devices:** Infusion pumps and ventilators use selective-attention principles; alarm fatigue is a critical attention-management problem. - **Automotive HMIs:** Driver monitoring systems detect attention lapse via eye-gaze tracking (Posner's covert orienting principle). - **Nuclear & Process Control:** Vigilance decrement mitigated by adding brief micro-events; rotating tasks every 30–45 minutes. - **Consumer Electronics:** Notification systems exploit pre-attentive features (color, motion) to grab attention without explicit user action. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Hick's Law (Decision Time as a Function of Number of Choices) Hick's Law models the time required to make a choice among $n$ alternatives. Below is the complete, step-by-step derivation from first principles of information theory. ### Step 1 — Define Information per Choice In an equiprobable choice situation, each alternative carries equal probability $p_i = 1/n$. Shannon's information content (in bits) for a single selection is: $$ H_{bits} = \log_2(n) $$ ### Step 2 — Map Information to Reaction Time Empirically, reaction time $RT$ is linearly related to the information processed (Hick-Hyman experiments, 1952): $$ RT = a + b \cdot H_{bits} $$ Substituting $H_{bits} = \log_2(n)$: $$ RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n) $$ where: - $a$ = **baseline reaction time** (sensorimotor delay, typically $\approx 200$ ms) - $b$ = **slope** (time per bit of information, typically $\approx 100$ ms/bit) ### Step 3 — Generalize to Non-Equiprobable Choices When alternatives have different probabilities $p_i$, the *expected* information is Shannon's entropy: $$ H = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2(p_i) $$ The general Hick-Hyman equation becomes: $$ RT = a + b \cdot H $$ This is the form preferred for HMI menu optimization, where frequently used commands should be more probable (shorter RT). ### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example > A pilot must select one of $n = 8$ equally probable warning lights. Find the expected reaction time. Given $a = 200$ ms, $b = 100$ ms/bit: $$ H_{bits} = \log_2(8) = 3 \text{ bits} $$ $$ RT = 200 + 100 \cdot 3 = 500 \text{ ms} $$ If the designer reduces the choice to $n = 2$ lights (e.g., through hierarchical grouping): $$ H_{bits} = \log_2(2) = 1 \text{ bit} $$ $$ RT = 200 + 100 \cdot 1 = 300 \text{ ms} $$ **Engineering takeaway:** Reducing choice-set from 8 to 2 saves **200 ms** of decision time — a critical margin in life-critical HMIs. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Fitts' Law (Movement Time to a Target) Fitts' Law predicts the time $MT$ to move a pointer (finger, cursor, or gaze) to a target region. ### Step 1 — Define Index of Difficulty The **Index of Difficulty** $ID$ (in bits) captures the relative challenge: $$ ID = \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) $$ where $D$ = distance to target center, $W$ = target width (along the axis of motion). ### Step 2 — Empirical Linear Relation Empirically (Fitts, 1954): $$ MT = a + b \cdot ID $$ Substituting $ID$: $$ MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) $$ ### Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example > A surgeon moves a robotic instrument $D = 20$ cm to a target of width $W = 1$ cm. Given $a = 50$ ms, $b = 150$ ms/bit, find $MT$. $$ \frac{D}{W} = \frac{20}{1} = 20 $$ $$ ID = \log_2(20 + 1) = \log_2(21) \approx 4.39 \text{ bits} $$ $$ MT = 50 + 150 \cdot 4.39 = 50 + 658.5 = 708.5 \text{ ms} $$ **Design takeaway:** Doubling target width $W$ from 1 cm to 2 cm reduces $ID$ substantially, lowering $MT$ and reducing error. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Attention Model | Design Situation | Recommended Model | Key Design Rule | |---|---|---| | Multiple competing alarms | Treisman's Attenuation | Use distinct physical features (pitch, color, location) so attenuation is partial | | Two simultaneous displays | Kahneman's Capacity | Do not exceed $\sim 50$% total capacity per task | | Sustained monitoring | Vigilance Theory | Inject micro-events every 30 min to restore arousal | | Search task in cluttered display | Wolfe's Guided Search | Use saliency map (color, motion) to guide pre-attentive bottom-up processing | | Gaze-contingent display | Posner's Covert Orienting | Highlight foveal region (2° radius) to match covert attention focus | | Menu navigation | Hick-Hyman | Limit to $7 \pm 2$ items per level (Miller's rule) | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Multi-Model Attention Processing Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Sensory Input Stream] --> B[Sensory Register Buffer] B --> C{Attentional Filter / Selector} C -- Broadbent: All or None --> D1[Blocked Channel] C -- Treisman: Attenuate --> D2[Attenuated Channel] D2 -- Highly Relevant Stimulus --> E[Passes Through] D1 --> F[Excluded from Awareness] C -- Selected Channel --> E E --> G[Pattern Recognition
Schema Matching] G --> H[Decision & Response Selection] H --> I[Motor Output] I --> J[Feedback Loop] J --> C G --> K[Late Selection Deutsch Deutsch] K --> H H --> L[Capacity Pool Kahneman] L --> M{Yerkes Dodson
Arousal Check} M -- Under Arousal --> N[Vigilance Failure] M -- Optimal --> O[Peak Performance] M -- Over Arousal --> P[Stress Induced Error] ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The flowchart contrasts **early selection** (Broadbent / Treisman, left side) with **late selection** (Deutsch & Deutsch, lower middle) pathways. - Kahneman's capacity pool modulates the entire decision stage. - The Yerkes-Dodson arousal branch shows the inverted-U relationship between arousal and performance, critical for alarm-system design. ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Pre-Attentive vs. Focused Attention in HMI Display ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PreAttentive[Pre-Attentive Processing - Parallel, Free] PA1[Color Red] PA2[Motion Blinking] PA3[Size Large] PA4[Orientation Tilted] end subgraph Focused[Focused Attention - Serial, Costly] FA1[Reading Text Label] FA2[Comparing Numeric Values] FA3[Logical Inference] end PreAttentive --> Display[Visual Display Layout] Focused --> Display Display --> Operator[Human Operator] Operator --> A1[Immediate Response to Red Blinking] Operator --> A2[Slower Response to Textual Data] ``` **Design principle:** Critical warnings should use **pre-attentive** features so they are detected in **< 250 ms** without conscious effort. ## 4.3 Schematic: The Cocktail Party Effect in HMI ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Speech Channel 1 ATC] --> Mix[Auditory Mixture] S2[Speech Channel 2 Co pilot] --> Mix S3[Speech Channel 3 Passenger Cabin] --> Mix S4[Audio Warning Altitude Alert] --> Mix Mix --> Filter{Attenuation Filter Treisman} Filter -- Strong Signal --> Con[Conscious Awareness] Filter -- Weak Signal --> Unc[Unconscious Monitoring] Unc -- Critical Keyword Detected --> Con Con --> Resp[Pilot Response] ``` This explains why **specific auditory signatures** (e.g., the "Master Caution" chime) are more effective than generic tones — they survive attenuation. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]** > Define **selective attention**. Explain how Treisman's attenuation model accounts for the cocktail party effect. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - *Definition (1 mark):* Selective attention is the cognitive process of focusing on a particular stimulus or channel of information while ignoring other competing stimuli. - *Attenuation model (2 marks):* Treisman proposed that unattended sensory channels are not completely blocked (unlike Broadbent's strict filter) but are *attenuated* (reduced in signal strength). Highly salient or personally relevant stimuli (e.g., one's own name, a critical alarm tone) can still exceed the perceptual threshold of the attenuated channel and reach conscious awareness, explaining the cocktail party effect. --- ### Question 2 > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]** > Differentiate between **sustained attention (vigilance)** and **divided attention**, with one HMI example for each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - *Sustained attention (1.5 marks):* The ability to maintain focus on a task over a prolonged period. Performance shows a *vigilance decrement* typically after 20–30 minutes of monotonous monitoring. - **HMI example:** An air traffic controller monitoring radar for rare conflict alerts. - *Divided attention (1.5 marks):* The ability to process two or more task streams simultaneously. Performance is limited by total attentional capacity (Kahneman's model). - **HMI example:** A driver navigating traffic while listening to a GPS voice prompt. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** ### Question A (14 Marks) — Attention Models & Display Design **(a)** Compare and contrast Broadbent's filter model and Deutsch & Deutsch's late selection model. Discuss which model better explains user behavior in a multi-display control room. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A nuclear plant operator must select 1 of 16 identical alarm indicators. Using Hick's Law, compute the reaction time with $a = 200$ ms and $b = 100$ ms/bit. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) — Comparison (7 marks):** | Criterion | Broadbent (Early Selection) | Deutsch & Deutsch (Late Selection) | |---|---|---| | Point of filtering | Before semantic processing | After semantic processing | | Filter type | All-or-none | All information is processed; response selection is selective | | Processing cost | Low (efficient) | High (semantic analysis on all inputs) | | Explains cocktail party effect | No | Partially yes (semantic relevance) | | Explains inattentional deafness | Yes (filtered out) | No (semantic processing still occurs) | | HMI implication | Useful for pre-attentive design | Useful for designing semantic icons / labels | In a multi-display **control room**, the Deutsch & Deutsch model better explains operator behavior because operators often *process* multiple displays semantically before deciding which requires action — especially under low load. However, under high workload, the *Broadbent-style* early filter dominates, and unattended warnings may be missed. A hybrid Treisman attenuation model is therefore the most realistic design assumption. **(Concluding 1 mark)** **(b) — Hick's Law Calculation (7 marks):** $$ n = 16, \quad a = 200 \text{ ms}, \quad b = 100 \text{ ms/bit} $$ $$ H_{bits} = \log_2(16) = 4 \text{ bits} $$ $$ RT = a + b \cdot H_{bits} = 200 + 100 \cdot 4 = 600 \text{ ms} $$ **Design improvement (1 mark):** If the 16 alarms are grouped hierarchically into 4 categories of 4 alarms each, the operator first selects a category ($\log_2(4) = 2$ bits, $RT = 400$ ms), then the specific alarm ($\log_2(4) = 2$ bits, $RT = 400$ ms), totaling $800$ ms in sequence but allowing parallel cognitive processing of groups. Better yet, apply *pre-attentive coding* (color) to reduce effective choice count to $n = 2$, achieving $RT = 300$ ms — a 50% reduction. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Vigilance & Fitts' Law in Interface Design **(a)** Explain the **vigilance decrement** phenomenon. Discuss two engineering strategies to mitigate it in process-control HMIs. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A pilot must touch a rectangular button of width $W = 1.5$ cm located at distance $D = 12$ cm from the current finger position. Using Fitts' Law with $a = 50$ ms and $b = 150$ ms/bit, calculate the movement time. Recommend one design change to reduce the movement time by at least 25%. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) — Vigilance Decrement (7 marks):** *Definition (2 marks):* Vigilance decrement is the progressive decline in detection performance and reaction time observed during prolonged monitoring of low-event-rate signals. It typically becomes significant after **20–30 minutes** of continuous monitoring. *Causes (2 marks):* - Reduced arousal (Yerkes-Dodson: under-arousal zone) - Habituation of orienting response - Attentional resource depletion (Kahneman's capacity) *Engineering strategies (3 marks — 1.5 marks each):* 1. **Signal enrichment:** Inject brief, predictable micro-events (e.g., periodic system status confirmations) to maintain arousal without raising false alarms. 2. **Rotating task schedules / breaks:** Implement mandatory task switches every 30–45 minutes; pair vigilance tasks with secondary motor tasks to maintain engagement. 3. **Adaptive automation:** Detect operator drowsiness via eye-tracking and automatically increase display saliency or trigger an auditory nudge. **(b) — Fitts' Law Calculation (7 marks):** $$ D = 12 \text{ cm}, \quad W = 1.5 \text{ cm}, \quad a = 50 \text{ ms}, \quad b = 150 \text{ ms/bit} $$ $$ ID = \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) = \log_2\!\left(\frac{12}{1.5} + 1\right) = \log_2(8 + 1) = \log_2(9) $$ $$ \log_2(9) = \frac{\ln 9}{\ln 2} = \frac{2.197}{0.693} \approx 3.17 \text{ bits} $$ $$ MT = a + b \cdot ID = 50 + 150 \cdot 3.17 \approx 50 + 475.5 = 525.5 \text{ ms} $$ *Design change (1 mark):* Increase button width to $W = 3$ cm (doubling the target): $$ ID_{new} = \log_2\!\left(\frac{12}{3} + 1\right) = \log_2(5) \approx 2.32 \text{ bits} $$ $$ MT_{new} = 50 + 150 \cdot 2.32 \approx 398 \text{ ms} $$ **Reduction:** $(525.5 - 398) / 525.5 \approx 24.3\%$ — close to the target. For a guaranteed $\geq 25\%$ reduction, set $W = 3.5$ cm, giving $ID \approx 2.11$ bits and $MT \approx 366.5$ ms (30% reduction). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **In Hick's Law problems, students frequently forget to convert to base-2 logarithm.** Always use $\log_2(n)$ — never $\log_{10}$ or $\ln$ without explicit conversion. > 2. **In Fitts' Law, do not omit the "+1" inside the logarithm.** This is a common KTU-specific trap; $ID = \log_2(D/W + 1)$ is the canonical form. > 3. **Avoid vague answers in Part A.** KTU examiners award only partial credit for "Broadbent blocks, Treisman attenuates" — you must explicitly mention the *implication* (cocktail party effect, etc.) to earn full 3 marks. > 4. **In Part B (14-mark) answers,** always include a *practical HMI implication* at the end of theoretical sub-parts. The 2024 scheme explicitly tests application-level outcomes (CO3 / CO4). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Attention (Module 1)** - **Definition (Must-Memorize):** Attention = selective concentration of limited cognitive resources; a bottleneck between high-capacity sensory input and low-capacity conscious processing. - **Five types:** Selective, Sustained (Vigilance), Divided, Alternating, Focused. - **Three classical filter models:** Broadbent (early, all-or-none), Treisman (early, attenuation), Deutsch & Deutsch (late selection). - **Kahneman's Capacity Model** and **Yerkes-Dodson Law** are the most-tested in KTU examinations. - **Hick's Law:** $RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n)$ — apply to menu and alarm selection problems. - **Fitts' Law:** $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W + 1)$ — apply to touchscreen, button, and cursor-targeting problems. - **Posner's Covert Orienting:** Disengage → Move → Engage; explains why eye-gaze is not the only locus of attention. - **Pre-attentive features** (color, motion, size, orientation) are *free* — use them for critical HMI warnings. - **Useful Field of View** ≈ 30° (static) and 15° (under load); central fixation zone for high-priority information is ≈ 2°. - **Vigilance decrement** sets in after 20–30 min — schedule breaks or use signal enrichment. - **Yerkes-Dodson:** Inverted-U; both under- and over-arousal degrade performance. Alarm design must target the *optimal arousal zone*. - **Miller's Rule:** $7 \pm 2$ items per chunk — limits hierarchical menu depth. - **Engineering applications:** Aviation cockpits (HUDs, auditory warnings), medical devices (alarm fatigue), automotive (driver monitoring), nuclear control rooms (vigilance aids). - **Common pitfalls:** Confusing Broadbent with Treisman; forgetting the "+1" in Fitts' Law; using $\log_{10}$ instead of $\log_2$ in Hick's Law; ignoring arousal effects in vigilance problems. > **End of Module 1 Notes — Topic: Attention (PEBRT753, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

topics.title: Thinking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT753 - HUMAN-MACHINE INTERFACE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thinking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of Human–Machine Interface (HMI) and cognitive engineering, **thinking** is formally defined as the internal, symbolic, goal-directed cognitive process by which a human operator manipulates internal representations (mental models, concepts, propositions, images) to derive new knowledge, solve problems, make decisions, or comprehend complex situations. As per KTU 2024 Module 1, thinking bridges **perception (input)** and **action (output)**, and is central to the *human* half of the human–machine loop.

From the KTU prescribed HCI framework (Don Norman's *The Design of Everyday Things* and Dix et al. *Human–Computer Interaction*), thinking is one of the **seven stages of action** (executing, evaluating, perceiving, remembering, analyzing, deciding, specifying) that the human undertakes while interacting with any interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> Thinking is a **psychological concept** that the designer must understand to predict *why* users behave in unexpected ways when using a system. The machine does not "think" (in the narrow cognitive sense); it *processes*. The user must do the thinking.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are in an unfamiliar city with a paper map in your hand. You are trying to reach a museum:

- **Seeing the streets** = *Perception*
- **Remembering the museum name** = *Memory*
- **Mentally rotating the map to align with your facing direction** = *Thinking*
- **Deciding to turn left** = *Decision-making*
- **Physically walking** = *Action*

**Thinking is the invisible "translation layer" between what you know and what you decide.** In HMI, if the interface does not *support* the user's natural thinking, the user will commit errors, become frustrated, or abandon the task.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of the human brain as a CPU and *thinking* as the algorithm running inside it. The machine interface is the I/O device. If the I/O does not match what the algorithm expects, the program crashes (the user makes a mistake).

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Cognitive Metrics

There are no SI physical constants, but the following **empirical cognitive metrics** are standard in HMI literature and KTU answers:

| Metric | Standard Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Working Memory Capacity (Miller's Law)** | **7 ± 2 chunks** | Short-term memory holds ~7 items |
| **Reaction Time (Hick's Law)** | ~**100–300 ms** | Time to choose between N options |
| **Recognition over Recall** | Recognition is ~**10× faster** | Users prefer menus to commands |
| **Short-term memory decay** | ~**15–30 seconds** | Without rehearsal, info is lost |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold-highlighted constants above must be memorized** — KTU frequently tests them as 3-mark direct questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Breaking Down the Concept of Thinking

Thinking is not a single process; it is a **hierarchy of cognitive sub-processes** that the HMI designer must support.

#### 2.1.1 Levels of Thinking (Bloom's Taxonomy — KTU-favoured)
- **Remembering** — Recall facts (lowest cognitive load)
- **Understanding** — Explain ideas
- **Applying** — Use knowledge in new situations
- **Analyzing** — Break into parts
- **Evaluating** — Justify a decision
- **Creating** — Produce something new (highest load)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for HMI:** A well-designed interface reduces the cognitive level required. For example, a calculator *automates* the "applying" step — the user no longer needs to do long division mentally.

#### 2.1.2 Types of Thinking (KTU High-Yield)
1. **Convergent Thinking** — Single correct answer (e.g., "What is 12 × 8?")
2. **Divergent Thinking** — Many possible answers (e.g., "List 10 uses of a brick")
3. **Critical Thinking** — Judging validity of arguments
4. **Creative Thinking** — Producing novel, useful ideas
5. **Lateral Thinking** (Edward de Bono) — Indirect, non-obvious approaches
6. **Vertical / Logical Thinking** — Sequential, step-by-step deduction

#### 2.1.3 Reasoning (Critical for Module 1)

| Type | Direction | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Deductive Reasoning** | General → Specific | "All mammals breathe. Whales are mammals. ⟹ Whales breathe." |
| **Inductive Reasoning** | Specific → General | "Swan 1, 2, 3, … are white. ⟹ All swans are white." (can be falsified) |
| **Abductive Reasoning** | Best explanation for observation | "The grass is wet. ⟹ It must have rained last night." |

#### 2.1.4 Problem Solving & Decision Making
- **Problem Solving** = recognizing a gap between current state and goal state, then devising a sequence of operators to close the gap.
- **Decision Making** = choosing between alternative courses of action under uncertainty.
- **Mental Models** (Johnson-Laird) = the internal, simplified, analogical representation a user builds of a system. *Gulf of Execution* and *Gulf of Evaluation* (Norman) arise when the mental model mismatches the system model.

#### 2.1.5 The Seven Stages of Action (Norman, 1988)
1. **Forming the goal**
2. **Forming the intention**
3. **Specifying an action sequence**
4. **Executing the action**
5. **Perceiving the system state**
6. **Interpreting the perception**
7. **Evaluating the outcome**

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Miller's Law | $C = 7 \pm 2$ chunks | Working memory capacity |
| 2 | Hick's Law | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(N+1)$ | $T$ = reaction time, $N$ = choices |
| 3 | Fitts's Law | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{D}{W}+1\right)$ | $D$ = distance, $W$ = target width |
| 4 | Recognition over Recall | $T_{recog} \approx 0.1 \cdot T_{recall}$ | Recognition is ~10× faster |
| 5 | Cognitive Load (Sweller) | $L = L_{int} + L_{ext} + L_{rel}$ | Intrinsic + Extraneous + Germane |
| 6 | Information Bits (Shannon, simplified) | $I = \log_2(N)$ | $N$ = equally likely options |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do not use vertical bars `|x|` in your exam answer tables.** Write `\vert x \vert` or `mod x`. KTU valuer machines silently corrupt the table.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Aviation Cockpit Design** — Supports *convergent* thinking under stress (Boeing 737 MAX redesign lesson).
- **Medical Devices** — Infusion pumps minimize *abductive* error by displaying reasoning chains.
- **Automotive HMI** — Touchscreens reduce *recall* load by using icons (recognition).
- **AI/Chatbots** — Must *simulate* human-like thinking using NLU + reasoning engines.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hick's Law (Reaction Time vs. Number of Choices)

**Statement:** The time taken for a user to make a decision grows logarithmically with the number of equally probable choices.

**Step 1 — Shannon's Information Theory Foundation**

Each choice carries an information content of:

$$
I = \log_2(N) \text{ bits}
$$

where $N$ is the number of equiprobable options. The more choices, the more *bits* the user must process.

**Step 2 — Linear Assumption Between Information and Time**

Empirically (Hick, 1952; Hyman, 1953), reaction time $T$ is linearly proportional to the information content:

$$
T = k \cdot I
$$

Substituting Step 1:

$$
T = k \cdot \log_2(N)
$$

**Step 3 — Adding the +1 Correction (Why we add 1 inside the log)**

For $N=0$ the formula is undefined. To keep it meaningful and to model the *baseline motor execution time* (even with 0 choices the user spends time perceiving), we add 1:

$$
T = a + b \cdot \log_2(N + 1)
$$

- $a$ = baseline perceptual-motor delay (≈ 100–200 ms).
- $b$ = slope, dependent on user expertise (≈ 100–150 ms/bit).

**Step 4 — HMI Design Rule Derived**

Doubling the menu options (e.g., from 4 to 8) increases reaction time by:

$$
\Delta T = b \cdot \left[\log_2(8+1) - \log_2(4+1)\right] = b \cdot [3.17 - 2.32] = 0.85\,b
$$

That is roughly **85 ms extra decision time per doubling** — a perceptible delay.

> **Conversion logic:** We treat each menu item as one bit of information, add a constant perceptual offset, and use the empirical linear relationship.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Type of Thinking

| Design Scenario | Thinking Type to Support | HMI Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| ATM cash withdrawal | Convergent | Fixed menu, numeric keypad |
| Brainstorming tool (Miro) | Divergent | Sticky notes, free canvas |
| Medical diagnosis DSS | Abductive | Show ranked hypothesis list |
| Flight autopilot override | Deductive | Clear status + clear cause-effect |
| Spam classifier UX | Inductive | Confidence %, training feedback |
| Creative design software | Lateral | "What if…" toggles, random prompts |

### 3.3 Worked Example: Identifying the Gulf of Execution

A user wants to **print double-sided**. The mental model says: "there should be a duplex option." The system model has it hidden under **Settings → Printer → Setup → Advanced → Duplex**.

$$
\text{Gulf of Execution} = \text{Goal} \rightarrow \text{Intention} \rightarrow \text{Action Spec} \rightarrow \text{Physical Action}
$$

**Analysis of failure points:**
- **Goal formulation** — Clear ("print duplex").
- **Intention** — Clear ("open printer menu").
- **Action specification** — *Cognitive bottleneck* — user does not know the correct menu path.
- **Physical action** — Easy once path is known.

The HMI must therefore **expose the intention-to-action mapping** (e.g., a one-click "Duplex" toggle on the print dialog).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Norman's Seven Stages of Action with Gulfs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Stage 1 Forming the Goal]
    node2[Stage 2 Forming the Intention]
    node3[Stage 3 Specifying Action Sequence]
    node4[Stage 4 Executing the Action]
    node5[Stage 5 Perceiving System State]
    node6[Stage 6 Interpreting the Perception]
    node7[Stage 7 Evaluating the Outcome]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7

    node3 -. Gulf of Execution .-> node4
    node5 -. Gulf of Evaluation .-> node6

    userM[User Mental Model] -. informs .-> node1
    userM -. informs .-> node2
    sysM[System Image Interface] -. informs .-> node5
    sysM -. informs .-> node6
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Types of Thinking and Reasoning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    think[Thinking] --> conv[Convergent]
    think --> div[Divergent]
    think --> crit[Critical]
    think --> cre[Creative]
    think --> lat[Lateral]
    think --> vert[Vertical]

    reason[Reasoning] --> ded[Deductive General to Specific]
    reason --> ind[Inductive Specific to General]
    reason --> abd[Abductive Best Explanation]

    think --> prob[Problem Solving]
    think --> dec[Decision Making]
    think --> mm[Mental Models]

    mm --> gulfE[Gulf of Execution]
    mm --> gulfV[Gulf of Evaluation]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Human Thought in HMI

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph StimulusLayer["Stimulus Layer External World"]
        stimA[Visual Cues]
        stimB[Auditory Cues]
        stimC[Haptic Cues]
    end

    subgraph PerceiveLayer["Perception Layer Bottom Up"]
        perA[Pattern Recognition]
        perB[Feature Extraction]
    end

    subgraph ThinkLayer["Thinking Layer Core Cognition"]
        thA[Working Memory 7 plus minus 2]
        thB[Long Term Memory Schemas]
        thC[Reasoning Engine]
        thD[Mental Model Builder]
    end

    subgraph DecideLayer["Decision Layer"]
        decA[Goal Formation]
        decB[Action Selection]
    end

    subgraph ActionLayer["Action Layer Top Down"]
        actA[Motor Execution]
        actB[Speech Output]
    end

    subgraph FeedbackLayer["Feedback Loop"]
        fb[Outcome Evaluation]
    end

    stimA --> perA
    stimB --> perA
    stimC --> perA
    perA --> thA
    thA <--> thB
    thA --> thC
    thC --> thD
    thD --> decA
    decA --> decB
    decB --> actA
    actB --> actA
    actA --> fb
    fb --> stimA
    thD -. predicted state .-> fb
```

> **Note for students:** Mermaid v10+ supports the `subgraph` syntax used above. All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `thA`, `decB`) — none use reserved keywords. Labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text, no markdown bolding inside quotes.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar]`
**Define the term "thinking" in the context of Human–Machine Interface. List any four types of thinking with one-line examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Thinking is the internal cognitive process of manipulating mental representations (concepts, images, propositions) to solve problems, make decisions, or understand situations, bridging the user's perception and action during interaction with a machine.

Four types:
1. **Convergent** — One correct answer (e.g., 5 × 6 = 30)
2. **Divergent** — Many possible answers (e.g., uses of a paperclip)
3. **Critical** — Evaluating argument validity
4. **Creative** — Producing novel ideas

*(1 mark for definition, 0.5 each for 4 types = 3 marks)*

---

#### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Similar]`
**State Miller's Law. Mention its significance in interface design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Miller's Law (1956):** The short-term (working) memory of a human can hold approximately **$7 \pm 2$ chunks** of information at any given time.

**Significance in HMI (any two):**
- Menus should not display more than ~7 ± 2 items per level.
- Phone numbers, IDs, and OTP codes must be chunked (e.g., 4-3-3 grouping).
- Reduces cognitive overload, decreases error rate.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A** (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the seven stages of action proposed by Don Norman. How do the Gulf of Execution and Gulf of Evaluation arise? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The seven stages of action (Norman, 1988) describe the complete cognitive cycle a user goes through when interacting with a system:

1. **Forming the goal** — What do I want to achieve? (e.g., save my document)
2. **Forming the intention** — What general strategy? (use the File menu)
3. **Specifying an action sequence** — Exact steps (File → Save → Click)
4. **Executing the action** — Physical motor action (mouse click)
5. **Perceiving the system state** — What happened? (saw dialog)
6. **Interpreting the perception** — What does it mean? (saved successfully)
7. **Evaluating the outcome** — Did I meet my goal? (yes)

**Gulf of Execution** = the gap between the user's *intention* and the *physical actions* the system allows. It arises when:
- The user does not know which control to operate.
- The mapping between intent and action is non-obvious (e.g., hidden menus).

**Gulf of Evaluation** = the gap between the system's *physical state* and the user's *perception/interpretation* of that state. It arises when:
- Feedback is missing, delayed, or unclear.
- The display does not match the user's mental model (e.g., a progress bar that resets to 0%).

A good HMI design **bridges both gulfs** by making system state and possible actions visible and mappable to user expectations.

*(1 mark per stage, 2 marks for both gulfs, 1 mark for bridging — total 7)*

---

**(b) Differentiate between convergent and divergent thinking. How does an interface support each? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Convergent Thinking | Divergent Thinking |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Narrowing toward one answer | Expanding to many answers |
| **Goal** | Find the correct solution | Generate many possible solutions |
| **Evaluation** | Right vs. wrong | Novelty and variety |
| **Time pressure** | Tolerates | Often hampers |
| **Example task** | Solve 2x + 3 = 7 | List 20 uses of a brick |
| **Cognitive process** | Deductive, analytical | Associative, free-flowing |

**Interface Support for Convergent Thinking:**
- Constrained menus, single correct workflow.
- Clear wizards with linear steps.
- Validation messages for correct inputs.
- Example: ATM cash withdrawal screen.

**Interface Support for Divergent Thinking:**
- Open whiteboards (Miro, FigJam).
- Sticky notes, free-form canvas, no time limit.
- "What if" toggles, randomization, prompts.
- Example: Brainstorming tool with auto-clustering.

**Designer's role:** Identify whether the user task is convergent or divergent and provide the right scaffolding — too much constraint kills creativity; too little overwhelms novices.

*(2 marks definition, 2 marks differences, 3 marks interface support — total 7)*

---

#### **Question B (Alternative to Question A)** (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the three types of reasoning — deductive, inductive, and abductive — with examples. How does abductive reasoning cause user errors in HMI? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Deductive Reasoning** — *General → Specific*. Logically certain if premises are true.
   - Example: All software updates improve security. This is a software update. ⟹ It improves security.

2. **Inductive Reasoning** — *Specific → General*. Probabilistic, can be falsified.
   - Example: The last three updates crashed my PC. ⟹ All updates crash my PC.

3. **Abductive Reasoning** — *Observation → Best explanation*. A default-to-most-likely inference.
   - Example: My screen is blank. ⟹ Probably the monitor is off.

**How abductive reasoning causes user errors:**
- Users always form a *best guess* about why the system behaves a certain way.
- If the interface provides **incomplete or misleading feedback**, the user's abductive inference will be wrong, leading to:
  - Repeated incorrect actions (e.g., pressing a non-responsive button harder).
  - Mistrust of the system.
