**Textual Logic:** A WER of 10% is considered **acceptable for command-and-control applications** (e.g., "Turn on the lights"). Conversational ASR systems (e.g., transcription) target WER < 5%.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: HMI Input Technologies

| Technology | Bandwidth | Cognitive Load | Accuracy | Learning Curve | KTU Use Case |
|------------|-----------|----------------|----------|----------------|--------------|
| **Mechanical Keyboard** | High (~10 char/s) | Low | Very High (~99%) | Low | Data entry systems |
| **Mouse** | Medium (~6–10 bits/s) | Low | High | Low | Desktop GUIs |
| **Touchscreen** | Medium-High | Low | High | Very Low | Smartphones, kiosks |
| **Voice Input** | High (150 wpm) | Very Low | Medium (WER 5–15%) | Very Low | Virtual assistants |
| **Gesture (Vision)** | Medium | Medium | Medium (90–95%) | Medium | AR/VR, gaming |
| **Eye Tracking** | Very High (~10 bits/s) | Low | Medium | High | Accessibility, fMRI |
| **EEG BCI** | Low (~1–5 bits/s) | High | Low-Medium | Very High | Assistive tech |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: HMI Technology Stack (Layered Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Layer 4: Application and Presentation] --> B[Layer 3: Perceptual and Cognitive Processing]
    B --> C[Layer 2: Signal Processing and Drivers]
    C --> D[Layer 1: Physical Sensors and Actuators]
    D --> E[Human Operator]
    E --> F[Layer 1: Sensors Capture Human Input]
    F --> G[Layer 2: ADC and Noise Filtering]
    G --> H[Layer 3: Pattern Recognition and AI Models]
    H --> I[Layer 4: GUI and Multimodal Output]
    I --> J[Machine Acts on Command]
    J --> K[Feedback to Human via Display Audio Haptics]
    K --> E
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The flow is **bidirectional** (human → machine → human), which is the defining characteristic of an interactive HMI.
- Each layer adds latency and abstraction, but also adds **intelligence** (semantic understanding).

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Voice Recognition Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Microphone Input] --> B[Anti-Aliasing Filter]
    B --> C[ADC Sampling at 16 kHz]
    C --> D[Feature Extraction MFCC]
    D --> E[Acoustic Model DNN]
    E --> F[Language Model Transformer]
    F --> G[WFST Decoder]
    G --> H[Final Text Output]
    H --> I[NLU Intent Classification]
    I --> J[Action: Execute Command]
```

**Explanation:** This block diagram is the canonical pipeline for any modern voice assistant (Alexa, Google Assistant, Siri). KTU exam questions often ask students to **label each block** or **identify the function of a given stage**.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Brain-Computer Interface Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Human Brain Neural Activity] --> B[EEG Electrodes on Scalp]
    B --> C[Amplifier and Bandpass Filter]
    C --> D[ADC and Digital Storage]
    D --> E[Feature Extraction: Band Power]
    E --> F[Machine Learning Classifier]
    F --> G{Intent Recognized?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Command Sent to External Device]
    G -->|No| I[Rejection or No Action]
    H --> J[Device Actuator: Wheelchair Cursor Prosthetic]
    J --> K[Feedback to User Visual or Haptic]
    K --> A
```

**Explanation:** The loop between $K \rightarrow A$ represents **closed-loop biofeedback**, a critical concept in BCI design for user adaptation.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Multi-Modal HMI Fusion in a Modern Car

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver] --> B[Voice Command]
    A --> C[Touch on Center Console]
    A --> D[Steering Wheel Buttons]
    A --> E[Gesture on Air Vent]
    B --> F[Multimodal Fusion Engine]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Navigation System]
    F --> H[Climate Control]
    F --> I[Infotainment]
    G --> J[Driver Feedback HUD]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> A
```

**Explanation:** Modern automotive HMIs (e.g., Tesla, Mercedes MBUX) use **multimodal fusion** to allow the driver to use the most natural input modality in any context, reducing cognitive load and improving safety.

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Generic HMI System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Intent Generation] --> B[Input Encoder]
    B --> C[Semantic Interpreter]
    C --> D[Context Manager]
    D --> E[Task Manager]
    E --> F[Output Renderer]
    F --> G[Multimodal Output Devices]
    G --> H[Perceptual Feedback to User]
    H --> A
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define Human-Machine Interface (HMI). List any four input technologies used in modern HMI systems.**

**Model Answer:**
Human-Machine Interface (HMI) is the hardware and software framework that facilitates communication and interaction between a human operator and a machine system. It encompasses all means by which a user issues commands to a machine and receives feedback from it.

**Four Input Technologies:**
1. **Touchscreen (Capacitive):** Detects finger touch via changes in electrical capacitance. Used in smartphones, kiosks.
2. **Microphone (Voice Input):** Converts acoustic speech into electrical signals for ASR systems like Alexa.
3. **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU):** Combines accelerometer, gyroscope, and magnetometer to sense motion (smartwatch gesture control).
4. **EEG Headset (Brain-Computer Interface):** Non-invasively records electrical activity of the brain for hands-free control.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: What is Fitts's Law? Write its mathematical form and explain the meaning of the Index of Difficulty.**

**Model Answer:**
Fitts's Law is a predictive model of human motor behavior that estimates the time required to move rapidly to a target area, based on the distance to the target and its size.

**Mathematical Form (Shannon's formulation):**

$$MT = a + b \cdot \log_2 \left( \frac{A}{W} + 1 \right)$$

**Index of Difficulty (ID):**

$$ID = \log_2 \left( \frac{A}{W} + 1 \right) \quad \text{[measured in bits]}$$

**Meaning:** $ID$ quantifies the **difficulty** of the motor task. It increases with the distance $A$ to the target and decreases with the target width $W$. A larger $ID$ requires more time and information processing.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the various generations of HMI technologies with suitable examples for each generation. Discuss the key advantages of multimodal interaction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the working principle of capacitive touchscreens. Compare resistive and capacitive touch technologies in terms of sensitivity, multi-touch support, and durability. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Generations of HMI Technologies:**

1. **First Generation — Mechanical / Direct Manipulation:** Used physical levers, switches, dials, and gauges. Example: Aircraft cockpits of the 1940s–60s with analog altimeters. Pros: Direct, tactile. Cons: Requires training, no flexibility.

2. **Second Generation — Graphical User Interface (GUI):** Introduced icons, windows, menus, and pointing devices (mouse). Example: Xerox Alto (1973), Apple Macintosh (1984). Pros: Visual recognition over recall, intuitive. Cons: Still bound to 2D screen.

3. **Third Generation — Natural / Multimodal Interaction:** Voice, gesture, touch combined. Example: Microsoft Kinect, Apple's Siri, modern infotainment systems. Pros: Reduced learning curve, accessible. Cons: Higher computational cost.

4. **Fourth Generation — Cognitive / Brain-Machine Interfaces:** Direct neural signals or AI-driven intent prediction. Example: Neuralink, EEG-controlled wheelchairs, Apple Vision Pro spatial computing. Pros: Potential to assist disabled users. Cons: Still experimental, ethical concerns.

**Advantages of Multimodal Interaction:**
- **Robustness:** If one modality fails (e.g., noisy environment → voice fails), another (e.g., gesture) succeeds.
- **Naturalness:** Users communicate as they would with another human.
- **Accessibility:** Supports users with varying abilities.
- **Reduced Cognitive Load:** Distributing input across modalities decreases mental effort.
- **Increased Bandwidth:** Combines the data rates of multiple channels.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Working Principle of Capacitive Touchscreen:**
A capacitive touchscreen consists of a grid of transparent electrodes (typically Indium Tin Oxide, ITO) coated on a glass substrate. Each electrode holds a small electrical charge. When a conductive object (like a human finger) approaches or touches the surface, it **disturbs the local electrostatic field**, changing the capacitance at that point. The touchscreen controller continuously measures the capacitance at each grid intersection.

There are two main types:
- **Self-Capacitance:** Measures the capacitance of a single electrode with respect to ground. Provides stronger signal but limited true multi-touch.
- **Mutual Capacitance:** Measures capacitance between a row electrode and a column electrode. Enables true multi-touch with multiple simultaneous contact points.

The controller IC samples these capacitance values thousands of times per second, and the **touch position is calculated** based on which electrodes reported the largest change. Modern systems also support hover detection, palm rejection, and water rejection via advanced algorithms.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Resistive Touch | Capacitive Touch |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|
| **Activation** | Pressure required | Conductive contact (skin) |
| **Sensitivity** | Works with gloves, stylus | Requires skin or special stylus |
| **Multi-Touch** | Limited / None | Native multi-touch support |
| **Optical Clarity** | Lower (multiple layers) | Higher (single glass layer) |
| **Durability** | Lower (top layer wears) | High (glass is scratch-resistant) |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Response Time** | Slower | Faster |
| **Power Consumption** | Lower | Slightly higher |

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) system. Define Word Error Rate (WER) and explain its significance in evaluating HMI technologies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a Brain-Computer Interface (BCI)? Compare invasive, semi-invasive, and non-invasive BCI technologies. Discuss two real-world applications of BCI in assistive HMI. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Block Diagram of ASR System:**

```
Microphone → Pre-amplifier → Anti-aliasing Filter → ADC (16 kHz) →
Feature Extraction (MFCC) → Acoustic Model (DNN) →
Language Model (Transformer/LLM) → Decoder (WFST) → Text Output →
Natural Language Understanding (NLU) → Intent Classification → Action
```

**Working Explanation:**

1. **Audio Capture:** A microphone converts the speaker's voice (acoustic pressure waves) into an analog electrical signal.
2. **Pre-amplification & Filtering:** The signal is amplified and passed through an anti-aliasing low-pass filter (cutoff ~8 kHz for telephony).
3. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion:** The signal is sampled at ≥16 kHz (Nyquist rate) and quantized to 16 bits per sample.
4. **Feature Extraction:** The raw digital waveform is transformed into a sequence of feature vectors, typically **Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCCs)**. MFCCs mimic the human ear's logarithmic frequency resolution and provide a compact representation robust to noise.
5. **Acoustic Model:** A Deep Neural Network (e.g., Conformer, LSTM) maps each feature vector to a probability distribution over **phonemes** (the smallest unit of sound in a language).
6. **Language Model:** Predicts the probability of word sequences using statistical N-grams or modern Transformer-based LLMs. This enforces linguistic plausibility.
7. **Decoder:** Combines the acoustic and language model probabilities using **Weighted Finite State Transducers (WFSTs)** to produce the most likely word sequence.
8. **Post-Processing (NLU):** The transcribed text is analyzed for **intent** (e.g., "play music") and **entities** (e.g., song name).

**Word Error Rate (WER):**

$$\text{WER} = \frac{S + D + I}{N}$$

- $S$ = number of word substitutions (wrong word recognized)
- $D$ = number of deletions (word missed)
- $I$ = number of insertions (extra word hallucinated)
- $N$ = total number of words in the reference (ground truth) transcript

**Significance:**
- WER is the **universal benchmark** for ASR system quality.
- Lower WER → better user experience, fewer corrections needed.
- Industry targets: **<5%** for dictation, **<10%** for command applications.
- Used to compare competing ASR systems (Google, Amazon, Apple, Microsoft).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Brain-Computer Interface (BCI) Definition:**
A Brain-Computer Interface is a direct communication pathway between the brain's electrical activity and an external device, bypassing the normal neuromuscular output channels (nerves, muscles). It acquires brain signals, processes them, and translates them into commands for a computer, prosthesis, or communication system.

**Comparison of BCI Types:**

| Parameter | Invasive BCI | Semi-Invasive (ECoG) | Non-Invasive (EEG) |
|-----------|--------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Electrode Placement** | Inside brain cortex | On surface of brain (under skull) | On scalp (external) |
| **Signal Quality (SNR)** | Very High | High | Low-Medium |
| **Spatial Resolution** | Very High (single neuron) | High (cortical columns) | Low (cortical mass) |
| **Temporal Resolution** | Excellent | Very Good | Good |
| **Medical Risk** | High (surgery, infection) | Moderate (surgery) | None (reversible) |
| **Setup Time** | Permanent (implanted) | Permanent | Minutes (wearable) |
| **Cost** | Very High | High | Low |
| **Example Systems** | Neuralink, Utah Array | NeuroPace, BrainGate | Emotiv EPOC, OpenBCI |

**Real-World Applications in Assistive HMI:**

1. **BCI-Controlled Wheelchair for ALS Patients:**
A patient with Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) loses all motor control but retains full cognition. An EEG-based BCI (e.g., using P300 evoked potentials) allows the patient to steer a wheelchair or operate a computer by **selecting letters/icons on a screen** through focused attention. The EEG detects the characteristic P300 brain response when the desired option flashes, enabling communication at **5–10 selections per minute**.

2. **Neuralink and Prosthetic Limb Control:**
Neuralink's implantable device (N1 chip with 1024 channels) records signals from the motor cortex. Machine learning decodes the patient's intent to move, and the signals are sent to a **robotic prosthetic arm** that mimics natural movement with sub-millisecond latency. The patient can grasp objects, type on a virtual keyboard, and even play video games.

3. **Communication Devices for Locked-In Syndrome:**
Using Steady-State Visual Evoked Potential (SSVEP) BCI, patients focus on flickering icons representing letters; the BCI detects the dominant flicker frequency and types the corresponding character. This restores communication ability to fully paralyzed individuals.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> - **Confusing WER with Accuracy:** WER is an *error* rate. Accuracy = $1 - \text{WER}$. A 5% WER means 95% accuracy. Do not write them interchangeably.
> - **Fitts's Law Constants:** Examiners often mark you down for forgetting the "+1" inside the logarithm. Use Shannon's formulation, not the older form $\log_2(A/W)$ which has a domain problem.
> - **Sampling Theorem Misapplication:** For *speech*, telephony uses 8 kHz bandwidth and 16 kHz sampling rate. For *music* (CD quality), it is 22.05 kHz bandwidth and 44.1 kHz sampling. State the application context.
> - **BCI Misclassification:** Always distinguish between "invasive" (in brain tissue), "semi-invasive" (on brain surface), and "non-invasive" (on scalp). Students frequently lose marks by writing "EEG is invasive" — it is not.
> - **Confusing Display Technologies:** LCD requires a backlight; OLED is self-emissive. Do not state that OLED cannot produce "true black" — its pixels simply turn off completely.
> - **Formula Units:** When asked for Movement Time, give the answer in **milliseconds (ms)** or **seconds (s)** explicitly. Always show units in final answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Yield for KTU ESE**

- [ ] **HMI = Human + Machine + Interface.** Always describe in terms of bidirectional flow: input (human→machine) and output (machine→human).
- [ ] **Four generations of HMI:** Mechanical → GUI → Natural (multimodal) → Cognitive (BCI/AI). Remember at least one example per generation.
- [ ] **Fitts's Law:** $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2(A/W + 1)$. Higher distance $A$ or smaller width $W$ → larger $ID$ → longer movement time.
- [ ] **Hick's Law:** Decision time grows logarithmically with number of choices. $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$.
- [ ] **Nyquist Theorem:** Sample at $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. Anti-aliasing filter is mandatory before sampling.
- [ ] **WER formula:** $\text{WER} = (S + D + I) / N$. Industry benchmarks: <5% excellent, <10% acceptable for command ASR.
- [ ] **Capacitive vs Resistive Touch:** Capacitive is multi-touch capable, more durable, but needs conductive input. Resistive works with gloves and stylus.
- [ ] **Display tech hierarchy (image quality):** LCD < OLED < MicroLED (rough order; nuances exist).
- [ ] **BCI three tiers:** Invasive (highest SNR, highest risk) < Semi-invasive < Non-invasive EEG (lowest SNR, lowest risk).
- [ ] **EEG bands:** Delta, Theta, Alpha, Beta, Gamma — increasing frequency. Alpha (~10 Hz) is the dominant resting rhythm.
- [ ] **Multimodal interaction** improves robustness, accessibility, and reduces cognitive load — a frequent 7-mark question.
- [ ] **Fitts's Law throughput** of 6–10 bits/sec is typical for pointing devices. Lower means inefficient device; higher means expert user or excellent device.
- [ ] **Stevens's Power Law:** Perceived intensity $P = k I^n$. The exponent $n$ is modality-specific (e.g., $n \approx 0.5$ for luminance, $n \approx 1.0$ for electric shock).
- [ ] **Haptic feedback types:** Vibrotactile, Force, Electrotactile, Ultrasound mid-air. Know one application for each.
- [ ] **Earcons vs Auditory Icons:** Earcons = abstract synthesized tones; Auditory icons = recorded natural sounds.
- [ ] **Sonification** = data → sound parameter mapping. Used in Geiger counters, ECG monitors, volcano monitoring.
- [ ] **SNR in dB:** $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(A_s / A_n)$. Higher dB = cleaner signal.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for HMI Technology Classification:**
> **"VIBES"** = **V**isual, **I**nteractive (touch), **B**rain-based, **E**ar-based, **S**kin-based (haptic). This covers the five primary sensory channels modern HMI technologies leverage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Existing Scientific and Commercial Projects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT753 - HUMAN-MACHINE INTERFACE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Existing Scientific and Commercial Projects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** refers to the hardware and software components that facilitate interaction between a human operator and a machine, system, or device. In the context of **Existing Scientific and Commercial Projects**, the term encompasses deployed, real-world implementations of HMI technologies that have been validated through research, industry adoption, or consumer markets.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, "Existing Scientific and Commercial Projects" refers to a survey and critical analysis of notable HMI systems that are currently operational across:

- **Scientific Domains**: Space exploration, biomedical research, neuroscience, particle physics, and high-performance computing environments.
- **Commercial Domains**: Consumer electronics, automotive systems, medical devices, industrial automation, and entertainment systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: The classification of projects must be understood under the dual lens of **Technological Maturity** (TRL – Technology Readiness Level) and **Human Factors Engineering** principles, where TRL 1 is basic research and **TRL 9** represents proven operational deployment in commercial markets.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy — The "Translator at a Global Conference"**

Imagine you are at an international conference where speakers of 50 different languages are presenting. To make communication possible, you need **translators** — people who understand both the speaker (machine) and the listener (human). An HMI is precisely this translator.

- **The Speaker (Machine)** generates signals in its native "language" — voltages, sensor data, binary codes, or radio frequencies.
- **The Listener (Human)** perceives information through sight, sound, touch, and motion.
- **The Translator (HMI)** converts machine-language into human-perceivable formats and vice versa.

Now, **Scientific HMI projects** are like the translators used in **cutting-edge diplomatic summits** (e.g., NASA mission control, CERN) where the highest accuracy is paramount, even if the system is complex and expensive. **Commercial HMI projects** are like **mass-market translation apps** (e.g., Google Translate, Siri) — designed for accessibility, affordability, and everyday use.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are used to evaluate existing HMI projects:

- **Fitts's Law Index of Difficulty (ID)**: $ID = \log_2\left(\frac{A}{W} + 1\right)$ where $A$ is the distance to target and $W$ is the target width.
- **Stewart's Human Throughput**: Average human information processing rate of approximately **50 bits per second** for motor tasks.
- **NASA TLX Score**: Task Load Index, ranging from **0 to 100**, used to evaluate cognitive workload in scientific HMI systems.
- **System Usability Scale (SUS)**: A standardized questionnaire yielding a score from **0 to 100**, with **> 68** considered above-average usability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway**: An "Existing Project" in HMI parlance is one that has crossed the prototype barrier (TRL 6+) and is either in active research deployment (scientific) or available in the consumer/industrial market (commercial).

## 1.4 Classification at a Glance

| Dimension | Scientific Projects | Commercial Projects |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Goal | Research, discovery, accuracy | Usability, profit, accessibility |
| Cost | High (often public-funded) | Optimized for mass production |
| User Base | Specialized experts | General consumers |
| Example | NASA Robonaut, BCI at MIT | iPhone Touchscreen, Alexa |
| Evaluation Metric | Task accuracy, mission success | SUS score, market share |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework for Classifying HMI Projects

Existing HMI projects can be classified along **three primary axes**:

### Axis 1 — Interaction Modality
- **Visual-based**: Displays, AR/VR, gesture recognition.
- **Auditory-based**: Voice assistants, audio alerts, sonification.
- **Haptic-based**: Force feedback, vibrotactile, exoskeletons.
- **Neural-based**: Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI), EEG-controlled systems.
- **Multimodal**: Combining two or more modalities (e.g., voice + gesture in smart homes).

### Axis 2 — Application Domain
- **Aerospace & Space Exploration**
- **Medical & Biomedical Engineering**
- **Automotive & Transportation**
- **Industrial Automation & Robotics**
- **Consumer Electronics & IoT**
- **Defense & Military Systems**

### Axis 3 — Level of Autonomy
- **Human-operated**: Full human control (e.g., manual surgical robots).
- **Human-supervised**: Machine executes, human intervenes (e.g., autopilot).
- **Human-collaborative**: Shared control (e.g., cobots in factories).
- **Fully autonomous**: Minimal human input (e.g., Roomba).

## 2.2 Why and How Behind Each Classification

- **Why classify?** To enable systematic comparison of design choices, identify reusable patterns, and avoid redundant research.
- **How to classify?** Begin with the **primary user** (scientist, consumer, patient, operator) and trace the dominant interaction modality.
- **The 'How' of evaluation**: Scientific projects use **objective metrics** (reaction time, error rate), while commercial projects use **subjective metrics** (user satisfaction, NPS — Net Promoter Score).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the key formulas, metrics, and reference values used in the analysis of existing HMI projects.

| Formula / Metric | Mathematical Form | Variables | Application in HMI |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fitts's Law | $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{A}{W} + 1\right)$ | $MT$ = Movement Time, $A$ = Distance, $W$ = Width | Predicting pointing/selection time in GUIs |
| Index of Difficulty | $ID = \log_2\left(\frac{A}{W} + 1\right)$ | $A$, $W$ | Quantifying UI task complexity |
| Throughput | $TP = \frac{ID_{avg}}{MT_{avg}}$ | bits/sec | Evaluating interface efficiency |
| Steering Law | $T = a + b \cdot \frac{A}{W}$ | $A$ = path length, $W$ = width | Tunnels, sliders in mobile UIs |
| Hick's Law | $RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ | $n$ = number of choices | Menu design response time |
| NASA TLX | Weighted average of 6 subscales | Mental, Physical, Temporal, Effort, Frustration, Performance | Workload in scientific HMIs |
| SUS Score | $\sum$ of 10 item scores $\times 2.5$ | Likert 1–5 | Commercial usability benchmarking |
| Speech Recognition WER | $WER = \frac{S+D+I}{N} \times 100\%$ | $S$ = substitutions, $D$ = deletions, $I$ = insertions, $N$ = words | Voice HMI accuracy evaluation |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace**: SpaceX Dragon capsule uses a **touchscreen-based ECLSS** (Environmental Control and Life Support System) HMI, reducing cockpit clutter and pilot cognitive load. The transition was validated using **NASA TLX** scoring during ISS docking simulations.
- **Medical**: The **Da Vinci Surgical System** employs a master-slave haptic HMI, allowing surgeons to perform minimally invasive procedures with **10x magnification** and tremor filtration.
- **Automotive**: Tesla's Autopilot HMI combines **visual (touchscreen), auditory (voice), and haptic (steering wheel vibration)** modalities, demonstrating the **multimodal design principle** in commercial deployment.
- **Consumer**: Apple's Force Touch and iOS touchscreens redefined mobile HMI by introducing **pressure-sensitive gestures**, which were quantitatively evaluated using Fitts's Law.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight**: A common pattern in successful commercial HMI projects (iPhone, Alexa, Tesla) is the **progressive disclosure principle** — only essential information is shown at any time, reducing cognitive load. This is empirically validated by Hick's Law.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Applying Fitts's Law to a Commercial HMI (iPhone Touchscreen)

**Problem Statement**: A user wants to tap an app icon on an iPhone. The icon is **7 mm wide** ($W$) and is located **35 mm** away from the thumb's resting position ($A$). The empirical constants for touchscreen tapping are $a = 50$ ms and $b = 150$ ms/bit. Estimate the **Movement Time (MT)**.

### Step 1 — Identify the Given Parameters

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 35 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{distance to target}) \\
W &= 7 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{target width}) \\
a &= 50 \text{ ms} \quad (\text{empirical intercept}) \\
b &= 150 \text{ ms/bit} \quad (\text{empirical slope})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic**: These values are typical of mobile touchscreen interactions measured in ISO 9241-9 standards.

### Step 2 — Compute the Index of Difficulty (ID)

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID &= \log_2\left(\frac{A}{W} + 1\right) \\
   &= \log_2\left(\frac{35}{7} + 1\right) \\
   &= \log_2(5 + 1) \\
   &= \log_2(6)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Logarithm

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID &= \frac{\ln(6)}{\ln(2)} \\
   &= \frac{1.7918}{0.6931} \\
   &\approx 2.585 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic**: $\log_2(6)$ represents the number of binary decisions required to uniquely identify the target among equally probable options.

### Step 4 — Substitute into Fitts's Law

$$
\begin{aligned}
MT &= a + b \cdot ID \\
   &= 50 + 150 \cdot 2.585 \\
   &= 50 + 387.75 \\
   &= 437.75 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Interpret the Result

A movement time of approximately **438 ms** is consistent with empirical iPhone usability studies. If Apple were to **double the icon size** to 14 mm, the ID would drop to $\log_2(3.5) \approx 1.81$ bits, reducing MT to **321 ms** — a **26% improvement**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Existing Scientific HMI Projects

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix provides a **systematic comparison** of major scientific HMI projects. KTU examiners often frame Part B questions around such comparisons.

| Project Name | Organization | Year Active | Primary HMI Modality | Application Domain | TRL | Key Innovation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NASA Robonaut 2** | NASA & GM | 2011–Present | Tactile glove + telepresence | Space station robotics | 8 | Anthropomorphic haptic dexterous manipulation |
| **BrainGate** | Brown University | 2004–Present | Intracortical BCI (Neural) | Assistive for paralysis | 7 | Direct neural cursor control |
| **CERN LHC Control** | CERN | 2008–Present | Multimodal (visual + haptic joystick) | Particle physics | 9 | Petabyte-scale real-time monitoring |
| **Mars Rover "Curiosity"** | NASA JPL | 2012–Present | Time-delayed command interface | Planetary exploration | 9 | Autonomy with 4–24 min signal delay |
| **Da Vinci Xi** | Intuitive Surgical | 2014–Present | Master-slave haptic | Robotic surgery | 9 | 3D HD vision + EndoWrist instruments |
| **DeepMind AlphaFold** | DeepMind (Scientific) | 2020–Present | Conversational + visual | Computational biology | 9 | AI-augmented scientific discovery HMI |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Existing Commercial HMI Projects

| Product | Company | Launch Year | HMI Modality | Target User | SUS Score (Reported) | Market Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **iPhone (Multi-Touch)** | Apple | 2007 | Capacitive multi-touch | Consumer | ~85 | Active |
| **Amazon Echo (Alexa)** | Amazon | 2014 | Far-field voice + light ring | Consumer | ~78 | Active |
| **Tesla Model S Touchscreen** | Tesla | 2012 | 17" capacitive touchscreen | Driver | ~72 | Active |
| **Microsoft HoloLens 2** | Microsoft | 2019 | Mixed reality gesture + eye tracking | Enterprise | ~68 | Active |
| **Neuralink N1** | Neuralink | 2024 (Human Trials) | Invasive BCI | Medical/Consumer | N/A (Research) | Early Commercial |
| **Sony PlayStation VR2** | Sony | 2023 | Haptic + eye tracking + VR | Gaming | ~80 | Active |

## 3.4 Critical Evaluation of a Specific Project: **BrainGate** (Detailed Walkthrough)

**Background**: BrainGate is a brain-computer interface developed at Brown University, allowing paralyzed individuals to control a computer cursor or robotic arm using decoded neural signals from a microelectrode array implanted in the motor cortex.

**Sub-step 1 — Input Acquisition**: A **96-channel microelectrode array** records neural spikes at **30 kHz** sampling rate.

**Sub-step 2 — Signal Processing**: Spikes are filtered (300–3000 Hz bandpass) and sorted using **PCA-based clustering**. The output is a per-channel spike train.

**Sub-step 3 — Decoding**: A **Kalman filter** or **LSTM neural network** maps spike trains to cursor velocity:

$$
v_t = A \cdot v_{t-1} + W \cdot x_t + \text{noise}
$$

where $x_t$ is the spike-rate vector and $W$ is the decoder weight matrix.

**Sub-step 4 — Output Rendering**: Cursor position is updated on screen at **60 Hz** refresh rate.

**Sub-step 5 — Closed-Loop Adaptation**: The decoder is recalibrated weekly using **closed-loop point-and-click tasks** to account for neural drift.

**Performance Metric**: Achieved **~90% target selection accuracy** for a 2D cursor, with information throughput of approximately **3–4 bits/sec** — far below the **50 bits/sec** motor task ceiling, indicating substantial room for improvement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in KTU Context**: Examiners may ask students to **identify the type of HMI** (invasive BCI), **list its components**, and **justify its TRL rating**. BrainGate is rated TRL 7 because it works in clinical trials but is not yet a deployable consumer product.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Classification of Existing HMI Projects

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Existing HMI Projects] --> B[Scientific Projects]
    A --> C[Commercial Projects]
    A --> D[Defence Projects]

    B --> B1[Space Exploration HMI]
    B --> B2[Biomedical BCI HMI]
    B --> B3[High Energy Physics HMI]
    B --> B4[Computational Science HMI]

    B1 --> B1a[NASA Robonaut]
    B1 --> B1b[Mars Rover Curiosity]
    B1 --> B1c[ISS Touchscreen Interfaces]

    B2 --> B2a[BrainGate]
    B2 --> B2b[Neuralink]
    B2 --> B2c[Emotiv EEG]

    B3 --> B3a[CERN LHC Control]
    B3 --> B3b[ITER Tokamak Control]

    B4 --> B4a[DeepMind AlphaFold]
    B4 --> B4b[SETI Radio Telescope HMI]

    C --> C1[Consumer Electronics]
    C --> C2[Automotive HMI]
    C --> C3[Medical Devices]
    C --> C4[Industrial Automation]

    C1 --> C1a[Apple iPhone]
    C1 --> C1b[Amazon Alexa]
    C1 --> C1c[Microsoft HoloLens]

    C2 --> C2a[Tesla Touchscreen]
    C2 --> C2b[BMW iDrive]
    C2 --> C2c[Android Auto]

    C3 --> C3a[Da Vinci Surgical Robot]
    C3 --> C3b[Insulet Omnipod]
    C3 --> C3c[Philips Patient Monitor]

    C4 --> C4a[Siemens SIMATIC HMI]
    C4 --> C4b[ABB Robot Studio]
    C4 --> C4c[Universal Robots Cobot]

    D --> D1[F-35 Helmet HMI]
    D --> D2[Drone GCS HMI]
    D --> D3[Submarine Sonar HMI]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Multimodal Commercial HMI (e.g., Tesla Cockpit)

```mermaid
graph LR
    user1[Driver User] --> input1[Touchscreen Input Layer]
    user1 --> input2[Voice Input Microphone Array]
    user1 --> input3[Steering Wheel Capacitive Sensors]

    input1 --> proc1[Central HMI Processor]
    input2 --> proc1
    input3 --> proc1

    proc1 --> proc2[Multimodal Fusion Engine]
    proc2 --> proc3[Context Awareness Module]
    proc3 --> proc4[Decision Arbitration Logic]

    proc4 --> out1[17 inch Touchscreen Display]
    proc4 --> out2[Audio Feedback Speakers]
    proc4 --> out3[Haptic Steering Vibration]

    out1 --> user1
    out2 --> user1
    out3 --> user1

    proc1 --> telemetry[Telemetry and Logging Module]
    telemetry --> cloud[Over the Air Cloud Updates]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: HMI Project Evaluation Pipeline (KTU Analytical Framework)

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Existing HMI Project] --> id[Step 1: Identify Domain]
    id --> mod[Step 2: Determine Interaction Modality]
    mod --> user[Step 3: Define Primary User Persona]
    user --> met[Step 4: Select Evaluation Metric]
    met --> ev[Step 5: Empirical Evaluation]
    ev --> out[Output: Usability Report and TRL Rating]

    met --> m2[Subjective: SUS, NASA TLX, NPS]

```

## 4.4 Text-Based Schematic: Typical BCI-Based Scientific HMI Pipeline

```
[Neural Signal Source: Motor Cortex]
            |
            v
   (96-Channel Microelectrode Array)
            |
            v
   (Analog Front-End: Amplification + Filtering, 300-3000 Hz)
            |
            v
   (Analog-to-Digital Conversion, 30 kHz, 16-bit)
            |
            v
   (Spike Detection and Sorting, PCA / Threshold)
            |
            v
   (Feature Extraction: Firing Rates per Channel)
            |
            v
   (Decoder: Kalman Filter / LSTM)
            |
            v
   (Output Command: Cursor Velocity / Robotic Arm)
            |
            v
   (Feedback to User: Visual Cursor / Haptic Cuff)
            |
            v
   (Closed-Loop Adaptation: Weekly Recalibration)
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define the term "Human-Machine Interface" and give two examples of commercial HMI systems.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** is the hardware and software system that enables communication and interaction between a human operator and a machine. It includes input devices (sensors, keyboards, microphones), output devices (displays, speakers, haptic actuators), and the software logic that translates between human intent and machine commands.

**Two examples of commercial HMI systems:**
1. **Apple iPhone Multi-Touch Display** — A capacitive touchscreen HMI that interprets finger gestures (tap, swipe, pinch) as commands.
2. **Amazon Echo (Alexa)** — A voice-based HMI using far-field microphone arrays and natural language processing.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: What is meant by "Technology Readiness Level (TRL)"? Why is it relevant when classifying existing HMI projects?**

**Model Answer:**
**Technology Readiness Level (TRL)** is a 9-point scale (1–9) used to assess the maturity of a technology, from basic research (TRL 1) to proven operational deployment (TRL 9). It was originally developed by **NASA** and is now adopted by the **European Space Agency** and many research institutions.

**Relevance in classifying HMI projects:**
- It allows systematic comparison of scientific vs. commercial projects.
- A scientific project (e.g., BrainGate) is typically at TRL 6–7 (system demonstrated in operational environment), while a commercial product (e.g., iPhone) is at **TRL 9** (fully deployed).
- TRL helps identify which projects are suitable for technology transfer from lab to market.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Brain-Computer Interface (BCI) used in a scientific HMI project. Discuss its working principle and list two real-world applications. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Compare and contrast Fitts's Law and Hick's Law in the context of commercial touchscreen HMI design. Apply Fitts's Law to compute the movement time for tapping a 5 mm wide button located 30 mm away, given $a = 60$ ms and $b = 130$ ms/bit. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block Diagram of a Generic BCI-Based HMI:**

```
[Brain Signal Source] --> [Signal Acquisition] --> [Pre-processing]
            --> [Feature Extraction] --> [Translation Algorithm]
            --> [Output Device] --> [User Feedback Loop]
```

**Working Principle:**
1. **Signal Acquisition**: Electrical activity from the brain is recorded using **non-invasive (EEG)** or **invasive (microelectrode arrays)** methods.
2. **Pre-processing**: The raw signal is amplified and filtered to remove noise (e.g., 50 Hz powerline interference, muscle artifacts).
3. **Feature Extraction**: Relevant features like **event-related potentials (ERP)**, **P300 amplitude**, or **mu-rhythm power** are extracted.
4. **Translation Algorithm**: Machine learning classifiers (e.g., **LDA**, **SVM**, or **deep neural networks**) decode the features into commands.
5. **Output**: Commands are sent to external devices (cursor, wheelchair, robotic arm).
6. **Feedback**: Visual or haptic feedback closes the loop for user adaptation.

**Two Real-World Applications:**
- **BrainGate** (Brown University): Enables paralyzed patients to control a computer cursor or robotic arm using motor cortex signals.
- **P300 Speller**: Allows locked-in patients to type characters by focusing on flashing letters, exploiting the P300 ERP.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Fitts's Law | Hick's Law |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Predicts movement time to a target | Predicts decision time given choices |
| Domain | Motor tasks (pointing, tapping) | Cognitive decision-making |
| Formula | $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{A}{W} + 1\right)$ | $RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ |
| Application in HMI | Button design, touch target size | Menu hierarchy, dropdown options |

**Fitts's Law Calculation:**

**Given:** $A = 30$ mm, $W = 5$ mm, $a = 60$ ms, $b = 130$ ms/bit.

**Step 1** — Compute the Index of Difficulty:

$$
ID = \log_2\left(\frac{A}{W} + 1\right) = \log_2\left(\frac{30}{5} + 1\right) = \log_2(7) \approx 2.807 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2** — Apply Fitts's Law:

$$
MT = a + b \cdot ID = 60 + 130 \cdot 2.807 = 60 + 364.91 = 424.91 \text{ ms}
$$

**Conclusion:** The predicted movement time is approximately **425 ms**. Designers can reduce this by increasing the button width (improving usability), demonstrating the practical utility of Fitts's Law in commercial HMI.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q: (a)** Describe the architecture and key features of the **Da Vinci Surgical System** as an example of a commercial medical HMI. Explain how haptic feedback is implemented. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** With a suitable block diagram, describe the multimodal HMI used in modern Tesla vehicles. Discuss how the principle of "progressive disclosure" enhances driver safety, supported by Hick's Law. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Da Vinci Surgical System Architecture:**

The Da Vinci system (by **Intuitive Surgical**) consists of three main components:

1. **Surgeon Console**: The master controller where the surgeon views a **3D HD image** and manipulates hand and foot controls.
2. **Patient Cart**: Holds up to **four robotic arms** that hold surgical instruments and a 3D camera.
3. **Vision Cart**: Houses the image-processing hardware.

**Key HMI Features:**
- **3D High-Definition Vision System** with **10x magnification**.
- **EndoWrist instruments** with 7 degrees of freedom, mimicking human wrist motion.
- **Tremor filtration** to scale down hand tremors.
- **Motion scaling** (e.g., 3:1 ratio) for precise micro-movements.

**Haptic Feedback Implementation:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Da Vinci system **does not provide true force feedback** to the surgeon's hands. Instead, it provides **visual and auditory cues** (e.g., instrument strain gauges trigger audio beeps). This is a known limitation. Newer research systems (e.g., **Haptic Da Vinci prototypes**) are introducing bilateral force feedback using **force sensors on instrument tips** and **actuated grippers** at the console.

**Advantages:**
- Reduced surgeon fatigue due to ergonomic console.
- Enhanced precision in minimally invasive surgery.
- Lower patient trauma and faster recovery.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Block Diagram of Tesla Multimodal HMI:**

```
[Driver] <--> [17 inch Touchscreen] (Visual)
[Driver] <--> [Voice Microphones] (Auditory Input)
[Driver] <--> [Steering Wheel Sensors] (Haptic Input)
        |
        v
[Central HMI Processor running Linux-based OS]
        |
        v
[Multimodal Fusion and Context Engine]
        |
        v
[Outputs: Display, Audio Speakers, Seat Vibration]
```

**Multimodal Inputs/Outputs:**
- **Visual**: Large central touchscreen for navigation, climate, media.
- **Auditory**: Voice commands ("Navigate to home"), audio alerts.
- **Haptic**: Steering wheel vibration for lane departure warnings.

**Progressive Disclosure & Hick's Law:**

**Progressive Disclosure** is a design principle where only essential information is shown initially, with deeper details accessible on demand. This reduces the **number of choices ($n$)** the driver must process at any moment.

According to **Hick's Law**:

$$
RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)
$$

**Step-by-step reasoning:**
- If Tesla displayed **all 50 vehicle settings** on one screen, $n = 50$, leading to $RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(51) \approx a + 5.67b$ — a long response time.
- By using progressive disclosure (grouping settings into 5 categories of 10 each), the driver first selects a category ($n = 5$, $RT = a + 2.32b$), then a setting within ($n = 10$, $RT = a + 3.46b$).
- The **total cognitive load** is reduced because each decision step is shorter, enhancing driver safety.

**Conclusion:** Progressive disclosure, validated by Hick's Law, is a key reason Tesla's HMI achieves high usability scores while minimizing driver distraction.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do not confuse modalities with domains.** Touchscreen = modality; Automotive = domain. Examiners deduct 1 mark for such confusion.
> 2. **Always show units in Fitts's Law calculations.** Writing $ID = 2.585$ without "bits" costs 0.5 mark.
> 3. **Cite TRL correctly.** Most deployed commercial products are TRL 9; scientific prototypes are typically TRL 6–7.
> 4. **Da Vinci has NO true haptic feedback** in the standard commercial model — students who claim otherwise lose marks.
> 5. **Use block diagrams** — textual explanations alone are insufficient for full marks in 7-mark sub-parts.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Existing Scientific and Commercial HMI Projects**

- **Definition**: HMI = hardware + software that mediates human-machine communication.
- **Two Main Categories**: **Scientific** (research-driven, high accuracy) and **Commercial** (usability-driven, mass-market).
- **TRL Scale**: 1 (basic research) → **9 (operational deployment)**. Most commercial products = TRL 9.
- **Key Scientific Projects to Remember**:
  - **BrainGate** — invasive BCI for paralysis.
  - **NASA Robonaut 2** — anthropomorphic space robot.
  - **CERN LHC Control** — multimodal particle physics HMI.
  - **Da Vinci Surgical System** — master-slave robotic surgery.
- **Key Commercial Projects to Remember**:
  - **Apple iPhone** — capacitive multi-touch.
  - **Amazon Alexa** — far-field voice assistant.
  - **Tesla Touchscreen** — 17" automotive HMI.
  - **Microsoft HoloLens 2** — mixed reality.
- **Fitts's Law**: $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2(A/W + 1)$ — used for target/touch evaluation.
- **Hick's Law**: $RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ — used for menu/decision evaluation.
- **SUS Score**: > 68 = above-average usability; **> 80 = excellent** (iPhone ~85).
- **NASA TLX**: Workload metric (0–100) used in scientific HMI evaluations.
- **Design Principles**:
  - **Progressive Disclosure** (Hick's Law application).
  - **Multimodal Interaction** (visual + auditory + haptic).
  - **Fitts's Law Compliance** (adequate target size).
  - **Fitts's Law Minimum Target Size**: For touch UIs, Apple guideline = **44 × 44 pixels minimum**.
- **Common Confusion Points to Avoid**:
  - Haptic ≠ tactile (haptic = force feedback; tactile = surface texture).
  - AR ≠ VR (AR overlays reality; VR replaces it).
  - BCI ≠ BMI (BCI = direct brain interface; BMI = broader muscle-signal interfaces).
- **High-Yield One-Liners for KTU**:
  - "The iPhone's success is rooted in Fitts's Law compliance with **44pt** minimum target size."
  - "BrainGate achieves ~3 bits/sec, far below the 50 bits/sec motor ceiling."
  - "Tesla's progressive disclosure reduces Hick's Law decision time by **grouping choices hierarchically**."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Models of Interaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT753 - HUMAN-MACHINE INTERFACE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Models of Interaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Models of Interaction** are formal conceptual frameworks that describe, analyze, and predict the **dynamics of communication** that occur between a **user** and an **interactive system**. They decompose the interaction loop into identifiable components, stages, and information flows, allowing designers to reason about *usability* before a single line of code is written.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, three canonical models form the spine of this topic:

1. **Donald Norman's Model of Interaction (Seven Stages of Action)** – explains the *psychological* lifecycle of a user trying to accomplish a goal.
2. **The GOMS Model (Goals, Operators, Methods, Selection Rules)** by Card, Moran & Newell – a *predictive, quantitative* model used to estimate task execution time.
3. **The Abowd & Beale Interaction Framework** – a generalized *four-component architectural* model that formalizes the user-system loop using four distinct states and four translations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"**
>
> Think of an interactive system as a **restaurant kitchen**:
> - You (the **User**) are a customer at a table holding a **menu** (your goal: "I want pasta").
> - The **waiter** is the **Input** channel — they take your words and convert them into a **kitchen ticket** (a structured command).
> - The **chef and the kitchen** form the **System** — they read the ticket and prepare the dish.
> - The **plated dish** arriving at your table is the **Output** — the visible state change in the world.
>
> If the waiter speaks a different language (**Gulf of Execution**), your goal is lost. If the dish arrives in a covered cloche you can't see through (**Gulf of Evaluation**), you cannot tell whether the chef did it right. *Models of Interaction are the blueprints that ensure the customer, waiter, ticket, kitchen, and dish all speak the same language.*

### 1.3 Why These Models Matter in Engineering

- **Predictive Power**: GOMS can forecast a trained user's task time *before* prototyping — saving months of iterative testing.
- **Diagnostic Power**: Norman's gulfs pinpoint *exactly* where in the interaction chain a usability failure occurs.
- **Architectural Clarity**: Abowd & Beale's framework gives software engineers a clean separation of concerns (UI, input layer, core logic, output layer).
- **Communication Tool**: All three models provide a shared vocabulary that designers, psychologists, and developers use to discuss usability in **objective** terms.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Donald Norman's Model of Interaction (1988)

Norman (in *The Design of Everyday Things*) argued that every interaction consists of **two bridging activities** separated by **seven stages**.

#### 2.1.1 The Two Gulfs

| Gulf | Definition | Design Implication |
|------|------------|--------------------|
| **Gulf of Execution** | The gap between the *user's intention* and the *physical actions* the system allows. | System affordances must match user mental models. |
| **Gulf of Evaluation** | The gap between the *system's actual state* and the *user's perception* of that state. | Feedback must be immediate, visible, and interpretable. |

#### 2.1.2 The Seven Stages (with Cognitive Mapping)

**Phase A — Execution (Goal → Action)**

1. **Establishing the Goal** — User forms a high-level desire (e.g., "Save my document").
2. **Forming the Intention** — User decides *what* must change to satisfy the goal ("I will use the Save command").
3. **Specifying the Action Sequence** — User mentally selects the *physical* actions ("Click File menu → Click Save → Choose folder").
4. **Executing the Action** — User carries out the physical actions on input devices.

**Phase B — Evaluation (Action → Perception)**

5. **Perceiving the System State** — User observes output (a flash, a sound, a dialog).
6. **Interpreting the State** — User decodes what the observation *means*.
7. **Evaluating the Outcome** — User compares interpretation with the original goal → loop or terminate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight**: The model is *cyclic*. Stages 5–7 feed back into Stage 1 of the next iteration. This is why iterative usability testing is fundamental.

### 2.2 The GOMS Model (Card, Moran & Newell, 1983)

GOMS is a **hierarchical, top-down task-decomposition** model with four components:

| Letter | Component | Meaning | Example |
|--------|-----------|---------|---------|
| **G** | **Goals** | What the user wants to achieve | "Edit a paragraph" |
| **O** | **Operators** | Atomic, system-level actions the user performs | *press-key*, *click-mouse*, *move-mouse*, *home-hands* |
| **M** | **Methods** | Sequences of operators that accomplish a sub-goal | Goal → Method A → Operators |
| **S** | **Selection Rules** | Heuristics that decide *which* method to use when multiple apply | "If experienced, use shortcut; else use menu" |

#### 2.2.1 The Keystroke-Level Model (KLM) — A Specialization of GOMS

Card, Moran & Newell refined GOMS into a *predictive timing model*. The total execution time of a task is the sum of the times spent on each operator:

$$T_{execute} = T_k + T_p + T_h + T_d + T_m + T_r + T_{c}$$

#### 2.2.2 KTU Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Operator | Description | Typical Time |
|--------|----------|-------------|--------------|
| $T_k$ | Keystroke | Press a single key on keyboard | **0.08 s** (skilled typist) |
| $T_p$ | Pointing | Mouse movement to a target | **1.10 s** (Fitts' Law dependent) |
| $T_b$ | Button press | Press/click a mouse button | **0.10 s** (often folded into $T_p$ in KLM) |
| $T_h$ | Homing | Switch hand between keyboard and mouse | **0.40 s** |
| $T_d$ | Drawing | Draw a single line segment with mouse | **0.9 s + 0.16 s × n** strokes |
| $T_m$ | Mental preparation | Cognitive deliberation before action | **1.20 s** |
| $T_r$ | System response | User waits for system to respond | **Actual measured time** |
| $T_c$ | **Concurrent** activity (advanced) | Operator executed in parallel with another | **0.0 s** if eligible, else penalty |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Do not confuse $T_d$ (drawing a line) with $T_m$ (mental act). Students often swap them in numerical problems. Also, $T_b$ is **included inside $T_p$** in the canonical KLM — the official summation formula uses $T_p$ alone, not $T_b$.

#### 2.2.3 Engineering Utility of GOMS/KLM

- **Workload Forecasting**: Estimating expert performance in air traffic control interfaces.
- **Comparative HCI**: Comparing *menu-based* vs *command-line* versions of the same editor.
- **Cost-Benefit**: Justifying UI redesign by showing predicted time savings.

### 2.3 The Abowd & Beale Interaction Framework (1991)

This is the **most general** of the three models. It formalizes interaction as a loop between **four components** exchanging information through **four translations**.

#### 2.3.1 The Four Components

1. **User** — The human, possessing a *Task* and a set of *Needs*.
2. **Input** — The input channel (keyboard, mouse, touch, voice, gesture).
3. **System** — The computational core that processes input and produces output.
4. **Output** — The output channel (screen, speaker, haptic, print).

#### 2.3.2 The Four Translations (and Their Gulfs)

| # | Translation | Direction | Failure Mode |
|---|-------------|-----------|--------------|
| 1 | **Articulation** | User → Input | User cannot *physically express* intent (Gulf of Execution) |
| 2 | **Performance** | Input → System | System misinterprets the raw input |
| 3 | **Presentation** | System → Output | Internal state not rendered correctly |
| 4 | **Observation** | Output → User | User cannot *perceive or interpret* the output (Gulf of Evaluation) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Connection**: Norman's Gulf of Execution = Abowd & Beale's **Articulation** translation. Norman's Gulf of Evaluation = Abowd & Beale's **Observation** translation. Knowing this cross-mapping is a guaranteed 2-mark reward in short-answer sections.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked KLM Numerical — Full Exhaustive Derivation

**Problem (KTU-style):** A user is typing the word `HELLO` in a text editor using the keyboard. Assume the user is a **highly skilled typist** and the system has **zero response delay**. Compute the predicted total execution time $T_{execute}$ for this action.

**Step 1 — Identify the operators involved.**
The user is performing only one type of operator: a single keystroke. The word has 5 letters, so there are **5 keystrokes**.

**Step 2 — List the time per operator.**

$$T_k = 0.08 \text{ s (highly skilled typist)}$$

**Step 3 — Check for any other operators.**
- Is there a mental act before the first keystroke? **Yes — the user must think** $T_m$.
- Is there pointing? **No** (keyboard only).
- Is there homing? **No** (hand already on home row).
- Is there drawing? **No**.
- Is there system response? **No** ($T_r = 0$ given).
- Is there button press? **No** (no mouse).
- Is there a concurrent operator? **No**.

**Step 4 — Substitute into the KLM equation.**

$$T_{execute} = T_m + (n \times T_k) + T_r + T_h + T_d + T_p$$

$$T_{execute} = T_m + (n \times T_k) + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0$$

**Step 5 — Plug the numerical values.**

$$T_{execute} = 1.20 + (5 \times 0.08) + 0$$

$$T_{execute} = 1.20 + 0.40$$

$$\boxed{T_{execute} = 1.60 \text{ seconds}}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked KLM Numerical — Menu Navigation

**Problem:** A novice user wants to perform "File → Save" in a word processor. The user must:
1. Mentally prepare to find the menu.
2. Move the mouse to the **File** menu.
3. Click the File menu.
4. Mentally prepare to find the Save option.
5. Move the mouse to **Save**.
6. Click Save.
7. Mentally prepare.
8. Type the filename `report.docx` (8 characters).
9. Press Enter.

Assume $T_m = 1.20$ s, $T_p = 1.10$ s, $T_k = 0.20$ s (novice typist), $T_h = 0$, $T_d = 0$, $T_r = 0.50$ s (system Save dialog loads).

**Step 1 — Decompose into atomic KLM operators.**

| # | Action | Operator(s) | Time |
|---|--------|-------------|------|
| 1 | Find File menu | $T_m$ | 1.20 |
| 2 | Move mouse to File | $T_p$ | 1.10 |
| 3 | Click File | (included in $T_p$) | 0 |
| 4 | Find Save | $T_m$ | 1.20 |
| 5 | Move to Save | $T_p$ | 1.10 |
| 6 | Click Save | (included in $T_p$) | 0 |
| 7 | Think of name | $T_m$ | 1.20 |
| 8 | Type 8 letters | $8 \times T_k$ | $8 \times 0.20 = 1.60$ |
| 9 | Press Enter | $T_k$ | 0.20 |
| 10 | System opens dialog | $T_r$ | 0.50 |

**Step 2 — Sum all operators.**

$$T_{execute} = 3(T_m) + 2(T_p) + 9(T_k) + T_r + T_h + T_d$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$$T_{execute} = 3(1.20) + 2(1.10) + 9(0.20) + 0.50 + 0 + 0$$

$$T_{execute} = 3.60 + 2.20 + 1.80 + 0.50$$

$$\boxed{T_{execute} = 8.10 \text{ seconds}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Logic for Students**: Notice how **mental preparation** ($T_m$) dominates the total. A common design lesson: *reduce user hesitation by clear iconography and visual cues — this lowers perceived $T_m$ or eliminates it entirely*.

---

### 3.3 Norman's Model — Application Matrix

| Interaction Failure | Where in Norman's Cycle? | Which Gulf? | Typical Fix |
|---------------------|--------------------------|-------------|-------------|
| User cannot find the "Save" button | Stage 3 (Specify Action) | **Execution** | Add visible affordance, label, icon |
| User clicks Save but nothing visible happens | Stage 5 (Perceive) | **Evaluation** | Add loading spinner, sound, status bar |
| Save dialog opens but user misreads "Save vs Save As" | Stage 6 (Interpret) | **Evaluation** | Use clearer terminology, tooltip |
| File is saved but in wrong folder | Stage 7 (Evaluate) | **Evaluation** | Confirm dialog showing path |
| User cannot drag-and-drop a file | Stage 4 (Execute) | **Execution** | Provide conventional drag affordance |

---

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix of the Three Models

| Dimension | Norman's Model | GOMS / KLM | Abowd & Beale |
|-----------|----------------|------------|----------------|
| **Type** | Descriptive / qualitative | Predictive / quantitative | Architectural / formal |
| **Focus** | User psychology | Task execution time | System structure |
| **Granularity** | 7 stages | 6–7 operators | 4 components + 4 translations |
| **Output** | Identifies gulfs | Time estimate (seconds) | Design blueprint |
| **Best For** | Diagnosing UX failures | Comparing design alternatives | Building new systems |
| **Limitation** | Does not predict time | Assumes expert user | Abstract — no timing data |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Norman's Seven Stages — Cyclic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    userA["USER MIND"]:::user
    s1["1. Goal Formation"]:::exec
    s2["2. Form Intention"]:::exec
    s3["3. Specify Action"]:::exec
    s4["4. Execute Action"]:::exec
    sys["INTERACTIVE SYSTEM"]:::system
    s5["5. Perceive State"]:::eval
    s6["6. Interpret State"]:::eval
    s7["7. Evaluate Outcome"]:::eval
    gulfE{{"GULF OF EXECUTION"}}:::gulf
    gulfV{{"GULF OF EVALUATION"}}:::gulf

    userA --> s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4
    s4 --> gulfE --> sys
    sys --> gulfV --> s5
    s5 --> s6 --> s7 --> userA

    classDef user fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
    classDef exec fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000
    classDef eval fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000
    classDef system fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#6d28d9,color:#000
    classDef gulf fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Abowd & Beale Interaction Framework — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    user["USER (Task & Needs)"]:::user
    inp["INPUT CHANNEL
Keyboard / Mouse / Touch / Voice"]:::inp
    sys["SYSTEM CORE
Logic + State + Computation"]:::sys
    out["OUTPUT CHANNEL
Display / Audio / Haptics"]:::out

    t1[/"1. ARTICULATION"/]:::tr
    t2[/"2. PERFORMANCE"/]:::tr
    t3[/"3. PRESENTATION"/]:::tr
    t4[/"4. OBSERVATION"/]:::tr

    user -- t1 --> inp -- t2 --> sys -- t3 --> out -- t4 --> user

    classDef user fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#a16207,color:#000
    classDef inp fill:#cffafe,stroke:#0e7490,color:#000
    classDef sys fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#5b21b6,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    classDef tr fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000
```

### 4.3 GOMS Hierarchical Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    g1["GOAL: Edit a Paragraph"]:::goal
    m1["METHOD A: Use Menu"]:::method
    m2["METHOD B: Use Shortcut Ctrl+E"]:::method
    sel{{"SELECTION RULE:
If expert use METHOD B
Else use METHOD A"}}:::sel

    o1["O: home-hands to mouse"]:::op
    o2["O: point to Edit menu"]:::op
    o3["O: click Edit"]:::op
    o4["O: point to Edit option"]:::op
    o5["O: click Edit option"]:::op
    o6["O: home-hands to keyboard"]:::op
    o7["O: type replacement text"]:::op

    g1 --> sel
    sel --> m1
    sel --> m2
    m1 --> o1 --> o2 --> o3 --> o4 --> o5 --> o6 --> o7
    m2 -.shortcut path.-> o7

    classDef goal fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#000
    classDef method fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000
    classDef sel fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000
    classDef op fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,color:#000
```

### 4.4 KLM Operator Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Analyze the Task"]:::start
    q1{"Action involves a key press?"}:::q
    q2{"Action involves mouse movement?"}:::q
    q3{"Hand switches device?"}:::q
    q4{"User pauses to think?"}:::q
    q5{"System needs time to respond?"}:::q

    k["Add T_k = 0.08 s"]:::op
    p["Add T_p = 1.10 s"]:::op
    h["Add T_h = 0.40 s"]:::op
    m["Add T_m = 1.20 s"]:::op
    r["Add T_r = measured system time"]:::op
    sum["Sum all operators
T_execute = T_k + T_p + T_h + T_m + T_r + T_d"]:::sum

    start --> q1
    q1 -- Yes --> k
    q1 -- No --> q2
    q2 -- Yes --> p
    q2 -- No --> q4
    q4 -- Yes --> m
    q4 -- No --> q3
    q3 -- Yes --> h
    q3 -- No --> q5
    q5 -- Yes --> r
    q5 -- No --> sum
    k --> q2
    p --> q3
    h --> q5
    m --> sum
    r --> sum

    classDef start fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
    classDef q fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#4338ca,color:#000
    classDef op fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    classDef sum fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,color:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the seven stages of action proposed by Donald Norman in the model of interaction.**

**Model Answer:**
Donald Norman's Seven Stages of Action (1988) divides user-system interaction into two phases — **Execution** and **Evaluation** — separated by the interactive system.

- **Execution Phase** (Stages 1–4): The user moves from a high-level goal to a physical action.
    1. *Establishing the Goal* — forming the desired outcome.
    2. *Forming the Intention* — deciding what to change.
    3. *Specifying the Action Sequence* — selecting concrete steps.
    4. *Executing the Action* — performing the steps via input devices.
- **Evaluation Phase** (Stages 5–7): The user moves from system output back to a revised understanding.
    5. *Perceiving the System State* — observing outputs.
    6. *Interpreting the State* — making sense of what was perceived.
    7. *Evaluating the Outcome* — comparing interpretation with the original goal.

The Execution side is bridged by the **Gulf of Execution**, and the Evaluation side by the **Gulf of Evaluation**. *(3 marks)*

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Briefly explain the GOMS model and its four components.**

**Model Answer:**
The **GOMS Model** (Card, Moran & Newell, 1983) is a *predictive, hierarchical* model of human-computer interaction that decomposes a task into goal hierarchies, the methods that satisfy them, the primitive operators used, and selection rules for choosing between methods.

- **Goals (G):** The desired state the user wants to reach.
- **Operators (O):** The atomic actions the system supports (e.g., key-press, click).
- **Methods (M):** Sequences of operators that accomplish a sub-goal.
- **Selection Rules (S):** Heuristics that decide which method to apply when multiple exist.

A specialization called the **Keystroke-Level Model (KLM)** extends GOMS with quantitative time estimates for each operator, enabling prediction of expert task time. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **ESE Module Internal Choice**: Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain Donald Norman's model of interaction in detail. Discuss the two **gulfs** in the model and how each can be reduced through good interface design. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **GOMS model** and its components. Using the **Keystroke-Level Model (KLM)**, estimate the time taken by a user to perform the following task: *Move the mouse to the Edit menu, click it, point to the Copy option, click it, then return the hand to the keyboard and type the letter X.* Given: $T_p = 1.10$ s, $T_m = 1.20$ s, $T_k = 0.08$ s, $T_h = 0.40$ s, $T_r = 0$, $T_d = 0$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution to (a):**

Norman's model describes a **7-stage cyclic process** divided into an **Execution Phase** (Stages 1–4) and an **Evaluation Phase** (Stages 5–7).

| # | Stage | Phase | Description |
|---|-------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Goal Formation | Execution | User defines the desired outcome |
| 2 | Form Intention | Execution | User decides what to change |
| 3 | Specify Action | Execution | User selects physical actions |
| 4 | Execute Action | Execution | User performs actions on the system |
| 5 | Perceive State | Evaluation | User observes system feedback |
| 6 | Interpret State | Evaluation | User decodes the observation |
| 7 | Evaluate Outcome | Evaluation | User judges success vs goal |

**Gulf of Execution** spans Stages 1–4. It is the distance between the user's intention and the actions the system permits. It is reduced by:
- Clear **affordances** (buttons that look pressable)
- Good **mapping** between controls and effects
- **Constraints** that prevent invalid actions
- Visible **signifiers** of how to operate the system

**Gulf of Evaluation** spans Stages 5–7. It is the distance between the system's actual state and the user's perception of it. It is reduced by:
- **Immediate feedback** after every action
- **Visible state indicators** (e.g., cursor change, status bar)
- **Appropriate feedback channels** (visual, auditory, haptic)
- **Natural mappings** between cause and effect

*(Award 1 mark per stage identification, 2 marks for the gulfs, 2 marks for design reduction strategies.)*

**Model Solution to (b):**

**GOMS Components:**
- **Goals** – what the user wants.
- **Operators** – atomic system actions.
- **Methods** – sequences of operators.
- **Selection Rules** – logic to choose methods.

**KLM Operator Decomposition:**

| # | Action | Operator | Time (s) |
|---|--------|----------|----------|
| 1 | Mental prep to find Edit | $T_m$ | 1.20 |
| 2 | Move mouse to Edit | $T_p$ | 1.10 |
| 3 | Click Edit | (in $T_p$) | 0 |
| 4 | Mental prep to find Copy | $T_m$ | 1.20 |
| 5 | Move mouse to Copy | $T_p$ | 1.10 |
| 6 | Click Copy | (in $T_p$) | 0 |
| 7 | Move hand to keyboard | $T_h$ | 0.40 |
| 8 | Type X | $T_k$ | 0.08 |

**Applying the KLM formula:**

$$T_{execute} = 2(T_m) + 2(T_p) + 1(T_h) + 1(T_k) + T_r + T_d$$

$$T_{execute} = 2(1.20) + 2(1.10) + 0.40 + 0.08 + 0 + 0$$

$$T_{execute} = 2.40 + 2.20 + 0.40 + 0.08$$

$$\boxed{T_{execute} = 5.08 \text{ seconds}}$$

*(Award 2 marks for GOMS description, 3 marks for operator table, 2 marks for substitution and final answer.)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2022]`

**(a)** Describe the **Abowd and Beale Interaction Framework** with a neat block diagram. Explain the four translations that occur during interaction. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of the **Keystroke-Level Model**, predict the time taken to perform the following: *Press key A, point to the screen, click, point to another icon, click, then type the word "KTU".* Use $T_k = 0.20$ s, $T_p = 1.10$ s, $T_m = 1.20$ s, $T_h = 0.40$ s, $T_r = 0$, $T_d = 0$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution to (a):**

The **Abowd & Beale Framework** (1991) is a general model consisting of **four core components**: **User**, **Input**, **System**, and **Output**. Interaction is described as a continuous loop where the user and the system exchange information through four **translations**.

| # | Translation | Between | What it does |
|---|-------------|---------|--------------|
| 1 | **Articulation** | User → Input | Converts user intent into physical input gestures |
| 2 | **Performance** | Input → System | Converts raw input into commands the system can interpret |
| 3 | **Presentation** | System → Output | Converts internal system state into perceivable output signals |
| 4 | **Observation** | Output → User | User perceives and interprets the output |

> The **Gulf of Execution** corresponds to a failure in **Articulation**, while the **Gulf of Evaluation** corresponds to a failure in **Observation**.

*(Award 2 marks for the four components, 2 marks for the four translations, 2 marks for gulf correspondence, 1 mark for the diagram.)*

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Decompose each action into KLM operators.**

| # | Action | Operator | Time (s) |
|---|--------|----------|----------|
| 1 | Press key A (no mental prep assumed) | $T_k$ | 0.20 |
| 2 | Mental prep to find first target | $T_m$ | 1.20 |
| 3 | Point to first target | $T_p$ | 1.10 |
| 4 | Click first target | (in $T_p$) | 0 |
| 5 | Mental prep to find second icon | $T_m$ | 1.20 |
| 6 | Point to second icon | $T_p$ | 1.10 |
| 7 | Click second icon | (in $T_p$) | 0 |
| 8 | Home hands back to keyboard | $T_h$ | 0.40 |
| 9 | Type 3 letters of "KTU" | $3 \times T_k$ | $3 \times 0.20 = 0.60$ |

**Step 2 — Apply the KLM summation formula.**

$$T_{execute} = 2(T_m) + 2(T_p) + 1(T_h) + 4(T_k) + T_r + T_d$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$$T_{execute} = 2(1.20) + 2(1.10) + 0.40 + 4(0.20) + 0 + 0$$

$$T_{execute} = 2.40 + 2.20 + 0.40 + 0.80$$

$$\boxed{T_{execute} = 5.80 \text{ seconds}}$$

*(Award 2 marks for the operator table, 3 marks for substitution into the formula, 2 marks for the final numeric answer with units.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting $T_m$ (mental prep)** — In KLM, *every* new thought segment incurs $T_m$. Students often omit it and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Counting clicks as a separate operator** — A mouse click is *included inside* $T_p$. Counting it again as $T_b$ inflates the time.
> 3. **Mixing Norman's Gulf of Execution with Gulf of Evaluation** — Execution is **action** (Stages 1–4); Evaluation is **perception** (Stages 5–7). Reversing these loses full marks.
> 4. **Confusing Articulation with Observation in Abowd & Beale** — Articulation is *user → input*; Observation is *output → user*.
> 5. **Not showing units in final KLM answer** — Always write the answer in **seconds (s)**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Viva & ESE**
>
> - **Norman's Model** has **7 stages** split into **Execution (1–4)** and **Evaluation (5–7)**.
> - **Gulf of Execution** = user intention ↔ system actions. **Reduce** with affordances, signifiers, mapping, constraints.
> - **Gulf of Evaluation** = system state ↔ user perception. **Reduce** with feedback, visibility, natural mapping.
> - **GOMS** = **G**oals + **O**perators + **M**ethods + **S**election Rules.
> - **KLM Formula** = $T_{execute} = T_k + T_p + T_h + T_d + T_m + T_r$.
> - **KLM Default Times**: $T_k = 0.08$ s, $T_p = 1.10$ s, $T_h = 0.40$ s, $T_m = 1.20$ s (use **$T_k = 0.20$ s** for novice typists if specified).
> - **Abowd & Beale Components**: **User → Input → System → Output → User** (cyclic).
> - **Abowd & Beale Translations**: **Articulation, Performance, Presentation, Observation**.
> - **Key Cross-Mapping**: Gulf of Execution ↔ Articulation; Gulf of Evaluation ↔ Observation.
> - KLM assumes an **error-free**, **expert-level** user — it is *predictive*, not *descriptive*.
> - Norman's model is *qualitative* (describes *why* a system fails); GOMS is *quantitative* (predicts *how long* a task takes).
> - Always show the **operator decomposition table** before substituting into the KLM equation — this guarantees partial marks even if the final number is wrong.
> - The interaction loop is **cyclic**, not linear — feedback from Stage 7 always loops back to Stage 1 of the next cycle.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Terms of Interaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBT753 / PEBRT753 - HUMAN-MACHINE INTERFACE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Terms of Interaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Terms of Interaction"?

In the formal study of **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)** and **Human-Machine Interfaces (HMI)**, the phrase **"Terms of Interaction"** refers to a precise *vocabulary of nine (9) core abstractions* that allow designers, psychologists, and engineers to describe, analyse, and predict what happens when a human uses an interactive system. These terms are the **fundamental primitives** of the interaction model proposed by **Dix, Finlay, Abowd and Beale** (in *Human-Computer Interaction*, Pearson) and are mapped directly onto **Donald Norman's Theory of Action**.

The nine canonical terms are:

1. **Domain**
2. **Goal**
3. **Task**
4. **Intention (Intent)**
5. **Action**
6. **Perception**
7. **Interpretation**
8. **Evaluation**
9. **Feedback**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2):**
> The university expects you to (a) *define each of the nine terms*, (b) *relate them to Norman's Seven Stages of Action*, and (c) *use them to identify the Gulf of Execution and Gulf of Evaluation* in any given interface.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "Ordering a Cappuccino at an Automated Coffee Kiosk"

Imagine you walk up to a touch-screen coffee vending machine.

| Step | You (Human) | The Machine (System) |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | "I want a hot cappuccino." | — |
| **Task** | "Insert money, select drink, take cup." | — |
| **Intention** | "I will press the 'Cappuccino' button." | — |
| **Action** | Your finger presses the screen icon. | Receives the touch input. |
| **Perception** | You see a progress bar. | Outputs the visual state. |
| **Interpretation** | You read the bar as "almost ready." | — |
| **Evaluation** | "Yes, this matches my goal." | — |
| **Feedback** | — | The cup fills with brown liquid. |
| **Domain** | The world of coffee and money. | The world of pumps, valves, coins. |

This single 30-second act exercises **every term of interaction**. The machine works *only* if both worlds overlap properly — that overlap is what HCI engineers call **good interaction design**.

## 1.3 Why these Terms Matter (Engineering View)

> [!NOTE]
> In safety-critical HMI (aviation cockpits, surgical robots, nuclear control rooms, ADAS in cars), a *mismatch* in any one of these nine terms can cause a **catastrophic failure**. For example, a confused *interpretation* of a red indicator light led to the **Air France Flight 447 (2009)** disaster. The KTU 2024 syllabus therefore emphasises these terms as the *first* analytical tool of the HMI engineer.

---

**Core Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):**
> **Terms of Interaction** are the conceptual building blocks — *Domain, Goal, Task, Intention, Action, Perception, Interpretation, Evaluation and Feedback* — that together describe the full *human–system dialogue*, allowing a designer to pinpoint where communication breaks down (the **Gulfs**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Nine Terms — Definitive Expansion

### 2.1.1 Domain
- The **specific area of work or play** for which the system is designed.
- It is the *raw material* on which interaction operates.
- Examples: *banking*, *air-traffic control*, *word-processing*, *CAD drafting*.
- A **domain object** is an item that exists inside the domain (a customer record, a runway, a paragraph).

### 2.1.2 Goal
- The user's **desired end-state**.
- Goals are *what* the user wants to achieve, not *how*.
- Goals are usually stated in the **user's natural language**, not in the system's vocabulary.
- Hierarchy: **Life-goal → Life-style goal → Project goal → Task goal** (from WUGGO / Cooper models).

### 2.1.3 Task
- A **sequence of actions** the user must perform to reach a goal.
- A goal is *abstract*; a task is *concrete*.
- Example: Goal = "send an email"; Task = "click New, type address, type subject, type body, click Send."

### 2.1.4 Intention
- The user's **specific plan** to carry out part of a task.
- *Task-level intention* — what to do.
- *Action-level intention* — how to do it (which widget, which key).
- *Translation* between the two happens in the user's head.

### 2.1.5 Action
- A **physical or cognitive operation** that the user actually performs (key-press, mouse-click, voice command, gesture).
