| 10 | Emotional Response Window | Visceral reaction occurs in $\leq 50$ ms | Pre-attentive perception | Milliseconds (ms) |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility of Experience Design

| Industry | Application of Experience Engineering |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Automotive** | Reducing driver cognitive load in infotainment systems (e.g., Tesla touchscreen design) |
| **Healthcare** | Improving patient compliance via better medical device interfaces (e.g., insulin pumps) |
| **Aviation** | Cockpit displays designed to minimize pilot error (Glass Cockpit philosophy) |
| **Banking** | Secure yet frictionless mobile payment UIs (e.g., Apple Pay confirmation flow) |
| **Industrial Control** | SCADA/HMI dashboards for plant operators — clarity under stress is life-critical |
| **Assistive Tech** | Screen readers, eye-tracking, brain-computer interfaces for disabled users |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In safety-critical HMI domains (aviation, medical, nuclear), **poor experience design has caused fatalities**. The Therac-25 radiation therapy accidents of the 1980s (in part due to interface confusion) and Boeing 737 MAX MCAS issues are textbook cases cited in HMI literature.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Calculating the System Usability Scale (SUS) Score

**Problem Statement:**
A team conducted a usability test of a new mobile banking HMI. Ten users completed the standardized SUS questionnaire. Their raw responses (on the 1–5 Likert scale) are given below. Calculate the SUS score and interpret it.

| Item | Description | U1 | U2 | U3 | U4 | U5 | U6 | U7 | U8 | U9 | U10 |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | I think I would like to use this system frequently. | 4 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 5 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 3 | 5 |
| 2 | I found the system unnecessarily complex. | 2 | 1 | 2 | 1 | 2 | 1 | 3 | 2 | 2 | 1 |
| 3 | I thought the system was easy to use. | 4 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 4 | 3 | 5 |
| 4 | I think I would need technical support. | 1 | 1 | 2 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 4 | 1 | 2 | 1 |
| 5 | I found the functions well integrated. | 4 | 4 | 5 | 3 | 4 | 4 | 3 | 4 | 3 | 5 |
| 6 | I thought there was too much inconsistency. | 2 | 1 | 1 | 2 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 2 | 2 | 1 |
| 7 | I imagine most people would learn quickly. | 4 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 5 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 3 | 5 |
| 8 | I found the system very cumbersome. | 1 | 1 | 2 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 4 | 1 | 2 | 1 |
| 9 | I felt very confident using the system. | 4 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 4 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 3 | 5 |
| 10 | I needed to learn a lot before I could get going. | 2 | 1 | 1 | 2 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 1 | 2 | 1 |

**SUS Scoring Algorithm (Standard):**

For each user:
- **Odd-numbered items (1, 3, 5, 7, 9):** Score contribution = (response − 1)
- **Even-numbered items (2, 4, 6, 8, 10):** Score contribution = (5 − response)
- **Per-user SUS** = sum of contributions × **2.5**
- **Final SUS** = average of per-user scores

**Step-by-Step Derivation — User 1 (U1):**

$$\text{Odd sum} = (4-1) + (4-1) + (4-1) + (4-1) + (4-1) = 3+3+3+3+3 = 15$$

$$\text{Even sum} = (5-2) + (5-1) + (5-2) + (5-1) + (5-2) = 3+4+3+4+3 = 17$$

$$\text{Total} = 15 + 17 = 32$$

$$\text{SUS}_{U1} = 32 \times 2.5 = 80.0$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation — User 2 (U2):**

$$\text{Odd sum} = (5-1) + (5-1) + (4-1) + (5-1) + (5-1) = 4+4+3+4+4 = 19$$

$$\text{Even sum} = (5-1) + (5-1) + (5-1) + (5-1) + (5-1) = 4+4+4+4+4 = 20$$

$$\text{Total} = 19 + 20 = 39$$

$$\text{SUS}_{U2} = 39 \times 2.5 = 97.5$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation — User 7 (U7) — Outlier Check:**

$$\text{Odd sum} = (2-1) + (3-1) + (3-1) + (2-1) + (2-1) = 1+2+2+1+1 = 7$$

$$\text{Even sum} = (5-3) + (5-4) + (5-3) + (5-4) + (5-3) = 2+1+2+1+2 = 8$$

$$\text{Total} = 7 + 8 = 15$$

$$\text{SUS}_{U7} = 15 \times 2.5 = 37.5$$

**Aggregate SUS Score (illustrative subset):**

$$\text{SUS}_{final} = \frac{80.0 + 97.5 + \text{scores of U3..U10}}{10}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Band (Brooke, 1996 — adopted as KTU standard reference):**
> - **SUS $\geq$ 80.3** → Grade A (Excellent)
> - **68 $<$ SUS $<$ 80.3** → Grade B (Good)
> - **68 = Average Benchmark**
> - **51 $<$ SUS $\leq$ 68** → Grade C (OK)
> - **SUS $\leq$ 51** → Grade D or F (Poor)
>
> A SUS score is **not a percentage**, despite ranging 0–100. This is a frequent KTU trick question.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: NPS Calculation and Customer Experience Tier Classification

**Problem Statement:**
A B.Tech project team surveys **200 users** of a smart-home HMI app. They ask: *"On a scale of 0–10, how likely are you to recommend this product?"*
- **Promoters (9–10):** 110 users
- **Passives (7–8):** 60 users
- **Detractors (0–6):** 30 users

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$\%\text{Promoters} = \frac{110}{200} \times 100 = 55\%$$

$$\%\text{Passives} = \frac{60}{200} \times 100 = 30\%$$

$$\%\text{Detractors} = \frac{30}{200} \times 100 = 15\%$$

$$\text{NPS} = \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors} = 55 - 15 = +40$$

**Interpretation (Industry Standard):**
- NPS $> +50$ → World-class (Apple, Tesla territory)
- $+30 \leq$ NPS $\leq +50$ → **Great** (this case: **+40**)
- $0 \leq$ NPS $< +30$ → Good
- $-30 \leq$ NPS $< 0$ → Needs improvement
- NPS $< -30$ → Critical

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always specify the **denominator** in NPS calculations. A common student mistake is to forget that *passives* are explicitly excluded from the subtraction.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Task Completion Rate and Time-on-Task Analysis

**Problem Statement:**
A team evaluated the checkout flow of an e-commerce HMI. **15 users** attempted the task.
- **11 users** completed it successfully.
- **Mean time on task** for successful users = **42 seconds**.
- **Standard deviation** = **8 seconds**.
- **4 users** failed; their mean time = **120 seconds**.

**Derivation — Task Completion Rate:**

$$\text{TCR} = \frac{N_{success}}{N_{total}} \times 100\% = \frac{11}{15} \times 100\% \approx 73.3\%$$

**Derivation — Mean Total Task Time (all users):**

$$\bar{T} = \frac{(11 \times 42) + (4 \times 120)}{15} = \frac{462 + 480}{15} = \frac{942}{15} = 62.8 \text{ s}$$

**Derivation — 95% Confidence Interval (using z = 1.96 for $n=15$):**

$$CI_{95\%} = \bar{T} \pm 1.96 \cdot \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$$

Using pooled variance approximated as $s \approx 30$ s (combined standard deviation across successful and failed users):

$$CI_{95\%} = 62.8 \pm 1.96 \cdot \frac{30}{\sqrt{15}} = 62.8 \pm 1.96 \cdot 7.746$$

$$CI_{95\%} = 62.8 \pm 15.18$$

$$\Rightarrow CI_{95\%} = [47.62, 77.98] \text{ seconds}$$

**Engineering Decision:** A TCR of 73.3% **falls below the 90% industry benchmark** for a critical checkout flow. The team should redesign the flow.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Norman, Hassenzahl, Morville, and Metrics

The following analytical matrix maps the three major experience theories to the corresponding engineering metrics. This is a high-value KTU synthesis point.

| Theory / Framework | Key Construct | Corresponding Measurable Metric | KTU Exam Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Norman Visceral | First-impression aesthetics | Time-to-first-impression (TTFI), A/B visual test | Define visceral design with example |
| Norman Behavioral | Pleasure & effectiveness of use | SUS, Task Completion Rate, Time-on-Task | Link to classical usability |
| Norman Reflective | Self-image & brand memory | Brand recall, NPS, qualitative interviews | Discuss long-term loyalty |
| Hassenzahl PQ | Task-goal achievement | Efficiency, error rate, learnability | Quantify pragmatic quality |
| Hassenzahl HQ | Identity & stimulation | UEQ-HQ subscale, qualitative diary studies | Quantify hedonic quality |
| Morville Useful | Solves a real need | Adoption rate, market share | Pre-launch validation |
| Morville Usable | Easy to use | SUS, TCR | Post-launch testing |
| Morville Findable | Easy to navigate | Search success rate, time-to-info | Information architecture |
| Morville Credible | Trustworthy | Trust questionnaires, NPS | Security and privacy |
| Morville Desirable | Appealing | Aesthetic appeal score, conversion rate | Brand language |
| Morville Accessible | Inclusive design | WCAG conformance, screen reader tests | Assistive tech |
| Morville Valuable | Business ROI | Conversion, LTV, churn | Executive reporting |

### 3.5 Comparative Derivation — UX vs. Usability Boundary Condition

A frequently asked KTU question is: *"Where does usability end and UX begin?"*

**Derivation of the Boundary Condition:**

Usability, per ISO 9241-11, is bounded by three explicit dimensions:

$$U = f(\text{Effectiveness}, \text{Efficiency}, \text{Satisfaction}) \quad \text{with context of use } C$$

UX, per ISO 9241-210, has a much broader scope:

$$UX = f(U, \text{Emotion}, \text{Brand}, \text{Value}, \text{Trust}, \text{Aesthetics}, C, U_{user})$$

**Boundary Derivation:**

$$\text{UX} \supset \text{Usability}$$

That is, usability is a **strict subset** of UX. In set notation:

$$U \subsetneq \text{UX}$$

The difference set is:

$$\text{UX} \setminus U = \{\text{Emotion}, \text{Brand}, \text{Value}, \text{Trust}, \text{Aesthetics}, \text{Meaning}\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam-Worthy Statement:** *"Usability asks: 'Can the user complete the task?' UX asks: 'Did the user feel good, valued, and willing to return?'"*

### 3.6 Emotional Response Time Derivation (Visceral Layer)

The visceral response (Norman) is governed by the speed of the human visual and affective processing system. Empirical data from neuroaesthetics (Chatterjee, 2014) shows:

$$t_{visceral} \approx 50 \text{ ms (visual cortex processing)} \rightarrow 300 \text{ ms (conscious affective response)}$$

Therefore, the **engineering implication** is:

$$T_{render\_first\_frame} \ll 300 \text{ ms}$$

Any HMI whose first frame takes longer than **300 ms** loses the visceral advantage. This is why modern UX guidelines (Google Material Design, Apple HIG) mandate:

- **First Contentful Paint (FCP) $<$ 1.0 s**
- **Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) $<$ 2.5 s**
- **Time to Interactive (TTI) $<$ 3.8 s**

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions (with Model Answers)

**Q1.** *Define User Experience (UX) as per ISO 9241-210. List any **four** components that constitute a good UX.* `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
UX is a person's perceptions and responses resulting from the use and/or anticipated use of a product, system, or service (ISO 9241-210:2019). It is subjective, context-dependent, and encompasses the entire interaction lifecycle.
Four components of a good UX:
1. **Usability** – Effectiveness, efficiency, and satisfaction in use.
2. **Usefulness** – The system solves a real user problem.
3. **Desirability** – Aesthetic and emotional appeal.
4. **Credibility** – User trust in the system.
*(Other valid components: Findability, Accessibility, Value — based on Morville's Honeycomb.)*

**Q2.** *Differentiate between **Usability** and **User Experience (UX)**. Mention one standard metric for each.* `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
| Aspect | Usability | User Experience |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Scope | Task-level performance | Whole-product holistic perception |
| Definition (ISO) | ISO 9241-11 | ISO 9241-210 |
| Focus | Effectiveness, efficiency, satisfaction | Emotion, meaning, value, identity |
| Metric Example | Task Completion Rate (TCR) | System Usability Scale (SUS) or NPS |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Pitfall #1:** Many students write *"UX = Usability + Aesthetics"* and lose 1 mark. While a *partial* truth, it oversimplifies. UX is a **broader, multi-dimensional construct** that includes emotion, brand, value, and meaning — *not just aesthetics added to usability*.

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

> **Note:** Two alternative question choices are provided. Either may be asked in the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE). Both questions carry 14 marks with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** Deep Dive into Emotional Design and UX Frameworks

**(a)** Explain in detail **Don Norman's three levels of emotional design** with suitable examples. Discuss how each level influences the engineering of a Human-Machine Interface. *(7 marks)* `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Donald Norman, in his influential 2004 work *Emotional Design: Why We Love (or Hate) Everyday Things*, argued that **attractive things work better**. He proposed a tri-level model of human emotional response to designed objects:

**1. Visceral Design (Pre-conscious, Sensory Layer)**
- This is the **immediate, pre-attentive** reaction to a system's appearance, sound, and feel.
- Driven by the **reptilian brain** — fast, automatic, and emotional.
- Decisions made here occur within **50–300 ms** of stimulus exposure.
- **Engineering Implication:** Designers must invest in visual aesthetics, micro-animations, sound design, and haptic feedback. Example: the *click* sound of an iPhone keyboard — purely visceral, enhances perceived quality without any functional benefit.

**2. Behavioral Design (Limbic + Motor Layer)**
- The **pleasure and effectiveness of use**. This is the level of classical **usability**.
- Four pillars: function, understandability, usability, physical feel.
- The user feels **competence and mastery** when the interface behaves predictably.
- **Engineering Implication:** Apply Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics, minimize Fitts's Law pointing times, ensure consistent interaction patterns. Example: a microwave door that opens smoothly with one hand — behavioral pleasure through good physical design.

**3. Reflective Design (Neocortex, Cognitive Layer)**
- Involves **self-image, brand, status, and memory**.
- This level takes time to develop but **outlasts the product lifecycle**.
- Users form **loyalty, advocacy, and identity bonds** at this level.
- **Engineering Implication:** Storytelling, brand language, premium materials, and meaningful interaction rituals. Example: the unboxing experience of an Apple product — designed to maximize reflective impact.

**Summary Table:**

| Level | Timeframe | Brain Region | HMI Engineering Focus |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Visceral | 0–300 ms | Reptilian | Aesthetics, sound, haptics |
| Behavioral | Minutes to hours | Limbic + motor | Usability, learnability |
| Reflective | Days to years | Neocortex | Brand, identity, memory |

> **Conclusion for KTU answer:** A successful HMI must be engineered to address **all three levels simultaneously** because users are whole beings, not just task-executors.

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain **Peter Morville's User Experience Honeycomb**. How does it extend Norman's model? *(7 marks)* `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Peter Morville's UX Honeycomb (2004)** is a framework that defines **seven facets** of User Experience. All seven must be present in a high-quality HMI — the framework is conjunctive, not disjunctive.

**The Seven Facets:**

1. **Useful** – Does the system deliver a value proposition? *Origin: market research, user interviews.*
2. **Usable** – Can users accomplish their goals easily? *Origin: usability testing, SUS.*
3. **Findable** – Can users locate what they need quickly? *Origin: information architecture.*
4. **Credible** – Do users trust the system? *Origin: transparency, security cues, testimonials.*
5. **Desirable** – Is the design emotionally appealing? *Origin: visual design, brand language.*
6. **Accessible** – Can users with disabilities use it? *Origin: WCAG, screen reader support.*
7. **Valuable** – Does it deliver business value? *Origin: ROI, conversion analytics.*

**Diagram (ASCII Representation for KTU Exam Sheet):**

```
                User Experience (UX)
                       |
        +---------+---------+--------+--------+
        |         |         |        |        |
     Useful   Usable  Findable Credible Desirable
        |         |         |        |        |
     [Need]  [SUS/TCR]  [IA]  [Trust]  [Style]
                       |
                  +----+----+
                  |         |
              Accessible  Valuable
              [WCAG]      [ROI]
```

**How it extends Norman's Model:**
- Norman's model is **psychological** (about *user cognition*).
- Morville's model is **engineering and managerial** (about *what to design* and *what to measure*).
- Morville's "Usable" aligns with Norman's **Behavioral** level.
- Morville's "Desirable" aligns with Norman's **Visceral** level.
- Morville's "Credible" and "Valuable" add **trust and business value** dimensions that Norman did not explicitly address.
- Morville's "Accessible" and "Findable" add **inclusive design** dimensions missing in Norman.

> **Synthesis for KTU answer:** Morville provides the **engineering checklist** that operationalizes Norman's **psychological theory**. Both frameworks are complementary, not competing.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** Quantitative Experience Evaluation and CX vs. UX Distinction

**(a)** Define **System Usability Scale (SUS)**. A team tests a new HMI with 10 users. The odd-item sum of a sample user is 18 and the even-item sum is 17. Calculate and interpret the SUS score for this user. *(7 marks)* `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of SUS:**
The **System Usability Scale (SUS)** is a **10-item, 5-point Likert scale questionnaire** developed by John Brooke in 1986. It provides a **quick and reliable** measure of perceived usability. Each item is rated 1 (Strongly Disagree) to 5 (Strongly Agree). The total SUS score ranges from **0 to 100**, where *higher is better*.

**SUS Scoring Algorithm:**

$$\text{SUS}_{user} = \left[\sum_{i=1}^{5}(\text{Item}_{2i-1} - 1) + \sum_{i=1}^{5}(5 - \text{Item}_{2i})\right] \times 2.5$$

**Given Data for the User:**
- Sum of contributions from odd items (1, 3, 5, 7, 9) = **18**
- Sum of contributions from even items (2, 4, 6, 8, 10) = **17**

**Calculation:**

$$\text{Total raw sum} = 18 + 17 = 35$$

$$\text{SUS}_{user} = 35 \times 2.5 = 87.5$$

**Interpretation (Brooke, 1996 Bands):**
A SUS score of **87.5** falls in the **Grade A / "Excellent"** band.
- **SUS $\geq$ 80.3** → Grade A (Excellent) ✓ (this case)
- 68 $<$ SUS $<$ 80.3 → Grade B (Good)
- **68 = Industry Average Benchmark**
- 51 $<$ SUS $\leq$ 68 → Grade C (OK)
- SUS $\leq$ 51 → Grade D/F (Poor)

> **Conclusion:** The HMI has *excellent* perceived usability. The team may proceed to next-stage evaluation (e.g., UEQ for emotional dimensions and NPS for loyalty).

**(b)** Distinguish between **Customer Experience (CX)** and **User Experience (UX)**. Explain with examples why an HMI can have a high UX score but a low CX score. *(7 marks)* `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definitions:**
- **User Experience (UX)** is the perception and response of a person resulting from the use of a *specific product, interface, or system* (ISO 9241-210).
- **Customer Experience (CX)** is the **broader, omnichannel** perception a customer has of a *brand or organization* across *all touchpoints and over the entire customer lifecycle*.

**Comparative Table:**

| Dimension | UX | CX |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Scope | Single product/interface | All touchpoints (web, app, store, support) |
| Time | Single session | Months/years (lifetime) |
| Owner | Product/UX team | Marketing/CX team |
| Measurement | SUS, UEQ, task success | NPS, CSAT, retention, CLV |
| Touchpoints | 1–3 (in a product) | Many (10–30+) |
| Discipline | HCI, ergonomics | Service design, brand strategy |

**Why High UX ≠ High CX — Real-World Example: A Smart-Watch Manufacturer**

Consider a hypothetical smartwatch company:
- **UX Score = 88/100 (Excellent)** — The watch app is beautiful, easy to navigate, and battery-efficient.
- **CX Score = 30/100 (Poor)** — Because:
  - The **packaging** is non-recyclable and frustratingly hard to open.
  - The **onboarding call** to activate the SIM is 47 minutes long.
  - The **billing portal** crashes on Chrome but works only on Safari.
  - The **return process** requires a faxed form (anachronistic).
  - The **support chatbot** is non-responsive after 6 PM.
  - The **warranty** is denied for a 2-day delay in registration.

The user *loves the product* (great UX) but *hates being a customer* (poor CX). This is a classic HMI engineering failure: the product team succeeded while the CX team failed. A complete experience engineering strategy must address **both**.

> **Conclusion for KTU answer:** UX is a *necessary but not sufficient* condition for CX. Engineering a delightful interface is meaningless if the surrounding brand and service touchpoints frustrate the customer. The two disciplines must be **integrated** in the organizational design process.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Pitfall #2:** When answering "CX vs. UX," students often confuse **CX with Customer Service**. Customer Service is *one touchpoint*; CX is the *aggregate* of all touchpoints including marketing, sales, support, billing, and even word-of-mouth. Examiners *specifically* deduct marks for this conflation.

---

### 5.3 Part B — Bonus: KTU 14-Mark Critical Thinking Question (For Higher Marks)

**Q3.** *"A self-driving car's touchscreen HMI scored SUS = 78 and NPS = −5. The marketing team calls it a 'great product.' The safety team flags it for two design flaws: (1) the emergency stop button is on the third sub-menu, and (2) the latency between user input and screen response is 600 ms during high GPU load. Using Hassenzahl's model and Don Norman's three levels, diagnose the experience failure."* *(14 marks)* `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 – Model Paper]`

**Model Answer Outline (For Examiner Reference):**

- **Pragmatic Quality (PQ) Failure:** The 600 ms latency and buried emergency stop violate **Behavioral Design** (Norman) — the interface is functionally broken. A 600 ms delay exceeds the visceral response window (300 ms) and falls in the **reflective loss zone** where users lose trust.
- **Hedonic Quality (HQ) Failure:** The −5 NPS indicates users *don't feel good* using the product. The discrepancy between SUS (78, "Good") and NPS (−5, "Poor") reveals that **users can use it without loving it** — the HQ subscale of UEQ would likely show very low stimulation/identity scores.
- **Engineering Recommendations:**
  1. Move emergency stop to first-level menu (Pragmatic fix).
  2. Optimize GPU load to achieve $<$ 100 ms input-to-render latency.
  3. Apply progressive disclosure to reduce cognitive load.
  4. Conduct contextual inquiry with distracted-driving scenarios.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 2 — "Experience" — High-Density Revision Notes**

- ✅ **UX Definition:** ISO 9241-210 — *"Person's perceptions and responses from use and/or anticipated use."*
- ✅ **Three temporal dimensions:** Anticipated use, moment of use, after-use reflection.
- ✅ **UX ≠ Usability:** Usability (ISO 9241-11) is a strict subset of UX. UX adds emotion, brand, value, trust, aesthetics, meaning.
- ✅ **Norman 3 Levels:** Visceral (sensory, $\leq$300 ms) → Behavioral (usability, function) → Reflective (identity, memory, brand).
- ✅ **Hassenzahl's Model:** UX = $f(\text{PQ}, \text{HQ}, \text{User}, \text{Context})$. PQ = do-goals; HQ = be-goals.
- ✅ **Morville Honeycomb:** 7 facets — Useful, Usable, Findable, Credible, Desirable, Accessible, Valuable. **All 7 are required.**
- ✅ **SUS:** 10-item, 5-point Likert. Score range 0–100. **68 = average.** $\geq$ 80.3 = Grade A. **NOT a percentage.**
- ✅ **SUS Formula:** $\text{SUS} = (\text{Odd}_{sum} + \text{Even}_{sum}) \times 2.5$. Odd items: (R−1). Even items: (5−R).
- ✅ **NPS:** %Promoters − %Detractors. Passives excluded. Range −100 to +100. $>+30$ = good.
- ✅ **Task Completion Rate:** $N_{success} / N_{total}$. **Target $\geq$ 90%** for critical tasks.
- ✅ **UX vs. CX:** UX = product-centric, single interface. CX = brand-centric, omnichannel, lifecycle-wide.
- ✅ **Visceral response time:** $\leq$ 50 ms (pre-attentive) to 300 ms (affective). First frame should render in $<$ 300 ms.
- ✅ **Iterative design:** Experience design is *cyclical*, not waterfall. Prototypes are tested cyclically.
- ✅ **Industry-standard metrics to memorize:** SUS, NPS, CSAT, TCR, UEQ, Time-on-Task, Error Rate.
- ✅ **Real-world safety case:** Therac-25 (medical HMI accidents) and Boeing 737 MAX (MCAS HMI confusion) — both caused by poor experience design in safety-critical systems.
- ✅ **Cross-link to KTU COs:** This topic maps to **CO2** (Apply HMI design principles), **CO3** (Evaluate usability and UX), and **CO5** (Appreciate user-centered design philosophy).
- ✅ **Examiner's favourite trick questions:**
  1. *"Is SUS a percentage?"* → **No, despite 0–100 range.**
  2. *"Can a usable system have poor UX?"* → **Yes. PQ + HQ both needed.**
  3. *"Is visceral or reflective design more important?"* → **All three are necessary; trade-offs differ by context.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Preparation Tip:** When answering any 14-mark question on "Experience," always structure your response in **three layers**:
> 1. **Theoretical foundation** (cite ISO, Norman, Hassenzahl, or Morville).
> 2. **Applied engineering mapping** (show how the theory translates to design decisions or metrics).
> 3. **Real-world example** (Therac-25, Apple, Google, self-driving cars, etc.).
>
> Examiners in the KTU 2024 scheme award **1–2 marks specifically for examples** that demonstrate applied understanding — do not omit them.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Engagement and Fun
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT753 - HUMAN-MACHINE INTERFACE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Engagement and Fun

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engagement** in Human-Machine Interface (HMI) is formally defined as the *qualitative state of a user characterised by active cognitive, emotional, and behavioural investment in a human-machine interaction session, marked by sustained attention, focused immersion, and a perceived sense of meaningful control over the system.*

In the KTU 2024 HMI syllabus framework, **engagement** is treated as a multi-dimensional construct that bridges classical **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)** metrics (usability, efficiency) with **User Experience (UX)** emotional metrics (delight, satisfaction, motivation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Fun** is the hedonic, pleasure-driven emotional response that emerges when a user interacts with a system that satisfies intrinsic motivational needs (curiosity, mastery, autonomy) and reduces friction. It is the *affective outcome* that converts a usable system into a *memorable* one.

The two concepts are interlinked but distinct:
- **Engagement** = *Process* (the user's persistent involvement over time).
- **Fun** = *Affective Peak* (an emotional state of playful enjoyment that fuels engagement).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Coffee Shop vs. The Arcade:**
> Imagine walking into two places. **Place A** is a clean, efficient self-service coffee kiosk — you press a button, get your latte, leave. It is *usable* but unmemorable. **Place B** is a retro arcade cafe — same coffee, but with neon lights, a leaderboard of high scorers, a "barista of the month" wall, and a small free sticker on every cup. You stay 90 minutes longer.
> 
> - The **coffee** is the *task* (utility).
> - The **neon lights and stickers** are the *engagement hooks*.
> - The **joy of beating the leaderboard** is *fun*.
> 
> An HMI designer who masters **engagement and fun** is the architect of *Place B* — adding emotional layers atop a functionally correct system.

## 1.3 Foundational Distinctions

| Term | Definition | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Hedonic Quality** | The pleasure/fun a system provides | Playing *Angry Birds* |
| **Utilitarian Quality** | The practical task-efficiency of a system | Filing income tax online |
| **Flow** | The optimal engagement state (Csikszentmihalyi) | Losing track of time while coding |
| **Intrinsic Motivation** | Doing something for inherent satisfaction | Playing music for joy |
| **Extrinsic Motivation** | Doing something for a reward | Working for salary |
| **Gamification** | Applying game-elements to non-game contexts | Step counters on smartwatches |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The course outcome mapped to this topic is **CO2 — Design engaging and emotionally resonant interfaces**, assessed under Bloom's levels *Understand (L2)*, *Apply (L3)*, and *Analyse (L4)*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillars of Engagement in HMI

Engagement in HMI rests on **four interlocking pillars** as described by O'Brien & Toms (2008), the de-facto reference model adopted in KTU 2024 HMI pedagogy:

1. **Attention (Focus)**
   - The user's perceptual and cognitive resources are directed toward the interface.
   - *Design lever:* visual hierarchy, motion, novelty, contrast.

2. **Interest (Curiosity)**
   - The user is intrinsically motivated to explore.
   - *Design lever:* progressive disclosure, micro-rewards, mystery boxes.

3. **Affect (Emotion)**
   - The interaction produces positive emotional states.
   - *Design lever:* micro-interactions, personality, surprise, delight.

4. **Temporal Investment (Time-on-Task)**
   - The user willingly extends the session duration.
   - *Design lever:* save states, streaks, narrative arcs.

## 2.2 Flow Theory (Csikszentmihalyi) — The Core Engagement Engine

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flow** is the *psychological state of complete absorption* in an activity, first formalised by psychologist **Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi (1975/1990)**. It is the *gold standard* of intrinsic engagement in HMI design.

The state arises at the precise intersection of three variables:

1. **Challenge Level** $\rightarrow$ difficulty of the task as perceived by the user.
2. **Skill Level** $\rightarrow$ the user's competence with the system.
3. **Feedback Loop** $\rightarrow$ clarity of the system's response.

### 2.2.1 The Flow Channel (4-Quadrant Model)

| Quadrant | Skill | Challenge | User State |
|---|---|---|---|
| I | Low | Low | **Apathy / Boredom** |
| II | High | Low | **Relaxation** |
| III | High | High | **FLOW ★ (Optimal Engagement)** |
| IV | Low | High | **Anxiety / Frustration** |

> [!NOTE]
> A well-designed HMI continuously shifts the user *upward and rightward* in this grid — by **scaffolding skills** (tutorials, hints) and **scaling challenges** (new levels, harder puzzles).

## 2.3 The Gamification Framework (Werbach & Hunter)

**Gamification** = the application of *game-design elements* and *game principles* in *non-game contexts*.

The three-tier taxonomy:

$$
\text{Gamification} = \underbrace{\text{Dynamics}}_{\text{big picture}} + \underbrace{\text{Mechanics}}_{\text{processes}} + \underbrace{\text{Components}}_{\text{concrete elements}}
$$

- **Dynamics (10):** Constraints, Emotions, Narrative, Progression, Relationships, etc.
- **Mechanics (15):** Challenges, Chance, Competition, Cooperation, Feedback, Resource Acquisition, Rewards, Transactions, Turns, etc.
- **Components (≈40):** Achievements, Avatars, Badges, Boss Fights, Collections, Combat, Content Unlocking, Gifting, Leaderboards, Levels, Points, Quests, Teams, Virtual Goods, etc.

## 2.4 Hedonic Motivation System (HMD Model — Hassenzahl)

The **Hedonic Motivation Model** by Marc Hassenzahl (and the HMD model with colleagues) describes *why* users find interfaces fun:

$$
\text{Appraisal} = f(\text{Novelty}, \text{Interest}, \text{Conveyed Meaning})
$$

- **Novelty:** Surprising, unfamiliar interactions.
- **Interest:** Stimulating, varied content.
- **Conveyed Meaning:** Signalling identity, status, belonging.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Domain | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Flow Condition | $\text{Skill} \approx \text{Challenge} \pm \epsilon$ | Dimensionless ratio | Detect flow state |
| 2 | Engagement Score (Norman) | $E = w_1 A + w_2 I + w_3 Af + w_4 T$ | Score 0–10 | UX audit |
| 3 | Fun / Hedonic Rating | $F = \alpha \cdot N + \beta \cdot In + \gamma \cdot M$ | 0–10 Likert | Self-report scale |
| 4 | Time-on-Task Ratio | $\tau = \dfrac{t_{\text{actual}}}{t_{\text{expected}}}$ | Dimensionless | Engagement metric |
| 5 | Retention Rate | $R = \dfrac{U_{\text{retained}}}{U_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Long-term engagement |
| 6 | Stickiness Index | $S = \dfrac{\text{DAU}}{\text{MAU}}$ | Ratio 0–1 | Daily-active / Monthly-active users |
| 7 | Octalysis Epic Score | $ES = \sum_{i=1}^{8} w_i \cdot c_i$ | Weighted score | Gamification audit |
| 8 | NPS Proxy for Fun | $\text{NPS} = \%P - \%D$ | -100 to +100 | Affective satisfaction |
| 9 | Boredom Threshold | $B_{th} = \dfrac{\text{Skill}}{\text{Challenge}} > 1.2$ | Ratio | Trigger difficulty boost |
| 10 | Frustration Threshold | $F_{th} = \dfrac{\text{Challenge}}{\text{Skill}} > 1.5$ | Ratio | Trigger help/tutorial |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, $E$ denotes engagement, $A$ attention, $I$ interest, $Af$ affect, $T$ temporal investment, $N$ novelty, $In$ interest (input), $M$ meaning, $w_i$ weights, $c_i$ core drives (Octalysis), $\alpha,\beta,\gamma$ weighting coefficients for fun dimensions, $\epsilon$ a small tolerance, and $U$ users.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Consumer Apps:** Instagram uses variable-ratio rewards (likes) — Skinner-box gamification.
- **Productivity Software:** Duolingo streaks create daily engagement loops.
- **Automotive HMIs:** Tesla's "Elon-mode" Easter eggs (light show) inject fun.
- **Medical Devices:** Rehabilitation robots use gamified exercises to boost therapy adherence by **30–60%** in published clinical studies.
- **Aviation Cockpits:** Modern glass cockpits embed fun elements (customisable HUD colours) without compromising safety-critical usability.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Deriving the Engagement Score ($E$)

The engagement score is a weighted linear combination of the four O'Brien & Toms pillars. We derive it from first principles using the **linear-additive multi-attribute utility principle**.

### Step 1: Identify the constituents

Let the four measurable engagement attributes be:
- $A$ = Attention score (0–10), measured via eye-tracking fixation.
- $I$ = Interest score (0–10), measured via self-report Likert.
- $Af$ = Affect score (0–10), measured via PANAS-style affect scale.
- $T$ = Temporal investment score (0–10), measured via time-on-task ratio normalised.

### Step 2: Define the engagement utility function

In multi-attribute utility theory, if the attributes are **mutually preference-independent**, the global utility $E$ is the weighted sum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(a_1, a_2, a_3, a_4) &= \sum_{i=1}^{4} w_i \cdot a_i \\
&= w_1 A + w_2 I + w_3 Af + w_4 T
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Apply the normalisation constraint

To keep $E$ in the range $[0, 10]$, the weights must sum to 1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 &= 1 \\
\text{with } \quad w_i &> 0 \;\; \forall i
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** An HMI designer evaluates a meditation app with the following observations:
> - Attention $A = 7$
> - Interest $I = 8$
> - Affect $Af = 9$
> - Temporal investment $T = 6$
> 
> Using equal weights ($w_i = 0.25$), compute the engagement score.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= w_1 A + w_2 I + w_3 Af + w_4 T \\
  &= (0.25)(7) + (0.25)(8) + (0.25)(9) + (0.25)(6) \\
  &= 1.75 + 2.00 + 2.25 + 1.50 \\
  &= 7.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $E = 7.50/10$ indicates *high engagement*; the app is above the typical KTU acceptance threshold of **6.0/10** for consumer-facing HMIs.

## 3.2 Deriving the Flow Equilibrium Condition

We model the user's psychological arousal as a function of challenge and skill. Let $A(\cdot)$ be arousal. The flow condition is the **psychological equilibrium point** where arousal is neither too low (boredom) nor too high (anxiety).

### Step 1: Define arousal as a quadratic function of the imbalance

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Imbalance: } \quad \Delta &= C - S \\
\text{Arousal: } \quad A(\Delta) &= -\Delta^2 + k
\end{aligned}
$$

where $C$ = challenge, $S$ = skill, and $k$ = a personal constant (max arousal capacity).

### Step 2: Find the maximum arousal (flow peak)

Take the derivative and set to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dA}{d\Delta} &= -2\Delta = 0 \\
\Rightarrow \Delta &= 0 \\
\Rightarrow C - S &= 0 \\
\Rightarrow C &= S
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **flow condition**: challenge must equal skill.

### Step 3: Verify with second derivative (concavity test)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d^2 A}{d\Delta^2} &= -2 < 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the second derivative is negative, the function is concave-down, confirming a **maximum** at $C = S$. This is the *flow peak*.

> [!NOTE]
> In a real HMI, designers aim to keep $\vert C - S \vert \leq \epsilon$ (a small tolerance, e.g., $\epsilon = 0.1$), so the user hovers in a flow band, not a flow point.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Design Levers for Engagement vs. Fun

| Design Lever | Engagement Effect | Fun Effect | HMI Example | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Streaks** | High (retention) | Medium | Duolingo | Stress on missed days |
| **Leaderboards** | Medium (competition) | High (status) | Strava | Demotivates low-skill users |
| **Micro-animations** | High (delight) | High | Apple's iOS buttons | Performance cost |
| **Progress Bars** | High (closure) | Medium | LinkedIn profile | Visible inadequacy |
| **Easter Eggs** | Medium | Very High | Google "do a barrel roll" | Discoverability low |
| **Sound Design** | High (feedback) | High | Twitter's "tweet" chime | Annoyance risk |
| **Customisation** | Medium (ownership) | High (identity) | Spotify Wrapped | Choice paralysis |
| **Narrative Arcs** | Very High | Very High | Headspace "Basics" | Time investment |
| **Surprise Rewards** | High | Very High | Snapchat streaks | Addictive loops |

## 3.4 Algorithm for Adaptive Difficulty (Flow Maintenance)

A real HMI can use this pseudo-rule to keep the user in flow:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{If } \frac{C}{S} > 1.5 \rightarrow \text{trigger tutorial / hint / reduce challenge} \\
&\text{If } \frac{C}{S} < 0.8 \rightarrow \text{introduce new challenge / new level} \\
&\text{If } 0.8 \leq \frac{C}{S} \leq 1.5 \rightarrow \text{maintain current state (FLOW)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This simple threshold-based controller is the conceptual basis of **Dynamic Difficulty Adjustment (DDA)** engines used in modern games and gamified productivity tools.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — The Engagement-Fun Interaction Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1([User Approaches Interface]) --> node2{Novel Stimulus Detected?}
    node2 -- Yes --> node3[Curiosity Activated]
    node2 -- No --> node4[Boredom / Disengagement]
    node3 --> node5[Exploration Begins]
    node5 --> node6[Micro-Reward Delivered]
    node6 --> node7{Challenge vs Skill Balanced?}
    node7 -- Yes --> node8[Flow State Achieved]
    node7 -- No --> node9[Anxiety or Boredom]
    node8 --> node10[Fun / Affect Peak]
    node10 --> node11[Behavioural Investment]
    node11 --> node12[Engagement Locked In]
    node12 --> node13[Session Extended]
    node13 --> node14{Novelty Decays?}
    node14 -- Yes --> node2
    node14 -- No --> node12
    node9 --> node15[System Adjusts Difficulty]
    node15 --> node7
    node4 --> node16[User Leaves System]
    style node8 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style node10 fill:#FF69B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style node12 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The golden node is *flow*, the pink node is *fun*, and the green node is *engagement lock-in*. The dashed logic shows that boredom forces a *re-engagement attempt* through novelty, while anxiety/boredom triggers the system's adaptive difficulty controller.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Csikszentmihalyi Flow Channel (4-Quadrant)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Q1["Q1: APATHY"]
        q1n["Low Skill + Low Challenge"]
    end
    subgraph Q2["Q2: RELAXATION"]
        q2n["High Skill + Low Challenge"]
    end
    subgraph Q3["Q3: FLOW ★"]
        q3n["High Skill + High Challenge"]
    end
    subgraph Q4["Q4: ANXIETY"]
        q4n["Low Skill + High Challenge"]
    end
    Q1 -->|Increase Challenge| Q4
    Q1 -->|Increase Skill| Q2
    Q2 -->|Increase Challenge| Q3
    Q4 -->|Increase Skill| Q3
    Q3 -->|Skill Grows Faster| Q2
    Q3 -->|Challenge Grows Faster| Q4
    style Q3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Gamification Pyramid (Werbach & Hunter)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    top["DYNAMICS - Big Picture Goals"] --> mid["MECHANICS - Engagement Processes"] --> base["COMPONENTS - Concrete Elements"]
    topDesc["Constraints, Emotions, Narrative, Progression, Relationships"] -.-> top
    midDesc["Challenges, Chance, Competition, Cooperation, Feedback, Rewards"] -.-> mid
    baseDesc["Points, Badges, Levels, Leaderboards, Quests, Avatars, Virtual Goods"] -.-> base
    style top fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style mid fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style base fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Engaging HMI

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input[Input Layer: Touch, Voice, Gesture] --> perception[Perception Module: Filters, Attention Capture]
    perception --> cognition[Cognition Module: Mental Model, Memory]
    cognition --> motivation[Motivation Engine: Intrinsic + Extrinsic Drivers]
    motivation --> action[Action Loop: User Performs Task]
    action --> feedback[Feedback System: Visual, Audio, Haptic]
    feedback --> reward[Reward Module: Points, Streaks, Praise]
    reward --> affect[Affect Engine: Fun, Delight, Surprise]
    affect --> engagement[Engagement State: Attention + Interest + Affect + Time]
    engagement --> memory[Memory: Form Lasting Imprint]
    memory --> retention[Retention: User Returns]
    retention --> perception
    style reward fill:#FFA500
    style affect fill:#FF69B4
    style engagement fill:#90EE90
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the architecture:** The system forms a **closed feedback loop**. Notice how the *Affect Engine* is a distinct sub-system from *Feedback* — this is the engineering separation between *informational* and *emotional* channels in modern UX engines.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**  
> **Define engagement in the context of HMI. List any two design techniques used to enhance user engagement.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Engagement** in HMI is the qualitative state in which a user becomes cognitively, emotionally, and behaviourally invested in a human-machine interaction, marked by focused attention, intrinsic interest, positive affect, and a willingness to extend session duration.
> 
> It is operationalised through the four pillars of the **O'Brien & Toms model**: Attention, Interest, Affect, and Temporal Investment.
> 
> **Two design techniques:**
> 1. **Micro-interactions and micro-animations** — small, delightful animations (e.g., the heart "burst" when liking an Instagram post) that convert a routine action into an emotionally rewarding moment.
> 2. **Progressive disclosure and streaks** — revealing information or challenges gradually to maintain a *flow-equilibrium* between challenge and skill.
> 
> *(Any other valid techniques like gamification, narrative arcs, customisation are acceptable.)*

**(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for the two techniques with brief justification)**

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**  
> **What is the concept of 'flow' as proposed by Csikszentmihalyi? Explain its relevance in HMI design.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Flow** is a psychological state of complete, focused immersion in an activity, first described by **Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi (1975)**. It occurs when the perceived **challenge** of a task is balanced against the user's **skill** level, with clear **feedback** closing the loop.
> 
> **Relevance in HMI Design:**
> - HMIs that sustain a state of flow (e.g., *Angry Birds*, *VS Code*) achieve high engagement and long session times.
> - Designers must continuously balance challenge and skill; if challenge exceeds skill, the user feels **anxiety**; if skill exceeds challenge, the user feels **boredom**.
> - Flow is operationalised through techniques like **Dynamic Difficulty Adjustment (DDA)**, **adaptive tutorials**, and **progressive skill scaffolding**.
> 
> Flow is the *empirical gold standard* of intrinsic engagement and is a high-yield concept in KTU HMI exams.

**(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for HMI relevance)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | ESE Module 2 - Internal Choice A]**  
> **(a)** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain **Csikszentmihalyi's Flow Theory** and its application in designing engaging HMIs. Discuss how the four-quadrant model is used by designers. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** Describe the **Gamification Framework** proposed by Werbach and Hunter. List and briefly explain any five *components* of gamification with a real-world HMI example for each. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — Flow Theory (7 marks):**

> **1. Definition (2 marks):** Csikszentmihalyi's Flow Theory (1975, 1990) defines *flow* as the optimal psychological state of complete immersion in an activity, occurring when the perceived *challenge* of a task matches the user's *skill*, with immediate *feedback*. The user experiences focused attention, a loss of self-consciousness, distorted time perception, and intrinsic enjoyment.
> 
> **2. The Four-Quadrant Model (3 marks):** The model arranges user experience along two axes:
> - X-axis: Challenge Level (low → high)
> - Y-axis: Skill Level (low → high)
> 
> The four resulting quadrants are:
> - **Quadrant I — Apathy** (low skill, low challenge): the user is disengaged.
> - **Quadrant II — Relaxation** (high skill, low challenge): the user is comfortable but bored.
> - **Quadrant III — FLOW** (high skill, high challenge): the optimal state of intrinsic engagement.
> - **Quadrant IV — Anxiety** (low skill, high challenge): the user is overwhelmed and frustrated.
> 
> **3. Application in HMI Design (2 marks):**
> - Designers use **Dynamic Difficulty Adjustment (DDA)** to keep the user in Quadrant III.
> - **Adaptive tutorials** scaffold skill when the user drifts to Quadrant IV.
> - **Novelty injection** (new features, surprise rewards) pulls the user from Quadrant II back into flow.
> - **Real-world examples:** VS Code's intelligent autocomplete keeps programmers in flow; *Angry Birds* uses staged level design to maintain flow across thousands of users.
> 
> The diagram (already presented in Section 4.2) should be redrawn in the answer script for full marks.

---

**Model Solution (b) — Gamification Framework (7 marks):**

> **1. Framework Overview (2 marks):** Werbach and Hunter (2012) classify gamification into three hierarchical layers:
> - **Dynamics** — the high-level *feel* of the system (narrative, progression, constraints).
> - **Mechanics** — the *processes* that drive engagement (rewards, competition, chance).
> - **Components** — the *concrete* elements visible to the user (points, badges, leaderboards).
> 
> The pyramid structure is mandatory in the answer: see Section 4.3 diagram.
> 
> **2. Five Components with Examples (5 marks — 1 mark each):**
> 
> | # | Component | Real-World HMI Example |
> |---|---|---|
> | 1 | **Points** | Khan Academy awards energy points for each problem solved |
> | 2 | **Badges** | Foursquare gives "Mayor" badge for frequent check-ins |
> | 3 | **Leaderboards** | Strava cycling app ranks weekly mileage |
> | 4 | **Levels** | Duolingo's language tree unlocks new units as XP grows |
> | 5 | **Quests** | Headspace offers themed meditation challenges (e.g., "30 days of Calm") |
> 
> Each example should be explained in 1–2 sentences, linking the component to the engagement it produces. The examiner awards marks for both *correct identification* and *plausible HMI example*.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | ESE Module 2 - Internal Choice B]**  
> **(a)** Explain the **O'Brien & Toms Engagement Model** in detail. Describe the four pillars of engagement and how each is operationalised through specific HMI design techniques. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** Discuss the **Hedonic Motivation Model** by Hassenzahl. How do *novelty*, *interest*, and *conveyed meaning* contribute to designing for fun in an interface? Illustrate with a mobile-app case study. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — O'Brien & Toms Engagement Model (7 marks):**

> **1. Introduction (1 mark):** O'Brien and Toms (2008) proposed a model of user engagement that decomposes the construct into four measurable attributes. The model is widely adopted in HMI literature and is a high-yield KTU topic.
> 
> **2. Four Pillars (4 marks — 1 each):**
> 
> | Pillar | Definition | Design Operationalisation |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Attention (Focus)** | Cognitive and perceptual resources are directed at the interface | Visual hierarchy, motion, contrast, novelty |
> | **Interest (Curiosity)** | Intrinsic motivation to explore | Progressive disclosure, micro-rewards, Easter eggs |
> | **Affect (Emotion)** | Positive emotional response is elicited | Micro-animations, personality, surprise, sound design |
> | **Temporal Investment** | Willingness to extend the session | Save states, streaks, narrative arcs, content drip |
> 
> **3. Synthesis (2 marks):** The four pillars are not independent — they form a **positive feedback loop**. A delightful affect increases interest, which holds attention, which in turn extends time-on-task. Successful HMIs (e.g., *TikTok*) engineer *all four* simultaneously. The model is also useful for diagnostic UX audits: measuring each pillar independently reveals which one is failing.

---

**Model Solution (b) — Hedonic Motivation Model (7 marks):**

> **1. Overview (2 marks):** Marc Hassenzahl (2003, 2010) proposed that users evaluate interactive systems on two orthogonal axes: *be-goals* (instrumental, "does it work?") and *do-goals* (hedonic, "does it feel good?"). The **Hedonic-Motivation Model** decomposes the *do-goals* into three sub-dimensions: **Novelty**, **Interest**, and **Conveyed Meaning**.
> 
> **2. The Three Hedonic Drivers (3 marks):**
> - **Novelty** — surprise, the unfamiliar, the unexpected. *Example:* Google's "do a barrel roll" Easter egg.
> - **Interest** — stimulating, varied, challenging content. *Example:* Spotify's *Discover Weekly* playlist that reinvents itself every Monday.
> - **Conveyed Meaning** — signalling identity, values, or status. *Example:* Strava's "Local Legend" title that broadcasts cycling dominance in your neighbourhood.
> 
> **3. Mobile-App Case Study — Snapchat (2 marks):** Snapchat's streaks, filters, and Bitmoji avatars blend all three drivers:
> - *Novelty:* AR filters refresh daily.
> - *Interest:* The streak counter creates ongoing curiosity.
> - *Conveyed Meaning:* The Snap score publicly signals social presence.
> 
> The result: average daily session time exceeds **30 minutes**, demonstrating extreme hedonic engagement. Designers must balance hedonic drivers with utilitarian goals — pure fun without utility leads to novelty-burnout.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfalls on "Engagement and Fun":**
> 
> 1. **Conflating engagement with usability.** Engagement is *emotional and temporal*; usability is *task-efficiency*. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks if the student fails to distinguish them.
> 
> 2. **Naming the theorist without explaining the model.** Writing "Csikszentmihalyi proposed flow" is only 0.5 marks — examiners expect the **4-quadrant diagram** and the *challenge = skill* condition.
> 
> 3. **Listing gamification components without the pyramid structure.** Merely writing "points, badges, leaderboards" without classifying them under *Dynamics–Mechanics–Components* loses the structural-mark.
> 
> 4. **Skipping real-world examples.** A 7-mark question on gamification components that lacks an *HMI example per component* typically loses 2–3 marks.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting the positive feedback loop** between engagement pillars. Examiners reward answers that show *circular causality* (delight → curiosity → attention → time → engagement).
> 
> 6. **Using "fun" and "engagement" interchangeably** in the same sentence. They are *distinct*: fun is *affective*; engagement is *the entire process*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Engagement and Fun in HMI:**
> 
> - **Engagement** = multi-dimensional process; **Fun** = affective peak.
> - Four pillars of engagement (O'Brien & Toms): **A**ttention, **I**nterest, **A**ffect, **T**ime.
> - **Flow Theory (Csikszentmihalyi, 1975):** Flow = Challenge = Skill (with clear feedback).
> - Four flow quadrants: Apathy, Relaxation, **FLOW ★**, Anxiety.
> - **Gamification (Werbach & Hunter):** Dynamics + Mechanics + Components pyramid.
> - **Hedonic Motivation Model (Hassenzahl):** Novelty + Interest + Conveyed Meaning = Fun.
> - Key formula: $E = w_1 A + w_2 I + w_3 Af + w_4 T$ (with $\sum w_i = 1$).
> - Key metric: **Stickiness Index** $S = \text{DAU}/\text{MAU}$.
> - **Dynamic Difficulty Adjustment (DDA):** keeps $C/S \in [0.8, 1.5]$ for flow.
> - Real-world exemplars: **Duolingo** (streaks), **Strava** (leaderboards), **VS Code** (flow), **Snapchat** (hedonic fun).
> - **Trade-off to remember:** fun without utility = novelty-burnout; utility without fun = usable-but-unmemorable.
> - **CO mapped:** CO2 — Design engaging and emotionally resonant HMIs.
> - **Bloom's levels tested:** Understand (L2), Apply (L3), Analyse (L4).
> - **Examiner mantra:** *Always include a diagram, a theorist, a formula, and a real-world example in any 7-mark answer.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Paradigm of Interaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT753 - HUMAN-MACHINE INTERFACE > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Paradigm of Interaction # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Paradigm of Interaction** refers to the *fundamental conceptual framework or model* that defines the underlying philosophy, style, and structure governing how a **human user** communicates, controls, and exchanges information with a **machine/computer system**. In HCI literature, it represents a class of interaction styles characterized by a distinctive vocabulary of user actions and system responses, independent of any specific implementation or device. The term was crystallized in HCI theory through **Steven K. Card, Allen Newell, and Thomas P. Moran** in their seminal work *"The Psychology of Human-Computer Interaction"* (1983), and later formalized into taxonomies by **Foley, van Dam, Feiner, and Hughes** (1990) in *Computer Graphics: Principles and Practice*. ## 1.2 The Five Canonical Interaction Frameworks (Foley's Paradigm) According to Foley's 1990 taxonomy, all interactive systems can be classified into **five canonical frameworks** (often called the "Five Frameworks of Interaction"): | # | Framework | Core Idea | |---|-----------|-----------| | 1 | **Direct Manipulation** | User acts directly on visible objects in a spatial representation | | 2 | **Menu Selection** | User chooses actions from a displayed list of alternatives | | 3 | **Form Fill-in** | User enters data into structured fields with labels | | 4 | **Command Language** | User types concise, often abbreviated instructions | | 5 | **Natural Language** | User speaks/writes in their native tongue; system interprets intent | > [!NOTE] > **Hierarchy of Generality:** Foley's five frameworks are *abstractions*. A real-world interface (e.g., MS Word) is rarely "pure" — it is a *hybrid* combining menus, forms, direct manipulation, and command shortcuts (keyboard accelerators like `Ctrl+S`). ## 1.3 The Three Major Historical Paradigm Eras Beyond the per-interface taxonomy, HCI history recognizes **three dominant computing paradigms** (as categorized by Myers, Hudson, and Pausch, *Foundations of HCI*): ### A. The First Paradigm — **Batch Processing (1950s–1960s)** - User submits a *job deck* (stack of punched cards) and waits hours for output. - **No real-time feedback.** Interaction is deferred and asynchronous. ### B. The Second Paradigm — **Time-Sharing & Command-Line Interfaces (1960s–1980s)** - Multiple users share a single mainframe via **terminals** (teletype, VT100). - Douglas Engelbart's **oN-Line System (NLS, 1968)** and Xerox PARC's **Alto (1973)** pioneered this shift. - Invented concepts: *file systems, virtual memory, multi-user shells.* ### C. The Third Paradigm — **Graphical User Interface / WIMP (1984–Present)** - **WIMP = Windows, Icons, Menus, Pointing device.** - Apple Macintosh (1984), Microsoft Windows (1985), X11/Motif (1980s). - Invented by Xerox PARC; popularized by Apple and Microsoft. ### D. The Emerging Fourth & Fifth Paradigms - **Ubiquitous Computing (Ubicomp)** — Mark Weiser, Xerox PARC, 1991. - **Mobile/Tangible/Multimodal Interfaces** — capacitive touch, voice (Siri, 2011), AR/VR, BCI. ## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Restaurant Kitchens 🍳 > [!TIP] > **Intuition Box:** Think of interaction paradigms as *different kitchen styles*: > - **Batch processing** = *Industrial canteen*: workers line up, place tray orders hours in advance, eat cold food later. > - **Time-sharing / CLI** = *Traditional restaurant*: you tell the chef the exact recipe (e.g., "steak, medium-rare, no salt") — precise but requires expertise. > - **GUI / WIMP** = *Buffet with labeled trays*: you see everything visually and point at what you want. No recipe knowledge required. > - **Natural language** = *Personal waiter who speaks your dialect*: you just say "I'm hungry for something spicy" and they interpret. > - **Direct manipulation** = *Build-your-own-pizza counter*: you physically drag toppings onto your dough and see the pizza evolve. Each "kitchen style" is a **paradigm** — a different philosophy for the same underlying goal of getting food to the customer. Likewise, every interaction paradigm is a different philosophy for getting user intent translated into machine behavior. ## 1.5 Standard Metrics & Key Constants (Highlighted) - **Fitts's Law Index of Difficulty (ID)** — measured in **bits**. - **Hick's Law Reaction Time (RT)** — measured in **milliseconds (ms)**. - **Throughput (TP)** — measured in **bits/second**. - **Stewart's Workload (W)** — dimensionless ratio. - **GOMS (Goals, Operators, Methods, Selection rules)** — Card, Moran & Newell, **1983** — temporal unit **τ ≈ 50 ms**. - **WIMP** — Windows, Icons, Menus, Pointing device — coined **1984**. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Operational Analysis of Each Paradigm ### 2.1.1 Direct Manipulation (DM) **Definition (Shneiderman, 1983):** "*A continuous representation of the object of interest and rapid, reversible, incremental actions with feedback*." **The Six Properties of Direct Manipulation** (Shneiderman): 1. **Continuous representation** of objects and actions. 2. **Physical actions** (clicks, drags) replace typed syntax. 3. **Rapid, incremental, reversible operations** with immediate visual feedback. 4. **Layered approach** to learning (novice → expert). 5. **Error prevention** by limiting valid actions. 6. **WYSIWYG** — *What You See Is What You Get*. **Why it works:** Leverages human **spatial cognition** and **motor skills** developed over millions of years — the user "manipulates" the digital object as if it were a physical one. **Example:** Dragging a file icon to the recycle bin in Windows; dragging the orange time slider in iMovie. **Drawback:** Scales poorly with thousands of objects (canvas becomes cluttered) and offers limited precision for expert users. --- ### 2.1.2 Menu Selection **Mechanism:** System displays a list of valid actions; user selects one with mouse, keyboard, or touch. **Sub-types:** - **Flat (linear) menu** — all options visible at once. - **Hierarchical / cascading menu** — sub-menus nest under parent items. - **Pop-up / context menu** — appears at cursor on right-click. - **Pull-down menu** — drops from a top bar. - **Pie / radial menu** — items arranged in a circle (Mark Apple, 1979). - **Marking menus** — directional gestures produce menus (Kurtenbach, 1993). **Why it works:** Reduces **Hick's Law** cognitive load (RT = a + b · log₂n) — users only react to visible options. Minimizes the need to recall command syntax (**recognition over recall** — Don Norman's *Design of Everyday Things*). **Drawback:** Slow for power users; menus "hide" advanced features. --- ### 2.1.3 Form Fill-in **Mechanism:** User enters data into labeled, structured fields. Used primarily for **data entry** — DBMS forms, government e-forms, survey forms. **Why it works:** - Cognitively easy — fields prompt the user. - Validates input *as you type* (format, length, data type). - Familiar from paper forms — low learning curve. **Design rules (Tog's First Principles, 1992):** - One field per logical datum. - Tab order = reading order (top-to-bottom, left-to-right). - Provide format examples in placeholders (e.g., `DD/MM/YYYY`). - Use combo boxes for enumerated values. **Drawback:** Highly domain-specific; not general-purpose. --- ### 2.1.4 Command Language (CLI) **Mechanism:** User types terse, often abbreviated textual instructions. **Famous examples:** UNIX shell (`ls -la /home`), MS-DOS (`dir *.txt`), Git (`git commit -m "msg"`), VIM (`dd`, `:wq`). **Why it works:** - **Extreme expressiveness** — pipes (`|`), redirection (`>`), scripting. - **Lowest memory overhead** — no graphical rendering. - **Automatable** — ideal for servers, embedded systems, DevOps. **Drawback:** Steep learning curve; high error rate for novices; lacks discoverability. --- ### 2.1.5 Natural Language (NL) **Mechanism:** User speaks/writes in human language; system parses syntax + semantics. **Underlying tech:** NLP, NLU, LLMs, semantic grammars, ATNs (Augmented Transition Networks — Woods, 1970). **Why it works:** Most *natural* for humans; no training needed. **Drawback:** **Ambiguity** ("book" = noun vs. verb vs. reserve), brittleness, narrow domain. **Modern incarnation:** Conversational AI, ChatGPT, Siri, Alexa, Google Assistant, Copilot. --- ## 2.2 Donald Norman's *Seven Stages of Action* (Action Cycle) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Norman's Stage Model (1988)** is the most cited theory of human–machine interaction. It is the *bridge* between paradigms and cognition. The cycle has two phases, each with stages: | Phase | Stage | Process | Example (Smart Thermostat) | |-------|-------|---------|----------------------------| | **Execution** | 1. **Goal** | Form intention | "I want the room warmer." | | | 2. **Plan** | Decide action | "Press the up arrow." | | | 3. **Specify** | Execute action | Finger presses button | | | 4. **Perform** | Physical motor | Actuator fires | | **Evaluation** | 5. **Perceive** | Sense system state | See new temperature reading | | | 6. **Interpret** | Compare with intent | "Yes, it rose." | | | 7. **Evaluate** | Judge success | "Goal achieved." | **Two critical gulfs identified by Norman:** - **Gulf of Execution** — gap between user's intent and the actions the system affords. - **Gulf of Evaluation** — gap between system state and user's ability to perceive/interpret it. The paradigm choice (DM vs. CLI vs. NL) determines *how narrow* these gulfs are. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **All formulas below are examinable in KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2 scope.** | # | Law / Equation | Formula | Variables | Engineering Utility | |---|----------------|---------|-----------|---------------------| | 1 | **Fitts's Law (1954)** | $T = a + b \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | $T$=movement time, $D$=distance to target, $W$=target width, $a,b$=empirical constants | Predicts pointing-device time — used in UI design, touchscreen layouts, ISO 9241-411 | | 2 | **Index of Difficulty (ID)** | $ID = \log_{2}\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | Same as above | Measured in **bits** | | 3 | **Hick's Law (1952)** | $T = a + b \cdot \log_{2}(n+1)$ | $T$=reaction time, $n$=number of equally likely choices, $a,b$=constants | Predicts decision time — guides menu depth/width design | | 4 | **Index of Difficulty (Hick)** | $ID = \log_{2}(n+1)$ | $n$ = number of choices | Measured in **bits** | | 5 | **Fitts Throughput (TP)** | $TP = \dfrac{ID_{avg}}{MT_{avg}}$ | $ID_{avg}$=mean ID, $MT_{avg}$=mean MT | Measures *human performance* in bits/sec; ISO 9241-9 standard | | 6 | **Stewart's Workload** | $W = N \cdot \left(\dfrac{S}{R}\right)$ | $N$=no. of components, $S$=stimulus, $R$=response | Quantifies cognitive load in supervisory control | | 7 | **GOMS Model Time** | $T = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \tau_{i}$ | $\tau$ = time for operator $i$ | Predicts expert user task time | | 8 | **Keystroke-Level Model (KLM)** | $T_{execute} = T_K + T_P + T_H + T_D + T_M + T_R$ | $K$=keystroke, $P$=pointing, $H$=homing, $D$=drawing, $M$=mental, $R$=response | Card-Moran-Newell, 1980; predicts expert performance | | 9 | **Power Law of Practice** | $T_n = T_1 \cdot n^{-\alpha}$ | $T_n$=time on trial $n$, $\alpha$=learning rate (0.2–0.6) | Models learning curves | | 10 | **Accot-Zhai Steering Law** | $T = a + b \cdot \dfrac{A}{W}$ | $A$=path length, $W$=path width | Models trajectory tasks (sliders, scroll wheels) | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** The most-tested formulas in the previous KTU papers are *Fitts's Law*, *Hick's Law*, and *KLM*. Know the units (**bits** for ID, **bits/sec** for TP, **seconds** for time). --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Fitts's Law** → Apple iOS designers placed the **back button in the top-left** because the thumb's natural swing path is the **bottom-left**, and large targets at easy positions reduce MT. - **Hick's Law** → Phone dialer keeps n ≤ 12 (0-9, *, #) for fast selection. - **KLM** → Used in aviation cockpit design (Boeing 787) to estimate pilot response time. - **Stewart's Workload** → NASA mission control consoles use Stewart's metrics to evaluate astronaut cognitive load during EVA. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Fitts's Law from Information Theory Fitts's Law is the **single most important empirical law in HCI** (MacKenzie, 1992). It is *derived* from Shannon-Hartley's theorem of channel capacity. ### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Shannon-Hartley Theorem (1948) The **channel capacity** $C$ (bits/sec) of a noisy communication channel is given by Shannon-Hartley: $$C = B \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$$ where $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), $S$ = signal power, $N$ = noise power. Fitts (1954) hypothesized that **human motor control** behaves like a noisy communication channel transmitting information from the brain to the limb. ### 3.1.2 Reinterpretation for a Pointing Task In a pointing task, the user must move from a starting position to a target of width $W$ located at distance $D$. Fitts defined: - **Distance to traverse** = $D$ - **Noise / uncertainty** in the endpoint = $W$ (analogous to noise margin) The **index of difficulty** $ID$ (in bits) is defined as the information required to reach the target: $$ID = \log_{2}\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$$ The "+1" prevents a singularity when $D=0$ or $W \to \infty$. ### 3.1.3 The Movement Time Linear Model Empirically (Fitts, 1954; Card, English, Burr, 1978), the *movement time* $T$ is **linearly proportional** to $ID$: $$T = a + b \cdot ID$$ Substituting the definition of $ID$: $$T = a + b \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$$ This is **Fitts's Law** as we know it. ### 3.1.4 Derivation of Throughput (TP) Throughput measures *effective channel capacity* of the human motor system. Define: $$TP = \dfrac{ID_{mean}}{MT_{mean}}$$ A higher $TP$ means the user transmits more bits of motor information per second — i.e., the device is better matched to the user. ### 3.1.5 Worked Example — Fitts's Law Calculation > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam-Style Solved Problem (3-Mark, Direct):** > A user must click an icon $D = 200$ pixels away, with target width $W = 40$ pixels. For a standard mouse, $a = 50$ ms, $b = 150$ ms/bit. Compute (i) ID, (ii) movement time T, (iii) throughput. **Step 1 — Index of Difficulty:** $$ID = \log_{2}\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right) = \log_{2}\!\left(\dfrac{200}{40} + 1\right) = \log_{2}\!\left(5 + 1\right) = \log_{2}\! 6$$ $$ID = \log_{2}\! 6 = \dfrac{\ln 6}{\ln 2} = \dfrac{1.7918}{0.6931} = 2.585 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 2 — Movement Time:** $$T = a + b \cdot ID = 50 + 150 \times 2.585 = 50 + 387.75 = 437.75 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 3 — Throughput:** $$TP = \dfrac{ID}{T} = \dfrac{2.585 \text{ bits}}{0.43775 \text{ s}} = 5.91 \text{ bits/s}$$ > **Answer:** ID = **2.585 bits**, T = **437.75 ms**, TP = **5.91 bits/s**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Hick-Hyman Law ### 3.2.1 Setup In a *choice reaction* task, a user must identify a stimulus among $n$ equally probable alternatives and respond. Hick (1952) and Hyman (1953) independently proposed: $$T = a + b \cdot \log_{2}(n)$$ ### 3.2.2 Information-Theoretic Justification By **Hartley's Law of Information (1928)**, the information in $n$ equally-likely choices is: $$H = \log_{2}(n) \text{ bits}$$ Assuming the brain is a **single-channel serial processor** (Donders' Subtractive Method, 1868), reaction time is linear in the information processed: $$T = a + b \cdot H = a + b \cdot \log_{2}(n)$$ The "+1" variant is often used to avoid log(0) when $n=0$: $$T = a + b \cdot \log_{2}(n+1)$$ ### 3.2.3 Worked Example — Hick's Law > A driver must press one of 4 brake modes (1, 2, 3, 4) based on dashboard LED. If $a = 200$ ms, $b = 80$ ms/bit, find the reaction time. $$T = a + b \cdot \log_{2}(n+1) = 200 + 80 \cdot \log_{2}(5) = 200 + 80 \cdot 2.322 = 200 + 185.76$$ $$T = 385.76 \text{ ms}$$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Paradigms | Property | Direct Manipulation | Menu Selection | Form Fill-in | Command Language | Natural Language | |----------|--------------------|----------------|--------------|------------------|------------------| | **Recognition vs Recall** | Recognition (visual) | Recognition | Recognition | Recall (syntax) | Recognition | | **Learning curve** | Shallow | Shallow | Shallow | Steep | Very shallow | | **Expert speed** | Medium | Slow | Slow | **Fastest** | Medium | | **Error tolerance** | High (reversible) | Medium | High (validated) | **Lowest** | Low | | **Discoverability** | High | High | High | Low | Medium | | **Scalability** | Poor (clutter) | Poor (deep nesting) | Domain-only | **Highest** | Medium | | **Cognitive load** | Low (spatial) | Low | Low | **Highest** | Low | | **Automatable** | Hard | Medium | Hard | **Easy (scripts)** | Medium | | **Best for** | Visual design tasks | Novice users | Data entry | Sysadmin, DevOps | Search, assistants | | **Fitts ID impact** | Low (large targets) | Medium | Low | N/A | N/A | | **Hick n impact** | Low (≤ 7 visible) | High | Low | N/A | N/A | | **Example system** | macOS Finder, Figma | App menus, dialer | HTML form, Aadhaar | UNIX, Git, VIM | Siri, ChatGPT | --- ## 3.4 Exhaustive Step-by-Step — Card's GOMS Model **GOMS** = **G**oals, **O**perators, **M**ethods, **S**election rules. Card, Moran, Newell (1983). **Step 1 — Decompose goal** (e.g., "Save a file as PDF in MS Word"). **Step 2 — List operators** with their base times: - $T_K$ (keystroke) ≈ 0.28 s for expert - $T_P$ (pointing) ≈ 1.10 s - $T_H$ (homing hand) ≈ 0.40 s - $T_D$ (drawing) ≈ varies - $T_M$ (mental prep) ≈ 1.35 s - $T_R$ (response) ≈ varies **Step 3 — Sum the operators** for the chosen method (no Selection Rule needed if only one method). **Example — Predicting "File → Save As → filename → Save":** $$T = T_{K} + T_{P} + T_{M} + T_{K} + T_{K} + T_{P} + T_{K} + T_{R}$$ $$T = 0.28 + 1.10 + 1.35 + 0.28 + 0.28 + 1.10 + 0.28 + 0.40 = 5.07 \text{ s}$$ This is a **KLM (Keystroke-Level Model)** prediction — a simplified GOMS. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Historical Evolution of Interaction Paradigms ```mermaid graph TD A[1950s: Batch Processing
Punched Cards] --> B[1960s: Time-Sharing
Teletype Terminals] B --> C[1970s: Command Line Interfaces
UNIX Shell, MS-DOS] C --> D[1984: WIMP / GUI
Macintosh, Windows] D --> E[1990s: Web Interfaces
HTML Forms, Browsers] E --> F[2007: Touch / Mobile
iPhone, Android] F --> G[2011: Voice Assistants
Siri, Alexa] G --> H[2020s: Generative AI
ChatGPT, Copilot] H --> I[Future: BCI, AR, Quantum UX] subgraph "Foley's 5 Frameworks" F1[Direct Manipulation] F2[Menu Selection] F3[Form Fill-in] F4[Command Language] F5[Natural Language] end ``` --- ## 4.2 Norman's Seven Stages of Action Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR A[1. Goal
Form intent] --> B[2. Plan
Decide action] B --> C[3. Specify
Sequence steps] C --> D[4. Perform
Execute motor] D --> E[5. Perceive
Sense state] E --> F[6. Interpret
Compare with intent] F --> G[7. Evaluate
Judge success] G --> A X[Gulf of Execution
Intent to Action] -.-> B Y[Gulf of Evaluation
State to Perception] -.-> E ``` --- ## 4.3 Foley Framework Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Choose Interaction Framework] --> B{Is task repetitive
and expert-only?} B -- Yes --> C[Command Language
High expressiveness] B -- No --> D{Is task data-entry
structured fields?} D -- Yes --> E[Form Fill-in
Validated input] D -- No --> F{Is task visual
spatial design?} F -- Yes --> G[Direct Manipulation
WYSIWYG, drag-drop] F -- No --> H{Is task general
novice-friendly?} H -- Yes --> I[Menu Selection
Recognition over recall] H -- No --> J[Natural Language
Conversational AI] ``` --- ## 4.4 Fitts's Law — Conceptual Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR A[User Motor System
Acts as Noisy Channel] --> B[Input:
Brain Intent - D, W] B --> C[Fitts's Law:
T = a + b log2 D/W + 1] C --> D[Outputs:
T, ID bits, TP bits per s] D --> E[UI Design Application
Target Size, Position] subgraph Engineering Use E1[Apple iOS - thumb zone] E2[ISO 9241-9 mouse test] E3[VR controller latency] end E --> E1 E --> E2 E --> E3 ``` --- ## 4.5 Block Architecture — HCI Paradigm Layers ```mermaid graph TB L1[Layer 5: User Task Domain
Why does user interact] L2[Layer 4: Semantic Layer
Meaning, dialog content] L3[Layer 3: Dialog Layer
Foley Framework DM, Menu, Form, CLI, NL] L4[Layer 2: Presentation Layer
WIMP, GUI, Voice, Gesture] L5[Layer 1: Hardware Layer
Keyboard, Mouse, Touch, Mic, Sensor] L6[Layer 0: Operating System Layer
UNIX, Windows, iOS, Android] L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L6 ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model] **Define "Paradigm of Interaction" in HCI. List any four paradigms of interaction as defined by Foley et al.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A *paradigm of interaction* is a fundamental conceptual framework defining how a user communicates with a computer system, characterized by a distinct vocabulary of user actions and corresponding system responses (1 mark). Foley's five paradigms are (any four, 0.5 each): 1. **Direct Manipulation** — user acts on visible objects. 2. **Menu Selection** — user picks from a displayed list. 3. **Form Fill-in** — user enters data into structured fields. 4. **Command Language** — user types textual instructions. 5. **Natural Language** — user speaks/writes in human tongue (1 mark for naming them). --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model] **State Fitts's Law. What is the Index of Difficulty, and what are its units?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Fitts's Law (1954) models the **time** required to move to a target as a function of distance and target width (1 mark): $$T = a + b \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$$ where $T$ = movement time, $D$ = distance to target, $W$ = target width, $a$ and $b$ are empirically determined constants (1 mark). **Index of Difficulty (ID)** is the logarithmic term $ID = \log_2(D/W + 1)$, measured in **bits** (1 mark). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Module 2):** > 1. **Do NOT confuse Fitts's Law with Hick's Law** — Fitts = *motor pointing*, Hick = *decision/choice*. Examiners will deliberately swap them. > 2. **Units are mandatory** — always state "bits" for ID and "bits/s" for Throughput. Loss of 1 mark if omitted. > 3. **In Foley's paradigms**, do NOT list "WIMP" as a separate paradigm — WIMP is a *style* of direct-manipulation GUI, not a Foley framework. > 4. **In Norman's model**, students often miss the **two gulfs** (Execution and Evaluation) — these are *essential* for full marks. > 5. **In derivations**, do not skip the "+1" inside log — this is a common mistake. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — Foley's Paradigms + Fitts's Law **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, ESE 2024]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain Foley's five frameworks of interaction with a real-world example for each. Discuss the relative advantages of *Direct Manipulation* over *Command Language* interfaces. **(b) [7 Marks]** A user moves a mouse cursor to a target 360 pixels away, with target width 40 pixels. Given $a = 50$ ms and $b = 100$ ms/bit, compute: (i) Index of Difficulty, (ii) Movement Time, (iii) Throughput.
