Comment on the result. **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** **Foley's Five Frameworks** (1990) provide a *taxonomy* of interaction styles (1 mark): 1. **Direct Manipulation** — User acts on visible objects in a spatial representation. *Example:* Drag-and-drop file move in Windows Explorer, Figma's canvas. *Properties:* WYSIWYG, reversible, immediate feedback (Shneiderman, 1983) (1 mark). 2. **Menu Selection** — User chooses from a displayed list. *Example:* "File → Open" dropdown in MS Word, mobile hamburger menu, pie menu in Apple watchOS (1 mark). 3. **Form Fill-in** — User fills structured labeled fields. *Example:* Aadhaar enrollment form, Google account signup, passport application (1 mark). 4. **Command Language** — User types terse textual instructions. *Example:* UNIX `grep -r "error" /var/log/`, Git `git rebase -i HEAD~3` (1 mark). 5. **Natural Language** — User speaks/writes in human tongue. *Example:* "Hey Siri, set alarm for 7 AM", ChatGPT query (1 mark). **DM vs CLI** (1 mark synthesis): | Aspect | Direct Manipulation | Command Language | |--------|---------------------|------------------| | Learning | Shallow (intuitive) | Steep (syntax-heavy) | | Error recovery | Reversible (undo) | Difficult | | Expert speed | Moderate | **Highest** | | Discoverability | High (visible objects) | Low (must remember) | | Automation | Limited | **Easy (shell scripts)** | Direct manipulation is preferred for **novice users** and **visual/spatial tasks**; CLI is preferred for **repetitive expert tasks** and **automation** (1 mark). **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** Given: $D = 360$ px, $W = 40$ px, $a = 50$ ms, $b = 100$ ms/bit. **(i) Index of Difficulty (3 marks):** $$ID = \log_{2}\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right) = \log_{2}\!\left(\dfrac{360}{40} + 1\right) = \log_{2}\!\left(9 + 1\right) = \log_{2}\! 10$$ $$ID = \dfrac{\ln 10}{\ln 2} = \dfrac{2.3026}{0.6931} = 3.322 \text{ bits}$$ **(ii) Movement Time (2 marks):** $$T = a + b \cdot ID = 50 + 100 \cdot 3.322 = 50 + 332.2 = 382.2 \text{ ms}$$ **(iii) Throughput (1 mark):** $$TP = \dfrac{ID}{T} = \dfrac{3.322 \text{ bits}}{0.3822 \text{ s}} = 8.69 \text{ bits/s}$$ **Comment (1 mark):** A TP of ~8.69 bits/s is in the normal range for a desktop mouse (typical: 4–10 bits/s). The user is a competent operator. A higher TP indicates better device mastery; lower TP would suggest fatigue or device inadequacy. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Norman's Action Cycle + Hick's Law **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, ESE 2024]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe Donald Norman's *Seven Stages of Action* model. Explain the concepts of *Gulf of Execution* and *Gulf of Evaluation* with examples. **(b) [7 Marks]** A pilot must select one of 8 warning lights on a cockpit panel. Using Hick's Law with $a = 200$ ms and $b = 100$ ms/bit, compute the reaction time. If the number of lights is reduced to 4, what is the percentage reduction in reaction time? Comment on the design implication. **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** **Norman (1988) Action Cycle** (4 marks for the table): The model has **two phases** with **seven stages** in a continuous loop: | # | Stage | Phase | Description | |---|-------|-------|-------------| | 1 | Goal | Execution | Form the high-level intention | | 2 | Plan | Execution | Decide the sequence of actions | | 3 | Specify | Execution | Identify the specific commands | | 4 | Perform | Execution | Execute the physical motor action | | 5 | Perceive | Evaluation | Sense the new system state | | 6 | Interpret | Evaluation | Compare state with intention | | 7 | Evaluate | Evaluation | Judge whether goal was met | **Gulf of Execution** (1.5 marks): The gap between the *user's intention* and the *actions the system affords*. - *Example:* In a microwave with 25 unlabeled buttons, the user knows they want to "defrost chicken" but cannot easily map this to a button. A well-designed interface (single "Defrost" button) narrows this gulf. **Gulf of Evaluation** (1.5 marks): The gap between the *system's actual state* and the *user's ability to perceive/interpret it*. - *Example:* A progress bar that updates 1% at a time without a "remaining time" label leaves the user uncertain. Adding a countdown closes the gulf. --- **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** Given: $a = 200$ ms, $b = 100$ ms/bit. **Case 1: n = 8 lights (3 marks):** $$T_1 = a + b \cdot \log_{2}(n+1) = 200 + 100 \cdot \log_{2}(9)$$ $$\log_{2}\! 9 = \dfrac{\ln 9}{\ln 2} = \dfrac{2.1972}{0.6931} = 3.170 \text{ bits}$$ $$T_1 = 200 + 100 \cdot 3.170 = 200 + 317.0 = 517.0 \text{ ms}$$ **Case 2: n = 4 lights (2 marks):** $$T_2 = 200 + 100 \cdot \log_{2}(5) = 200 + 100 \cdot 2.322 = 200 + 232.2 = 432.2 \text{ ms}$$ **Percentage reduction (1 mark):** $$\Delta\% = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1} \times 100 = \dfrac{517.0 - 432.2}{517.0} \times 100 = \dfrac{84.8}{517.0} \times 100 = 16.4\%$$ **Comment (1 mark):** Reducing 8 lights to 4 cuts reaction time by ~16.4% (~85 ms saved per decision). In critical **aviation design**, this is significant — designers should group warnings into ≤ 4–5 categories, use color-coding, and rely on **auditory** warnings for the *most* critical events (auditory reaction time is faster than visual, Hick's Law applies less strictly). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Paradigm of Interaction** - **Definition (must know verbatim):** A paradigm of interaction is a *fundamental conceptual framework* defining how a user communicates with a computer, characterized by its own vocabulary of actions and responses. - **Foley's 5 Frameworks (memorize with 1 example each):** - **Direct Manipulation** → Figma, Finder drag-drop - **Menu Selection** → File menu, hamburger - **Form Fill-in** → Aadhaar form, signup - **Command Language** → UNIX shell, Git - **Natural Language** → Siri, ChatGPT - **Historical Paradigms (3 eras):** Batch (1950s) → Time-share/CLI (1960s–80s) → WIMP/GUI (1984+). Now: *Ubiquitous*, *Mobile*, *Conversational AI*, *BCI*. - **WIMP** = Windows, Icons, Menus, Pointing device (1984, Apple Mac). - **Norman's 7 Stages of Action:** Goal → Plan → Specify → Perform → Perceive → Interpret → Evaluate (two phases: Execution + Evaluation). - **Two Gulfs (must know both):** - **Gulf of Execution** — Intent → Action - **Gulf of Evaluation** — State → Perception - **Fitts's Law:** $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W + 1)$, **ID in bits**, **TP in bits/s**. - **Hick's Law:** $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$, predicts *decision* time. - **KLM Operators:** $T_K, T_P, T_H, T_D, T_M, T_R$ — used in GOMS prediction. - **GOMS** = Goals, Operators, Methods, Selection rules (Card, Moran, Newell, 1983). - **Stewart's Workload:** $W = N \cdot (S/R)$ — NASA cognitive load metric. - **Power Law of Practice:** $T_n = T_1 \cdot n^{-\alpha}$, with $\alpha \approx 0.2$–$0.6$. - **Accot-Zhai Steering Law:** $T = a + b \cdot (A/W)$ — for sliders/scrolls. - **Key constants to memorize:** - $D$, $W$ in **pixels** (or cm in some studies) - $T$ in **milliseconds (ms)** - $ID$ in **bits** - $TP$ in **bits per second** - **Don Norman's book:** *The Design of Everyday Things* (originally *The Psychology of Everyday Things*, 1988) — cite if asked about "affordances" or "mapping". - **Shneiderman's 6 properties of DM:** Continuous representation, Physical actions, Rapid reversible operations, Layered learning, Error prevention, WYSIWYG. - **Recognition vs Recall (Mandler's Law):** Menus > Commands for novice users; Commands > Menus for experts. - **Modern paradigm shift:** From *Direct Manipulation* to *Indirect Manipulation* (voice/gesture/AI) — note for viva. ---

topics.title: Platforms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT753 - HUMAN-MACHINE INTERFACE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Platforms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** engineering, a **Platform** is defined as the complete hardware-software ecosystem (including its operating system, input/output modalities, form factor, and development framework) on which a user interacts with a computational system. A platform encapsulates the **physical substrate** (sensors, display, processor), the **logical substrate** (OS, runtime, APIs), and the **interaction vocabulary** (gestures, commands, affordances) that jointly constrain and enable HMI design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style):** A platform is a *unified technological base* that mediates between the user and the application logic, determining the available input vocabulary, output bandwidth, latency tolerance, and design constraints for the human-machine dialogue.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Stage and the Actor":**
> Imagine a theatre stage. The **stage** (its size, shape, curtains, lighting rigs, microphones) is the **platform**. The **actor** (the user) must adapt their performance to the stage they are given. A small thrust stage (a smartwatch) demands tiny, precise gestures; a vast proscenium (a desktop PC) allows rich, multi-window performances; a street corner (a vehicle dashboard) restricts the actor to brief, safe, glanceable movements. The *play* (the application) is the same in spirit, but the *performance vocabulary* changes entirely with the platform. HMI designers are essentially **stage managers** who choreograph the actor's movements to fit the platform's geometry.

### 1.3 Primary Categories of HMI Platforms

| # | Platform Class | Canonical Examples | Defining Trait |
|---|----------------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Desktop / Workstation** | Windows PC, macOS, Linux Workstation | High bandwidth input (keyboard, mouse, multi-monitor) |
| 2 | **Mobile / Handheld** | iOS, Android, iPadOS | Touch-first, small visual angle, on-the-go use |
| 3 | **Web (Cross-Platform)** | Browsers (Chrome, Safari, Firefox) | Resolution-independent, pointer+keyboard hybrid |
| 4 | **Wearable** | Smartwatch (Wear OS, watchOS), AR/VR headsets | Glanceable, micro-interactions, body-mounted |
| 5 | **Embedded / Appliance** | ATMs, kiosks, microwave ovens, industrial HMIs | Single-purpose, fixed context, restricted input |
| 6 | **Vehicle / Automotive** | In-Vehicle Infotainment (IVI), HUDs | Safety-critical, glanceable, driver-distraction bound |
| 7 | **Console / Entertainment** | PlayStation, Xbox, Nintendo Switch | Gamepad-centric, TV-distance viewing |
| 8 | **Smart Home / IoT** | Smart speakers (Alexa, Google Home), hubs | Voice-first, ambient, hands-busy contexts |
| 9 | **Large-Format / Public** | Interactive whiteboards, digital signage | Multi-user, long-distance viewing, gesture/touch hybrid |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The platform is the *first design decision* in any HMI project. Choosing a platform is **not a coding decision** — it is a *human-factors decision* because it determines the user's posture, attention budget, sensory bandwidth, and error-recovery options.

### 1.4 The Four Pillars of a Platform

Every HMI platform can be decomposed into exactly four orthogonal pillars:

1. **Form Factor** — physical geometry, weight, grip, mounting context.
2. **Input Vocabulary** — the catalogue of permissible user signals (touch, voice, gesture, gaze, button, stylus, mid-air).
3. **Output Vocabulary** — what the system can render back (visual pixels, haptic vibration, audio, olfactory, light).
4. **Runtime Environment** — OS, API surface, latency budget, accessibility services, and design system (HIG, Material, Fluent).

> [!NOTE]
> The *same* application (e.g., a calendar) must be **re-architected**, not merely rescaled, when moved between platforms, because these four pillars change non-linearly.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Platform-Design Constraint Cascade

When a platform is chosen, constraints propagate top-down through the entire HMI design. The cascade is:

**Form Factor → Input Vocabulary → Output Vocabulary → Runtime → Interaction Style → Error Model**

Each transition filters out design options. For example, a **smartwatch form factor** eliminates keyboard input, which eliminates text-heavy menus, which forces a **glanceable, iconographic, voice-augmented** style with a **tolerance-for-error model** that confirms destructive actions aggressively.

### 2.2 Theoretical Framework: The Platform Capability Vector

A platform $P$ can be formally modeled as a vector of measurable capabilities:

$$
P = \langle R, \, C_{in}, \, C_{out}, \, L, \, A, \, M, \, \tau \rangle
$$

Where each component is defined as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \text{Display resolution in pixels (width} \times \text{height)} \\
C_{in} &= \text{Input channel count (e.g., touch, mic, camera, buttons)} \\
C_{out} &= \text{Output channel count (e.g., display, speaker, haptic motor)} \\
L &= \text{Available luminance (nits)} \\
A &= \text{Available audio bandwidth (Hz)} \\
M &= \text{Mobility index (0=fixed, 1=fully mobile)} \\
\tau &= \text{End-to-end input-to-pixel latency budget (ms)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **latency budget $\tau$** is the single most over-looked platform parameter in KTU examinations. A desktop tolerates $\tau \approx 100\,\text{ms}$; a VR headset requires $\tau \leq 20\,\text{ms}$ to prevent motion sickness.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Parameter | Units / Range | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------|---------------------|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Visual Angle (acuity-based text sizing)** | $\theta = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{h}{2d}\right)$ | radians or degrees | Sets minimum legible font size per viewing distance |
| 2 | **Pixels-per-degree (PPD)** | $\text{PPD} = \dfrac{R_w}{2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{w}{2d}\right)}$ | pixels/deg | Quantifies display sharpness relative to eye resolution |
| 3 | **Touch Target Minimum (Fitts' Law)** | $T = a + b \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | seconds | Predicts selection time for a target of width $W$ at distance $D$ |
| 4 | **Effective Bandwidth (Stewart)** | $B_e = \dfrac{\text{Useful bits}}{\text{Time} + \text{Error penalty}}$ | bits/sec | Measures channel capacity of a modality |
| 5 | **Glove-Touch Attenuation Factor** | $C_{\text{gloved}} = C_{\text{bare}} \cdot \alpha$ | unitless, $\alpha \approx 0.45$ | Reduces effective input precision for industrial platforms |
| 6 | **Sunlight Contrast Ratio** | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{L_{\text{max}}}{L_{\text{min}}}$ | ratio (e.g., 1000:1) | Outdoor/automotive legibility requirement |
| 7 | **Frame Latency Budget** | $\tau_{\text{frame}} = \dfrac{1}{\text{FPS}}$ | ms | E.g., 60 FPS → 16.67 ms per frame |
| 8 | **Haptic Resolution** | $N_h = 2^{n_{\text{actuators}} \cdot n_{\text{levels}}}$ | discrete steps | Encodes distinguishable vibration patterns |
| 9 | **Pupil-Distance Coverage (VR)** | $\text{IPD range} = 54 - 74\,\text{mm}$ (typical adult) | mm | Must be supported by headset hardware |
| 10 | **Field of View (VR/AR)** | $\text{FoV} = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{w}{2d}\right)$ | degrees | Typical HMD: 90°–120° |

> [!TIP]
> **Fitts' Law** is the most-tested formula across KTU HMI papers. Memorize it as the *Index of Difficulty* $ID = \log_2(D/W + 1)$ form.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive HUD design** uses the **Visual Angle** formula to size digital speedometer digits so they subtend $\theta \geq 0.5°$ at the driver's eye position ($\approx 750\,\text{mm}$ from the steering column), guaranteeing a **2-second read time** at highway speeds.
- **VR sickness prevention** uses the **Frame Latency Budget** — a 90 Hz headset with motion-to-photon latency above 20 ms induces cybersickness in $\approx 25\%$ of users within 15 minutes.
- **Smartwatch UI design** uses **Fitts' Law** to justify **44 × 44 pt minimum touch targets** on Apple watchOS and **48 × 48 dp** on Wear OS — values empirically derived from finger-pad geometry, not arbitrary.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Platforms are not created equal in *interaction cost*. The same task ("reply to a message") has dramatically different completion times and error rates per platform, and the *cost asymmetry* drives the *form-factor economics* of the entire consumer electronics industry.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Derivations

### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Legible Font Size from Visual Angle

**Problem (KTU-Style):** A vehicle heads-up display (HUD) is mounted 800 mm from the driver's eye. The driver's minimum resolvable visual angle is $\theta_{\min} = 0.3°$. What is the minimum character height $h$ that guarantees legibility?

**Step 1 — State the governing relation.**

The geometry of human vision for small angles yields:

$$
h = 2d \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values.**

We have $d = 800\,\text{mm}$ and $\theta = 0.3° = 0.3 \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{180}\,\text{rad}$.

$$
h = 2 \cdot 800 \cdot \tan\!\left(\dfrac{0.3 \cdot \pi}{180 \cdot 2}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the half-angle.**

$$
\dfrac{0.3 \cdot \pi}{180 \cdot 2} = \dfrac{0.3 \pi}{360} \approx 0.002618\,\text{rad}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the small-angle approximation $\tan(x) \approx x$ for $x \ll 1$ (valid here because $0.002618 \ll 1$).**

$$
h \approx 2 \cdot 800 \cdot 0.002618
$$

**Step 5 — Final numeric evaluation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &\approx 1600 \cdot 0.002618 \\
h &\approx 4.19\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The minimum character height on a vehicle HUD viewed from 800 mm must be at least **4.2 mm**, which corresponds to roughly a **24 px** character on a typical HUD combiner. Anything smaller cannot be read while the driver's gaze is on the road (a glance budget of $\leq 2$ seconds).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Fitts' Law Touch-Target Sizing

**Problem:** A mobile app places a "Confirm Payment" button at distance $D = 35\,\text{mm}$ from the user's thumb rest position. The empirically measured constants for a thumb on a 6.5-inch smartphone are $a = 95\,\text{ms}$ and $b = 150\,\text{ms/bit}$. What button width $W$ keeps the movement time under **400 ms**?

**Step 1 — State Fitts' Law.**

$$
T = a + b \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the Index of Difficulty (ID).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
400 &= 95 + 150 \cdot \text{ID} \\
\text{ID} &= \dfrac{400 - 95}{150} \\
\text{ID} &= \dfrac{305}{150} \approx 2.033\,\text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Invert the ID equation for $W$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2.033 &= \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{35}{W} + 1\right) \\
2^{2.033} &= \dfrac{35}{W} + 1 \\
4.082 &= \dfrac{35}{W} + 1 \\
3.082 &= \dfrac{35}{W} \\
W &= \dfrac{35}{3.082} \\
W &\approx 11.36\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the platform guideline.**

> [!NOTE]
> iOS HIG mandates a minimum 44 × 44 pt target ($\approx 11.5$ mm at 1× scale). The analytical result of **11.36 mm** almost exactly matches the empirical guideline — confirming that platform design systems are themselves *engineered from Fitts' Law*, not arbitrarily chosen.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Platform × HMI Characteristic

This matrix is a **high-yield KTU revision artefact**. Each cell describes the *typical* design choice; always qualify with "platform-dependent" in exam answers.

| Design Property | Desktop | Mobile | Wearable | Vehicle | VR/AR | Embedded | Console |
|-----------------|---------|--------|----------|---------|-------|----------|---------|
| **Primary Input** | Keyboard + Mouse | Touch | Touch + Crown | Touch + Voice + Buttons | Gaze + Hand-tracking + Controllers | Buttons + Touch | Gamepad |
| **Visual Angle** | High ($\geq 20°$) | Medium ($5°$–$15°$) | Tiny ($2°$–$5°$) | Glanceable ($1°$–$3°$) | Immersive ($90°$–$120°$) | Variable | TV-distance ($10°$–$20°$) |
| **Session Length** | Hours | Minutes–1 hour | Seconds–Minutes | Glances ($< 5$ s) | Minutes–1 hour | Seconds | Hours |
| **Latency Budget ($\tau$)** | $\leq 100$ ms | $\leq 50$ ms | $\leq 50$ ms | $\leq 20$ ms (safety) | $\leq 20$ ms (sickness) | $\leq 200$ ms | $\leq 16$ ms (gaming) |
| **Error Tolerance** | Medium (undoable) | Low (confirm destructive) | Very Low (undo heavy) | Very Low (safety) | Low | High (recovery possible) | Medium |
| **Multitasking** | Heavy | Moderate | Minimal | Forbidden while moving | Moderate | None | Minimal |
| **Privacy Posture** | Private | Semi-public | Public | Public (passengers) | Public | Public | Public (living room) |
| **Audio Channel** | Stereo/Surround | Mono (receiver) | Mono (speaker) | Cabin-tuned | Spatial (HRTF) | Beep/TTS | Surround |
| **Haptic Channel** | None | Taptic (Apple) | Strong vibration | Steering-wheel buzz | Controllers + Suit | Buttons | Strong rumble |
| **Power Source** | Mains | Battery (1 day) | Battery (1–2 days) | Vehicle 12V | Battery (2–3 h) | Mains/Battery | Mains/Battery |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **error tolerance** column is *the most frequently tested* in KTU essays. Justify it in terms of **reversibility** (Can the user undo?), **consequence severity** (Does a wrong tap cost money/life?), and **attention level** (Is the user looking?).

### 3.4 Worked Example: Platform Selection for an Industrial Inspection App

**Scenario (KTU-Style):** A factory wants a tablet-based HMI for technicians wearing thick gloves to inspect pressure gauges on a noisy shop floor. Recommend design constraints.

**Step 1 — Identify platform-imposed constraints.**

- **Glove-wearing** → multiply target size by factor $1/\alpha \approx 2.2$ (from glove attenuation).
- **High ambient noise** → cannot rely on audio feedback; haptic and visual are mandatory.
- **Standing posture** → device held in one hand; thumb-only reach.
- **Glare from skylights** → requires $\geq 1000$ nits display luminance.

**Step 2 — Compute the new touch-target size.**

If bare-finger minimum is 9 mm, then:

$$
W_{\text{gloved}} = \dfrac{9}{0.45} = 20\,\text{mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Choose a display luminance.**

$$
L_{\min} = 1000\,\text{nits}, \quad \text{CR}_{\min} = 1000{:}1
$$

**Step 4 — Choose input redundancy.**

- Touch (gloved) + Voice (with throat-mic noise cancellation) + Hardware scan-button.
- No reliance on audio output; mandatory haptic confirmation on every critical action.

**Step 5 — Error model.**

Destructive actions require **two-step confirmation** with a 3-second hold, because a false tap with a gloved hand is statistically very likely.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Recommending "make the text larger" without computing the *visual angle* or *touch target* from platform constraints. KTU examiners mark for *justified numerical reasoning*, not vague advice.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Platform Ecosystem Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P1["Desktop Platforms"]
        D1["Windows PC"]

    end

        M1["Android Phones and Tablets"]

    end
    A["Application Logic Layer"] --> P1

```

### 4.2 Platform Capability Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    B --> C{"Mobility Need"}

```
