Real-world incidents (e.g., the 2010 PCA pump overdoses) have shown that such designs have caused patient deaths.

**Cognitive Load Analysis (2 marks):**

Using Sweller's Cognitive Load Theory:

$$
CL = \alpha I + \beta D + \gamma T
$$

- $I$ (Intrinsic load): High — programming involves 5+ parameters.
- $D$ (Extraneous load): **Excessive** — multiple screens, tiny text, confusing icons.
- $T$ (Germane load): Low — little scaffolding for schema construction.

The current design has $D$ dominating, which is the **failure mode**.

**Redesign Recommendations (3 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Single-screen programming with persistent drug library preview** — Nurse sees all parameters and the hospital's standard range simultaneously. Eliminates the "Swip-Away" pattern by removing the swipe.

2. **Mandatory two-step confirmation for any dose exceeding the soft limit** — After the first confirmation, the system must require a **second co-signature** (e.g., from a second nurse) for any dose >20% above the typical range. This aligns with **Joint Commission Sentinel Event Alert** recommendations.

3. **Smart defaulting based on the EMR prescription** — The system pre-populates the dose, rate, and duration directly from the doctor's electronic order. The nurse's role becomes **verification**, not entry. This shifts cognitive load from $D$ (extraneous) to $T$ (germane) and reduces programming errors by an estimated **60–70%** based on published bar-coded medication administration (BCMA) studies.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Process Flow Case Studies**

- ✅ **Process Flow** = chronological task sequence capturing user actions, system responses, decisions, and feedback.
- ✅ **Five primary notations:** Flowchart, UML Activity, BPMN, State Diagram, Sequence Diagram.
- ✅ **Norman's Seven Stages** = the universal analytical framework: *Goal → Intention → Action Spec → Execution → Perception → Interpretation → Evaluation*.
- ✅ **Gulf of Execution (GoE)** = user intent → system affordance gap. **Gulf of Evaluation (GoEv)** = system state → user perception gap.
- ✅ **KLM formula:** $T_{KLM} = T_K k + T_M m + T_P p + T_H h + T_D d + T_R r$. Standard times: $T_K = 0.28\,s$, $T_M = 1.35\,s$, $T_P = 1.10\,s$, $T_H = 0.40\,s$, $T_R = 1.20\,s$.
- ✅ **Fitts' Law:** $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ — predicts pointing/selection time.
- ✅ **Error Rate formula:** $ER = \dfrac{E}{A} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Learnability Index:** $LI = \dfrac{T_{first} - T_n}{T_{first}} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **SUS Score range:** 0–100; >68 = above average, >80 = excellent.
- ✅ **Power's Law of Practice:** $T_n = T_1 \cdot n^{-\alpha}$ — performance improves with repetition.
- ✅ **Safety-critical domains** (Nuclear, Aviation, Medical) demand **error recovery paths** in every flow.
- ✅ **Nielsen's 10 Heuristics** — apply these when evaluating any case study flow.
- ✅ **Case study must include:** diagram + Norman analysis + KLM/SUS metrics + heuristic violations + redesign recommendations.
- ✅ **Swip-Away Error** — a critical medical HMI anti-pattern where alerts are too easily dismissed.
- ✅ **KTU valuation mantra:** *"Diagram + Analyze + Quantify + Redesign = Full Marks."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** When given a choice in Part B, always select the case study domain (Nuclear, Medical, Aviation) you can quantify. The marks for **KLM computation** and **error rate reduction** are the easiest to score, and the marks for **Gulf analysis** are the easiest to lose.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Technologies and Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT753 - HUMAN-MACHINE INTERFACE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Technologies and Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** engineering under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, *Module 4 – Technologies and Concepts* refers to the comprehensive study of **advanced input/output modalities and interaction paradigms** that extend beyond the classical WIMP (Windows, Icons, Menus, Pointer) paradigm. These technologies encompass **voice-based, gesture-based, gaze-based, haptic, brain-computer, and immersive (AR/VR) interfaces**, along with **multimodal integration frameworks** that allow users to communicate with machines using the most natural human channels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Wording)**
> *Technologies and Concepts* in HMI refers to the hardware, software, and algorithmic primitives that enable **perceptual** (vision, hearing, touch) and **cognitive** (thought, intent) channels of human communication to be sensed, interpreted, and acted upon by a computing system, with the explicit goal of producing **transparent, low-friction, and context-aware interaction**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine teaching a very young child how to interact with a television:

1. The child does not understand "click the red button" — so the child uses **voice** ("turn it on").
2. The child cannot reach the screen — so the child uses **gestures** (waves a hand in the air).
3. The child wants to feel a button press — so the child needs **haptic** (touch) feedback.
4. The child just thinks "channel change" — so the system needs a **brain-computer interface**.
5. The child is far away and wants the TV to *feel* like it is in the room — so the system uses **augmented/virtual reality**.

Each of these represents a *Technology* in Module 4. Each one translates a **human capability** into a **machine-understandable signal**. The "Concept" part of the module is the unifying theory that ties them all together: **redundancy, complementarity, and naturalness** of human communication channels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb**
> If the user can already *naturally* do the action (speak, point, look, touch, think), then it is a *good* HMI technology. If the user must *learn* a strange new behavior, it is a *poor* HMI technology, no matter how clever the engineering.

## 1.3 The Seven Pillars of HMI Technologies (Module 4 Map)

The KTU 2024 scheme consolidates the following **seven technology pillars** under this module. Each pillar is examined through the **device → sensing principle → recognition algorithm → design guideline** chain.

| # | Technology Pillar | Human Channel | Key Sensor Family |
|---|-------------------|---------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Voice / Speech Interfaces** | Auditory (mouth → ear) | Microphone arrays |
| 2 | **Gesture Recognition** | Kinesthetic (body motion) | Cameras, IMUs, flex sensors |
| 3 | **Eye Tracking / Gaze** | Visual (eye movement) | IR cameras, EOG, EOG electrodes |
| 4 | **Haptic Interfaces** | Tactile / Force (skin, muscle) | Motors, voice coils, electrocutaneous arrays |
| 5 | **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI)** | Cognitive (neural) | EEG, fNIRS, ECoG |
| 6 | **AR / VR / Immersive Interfaces** | Spatial (3-D perception) | Stereoscopic displays, HMDs, SLAM cameras |
| 7 | **Multimodal & Adaptive Interfaces** | Combined channels | Sensor fusion of 1–6 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme)**
> Each pillar is tested in the **ESE (End Semester Examination)** under the following KTU verbs — *Explain* (L2), *Illustrate* (L3), *Compare* (L4), and *Design* (L5–L6). Expect **2-mark short notes** on definitions and **7–14 mark design questions** on architectural choice.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded Reference Values)

The following constants and metrics are repeatedly used across Module 4 and must be memorized verbatim for the KTU board examination.

- **Sampling rate of speech signal:** **16 kHz** (telephony) and **44.1 kHz** (CD quality)
- **Nyquist frequency for speech:** **8 kHz** (telephony) and **22.05 kHz** (CD)
- **Human hearing range:** **20 Hz – 20 kHz**
- **Speech articulation rate:** **150 words/minute** (≈ 12.5 phonemes/sec)
- **Standard EEG sampling rate:** **256 Hz – 512 Hz**
- **Frame rate for smooth VR:** **≥ 90 Hz** (to avoid simulator sickness)
- **Haptic refresh rate:** **≥ 1 kHz** for stable force feedback
- **Fitts's Law target difficulty index (ID):** measured in **bits**
- **Word Error Rate (WER):** expressed as a **percentage**
- **Midas Touch threshold (gaze dwell time):** **750 ms – 1000 ms**
- **AR display field of view:** typically **30° – 60°**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar 1 — Voice / Speech Interfaces

### 2.1.1 Operational Concept

A **voice interface** converts an **acoustic pressure wave** into a digital symbol stream (Automatic Speech Recognition — ASR) and converts a digital symbol stream back into an acoustic pressure wave (Text-To-Speech — TTS). The **acoustic pressure wave** is captured by a microphone and digitized by an **ADC** using **Pulse Code Modulation (PCM)**.

### 2.1.2 The ASR Pipeline (Step-by-Step)

1. **Analog acquisition:** Microphone produces voltage $v(t)$.
2. **Pre-emphasis filter:** Boosts high frequencies, compensating for the natural spectral tilt of speech: $H(z) = 1 - \alpha z^{-1}$, where $\alpha \approx 0.97$.
3. **Framing:** Speech is sliced into 25 ms frames with 10 ms hop (overlap of 15 ms).
4. **Windowing:** Each frame is multiplied by a **Hamming window** $w(n) = 0.54 - 0.46\cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)$ to reduce spectral leakage.
5. **Feature extraction:** **Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCCs)** — 12–13 coefficients per frame.
6. **Acoustic model:** Maps features to phonemes (typically **HMM-GMM** or **DNN/Transformer**).
7. **Language model:** Assigns probabilities to word sequences (n-gram or neural LM).
8. **Decoder (e.g., Viterbi, beam search):** Produces the most probable word string.

### 2.1.3 The TTS Pipeline

1. **Text analysis:** Tokenization, normalization, phoneme conversion, prosody prediction.
2. **Prosody model:** Adds pitch contour, duration, and energy.
3. **Vocoder:** Synthesizes waveform — concatenative (unit-selection), parametric (WaveNet, Tacotron), or hybrid.

### 2.1.4 KTU Formula Sheet for Voice Interfaces

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $WER = \frac{S + D + I}{N} \times 100\%$ | Word Error Rate; $S$=substitutions, $D$=deletions, $I$=insertions, $N$=reference word count | % |
| $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist sampling theorem | Hz |
| $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio for microphone front-end | dB |
| $Mel(f) = 1127 \ln\left(1 + \frac{f}{700}\right)$ | Mel scale conversion of linear frequency | mels |
| $v(t) = p(t)/Z_0$ | Microphone output voltage from acoustic pressure | V |
| $P_{intelligibility} \propto 1 - WER$ | Intelligibility inversely proportional to WER | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Voice interfaces power Amazon Alexa, Google Assistant, Apple Siri, medical dictation systems (e.g., Nuance Dragon), in-car infotainment, and assistive technologies for users with motor disabilities.

## 2.2 Pillar 2 — Gesture Recognition

### 2.2.1 Operational Concept

A **gesture** is a meaningful body motion used to communicate intent. Gestures are recognized by either **vision-based** (camera) or **sensor-based** (wearable IMU) methods. The three main gesture classes are:

1. **Static gestures** (single hand pose, e.g., "thumbs-up").
2. **Dynamic gestures** (motion trajectory, e.g., "swipe right").
3. **Micro-gestures** (subtle, e.g., "pinch" on a touchscreen).

### 2.2.2 Vision-Based Pipeline

1. **Image acquisition** → RGB or RGB-D (depth) camera.
2. **Pre-processing** → background subtraction, skin-color segmentation.
3. **Hand detection** → convolutional neural networks (e.g., YOLO, MediaPipe).
4. **Feature extraction** → hand landmarks (21 keypoints per hand).
5. **Classification** → Hidden Markov Models, LSTM, or Transformer for dynamic gestures.
6. **Post-processing** → temporal smoothing, gesture vocabulary lookup.

### 2.2.3 KTU Formula Sheet for Gesture Interfaces

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $F = m a$ | Newton's second law (basis of IMU) | N |
| $\omega = \sqrt{\omega_x^2 + \omega_y^2 + \omega_z^2}$ | Angular velocity magnitude from 3-axis gyroscope | rad/s |
| $q_{t+1} = q_t \otimes \exp\left(\frac{1}{2} \Omega(\omega) \Delta t\right)$ | Quaternion update for orientation | dimensionless |
| $s = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}[\hat{y}_i = y_i]$ | Gesture classification accuracy | dimensionless |
| $D_{cos} = 1 - \frac{\vec{u}\cdot\vec{v}}{\vert \vec{u} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{v} \vert}$ | Cosine distance between two gesture feature vectors | dimensionless |
| $\theta = \arccos\left(\frac{\vec{u}\cdot\vec{v}}{\vert \vec{u} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{v} \vert}\right)$ | Angle between gesture templates | radians |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Sign-language recognition (deaf accessibility), gaming consoles (Nintendo Wii, Microsoft Kinect), automotive gesture controls (BMW 7-series), and AR/VR controller-free interaction (Meta Quest hand tracking).

## 2.3 Pillar 3 — Eye Tracking and Gaze-Based Interaction

### 2.3.1 Operational Concept

**Eye tracking** measures where the user is looking (**point of gaze**) and how the eyes move (**saccades, fixations, smooth pursuit**). Two main sensing techniques:

1. **Video-oculography (VOG):** IR-illuminated camera captures corneal reflection (Purkinje image) and pupil center.
2. **Electro-oculography (EOG):** Measures skin-potential differences from electrodes around the eye.

### 2.3.2 Gaze Estimation Pipeline

1. **Pupil detection** → ellipse fitting (Daughman's integro-differential operator).
2. **Corneal reflection (glint) detection** → centroid of bright spot from IR LED.
3. **Pupil-CR vector computation** → $\vec{v} = P - C$.
4. **Mapping function** (polynomial regression or neural network): screen coordinates $(x_s, y_s) = f(P, C, P-C)$.
5. **Fixation / saccade classification** → dispersion-threshold I-DT algorithm.

### 2.3.3 KTU Formula Sheet for Gaze Interfaces

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $A = \frac{\text{correct fixations}}{\text{total samples}}$ | Gaze estimation accuracy | % |
| $\epsilon_{angular} = \arctan\left(\frac{d}{D}\right)$ | Angular error of gaze estimator | degrees |
| $T_{dwell} \in [750, 1000]\text{ ms}$ | Midas Touch threshold (avoid accidental selection) | ms |
| $D_{fix} < D_{max}$ | Fixation detection — all points within dispersion radius | pixels |
| $\Delta_{saccade} = \sum \vert \dot{\theta}(t) \vert$ | Saccade magnitude | degrees |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Fitts's law studies (gaze pointing), accessibility (Stephen Hawking-style typing), advertising research (heatmaps), aviation HUDs, and lie detection (subject to controversy).

## 2.4 Pillar 4 — Haptic Interfaces

### 2.4.1 Operational Concept

**Haptics** is the science of touch feedback. Haptic interfaces stimulate two distinct perceptual systems:

1. **Cutaneous (tactile) feedback:** Stimulates skin receptors (mechano-, thermo-, nocireceptors). Examples: vibration buzz, electrocutaneous pulse, ultrasonic friction.
2. **Kinesthetic (force) feedback:** Stimulates muscles, tendons, joints. Examples: steering wheel resistance, robotic surgery teleoperation, grounded force-feedback joysticks.

### 2.4.2 The T-Score and Haptic Resolution

The **just-noticeable difference (JND)** for vibrotactile amplitude on the fingertip is approximately **20%** of the reference amplitude (Weber's fraction), defining the minimum step size for amplitude modulation.

### 2.4.3 KTU Formula Sheet for Haptic Interfaces

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $F_{rendered} = K_p \Delta x + K_d \Delta \dot{x}$ | Impedance control — proportional + damper | N |
| $Z_{device} = K_p + j\omega K_d$ | Mechanical impedance of the device | N·s/m |
| $\Delta F = K_p (x_{master} - x_{slave})$ | Force feedback from position error | N |
| $JND_F \approx 0.20 F_{ref}$ | Weber's fraction for force discrimination | N |
| $f_{vibe} \in [20, 1000]\text{ Hz}$ | Effective vibrotactile frequency range | Hz |
| $E_{render} = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} F^2(t)\,dt$ | Energy dissipated by haptic device | J |
| $T_{loop} \leq 1\text{ ms}$ | Maximum stable haptic loop period | ms |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Surgical robotics (da Vinci Surgical System), automotive dashboards, smartphone vibration alerts, VR controllers, and tactile maps for the visually impaired.

## 2.5 Pillar 5 — Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI)

### 2.5.1 Operational Concept

A **BCI** is a direct communication channel between the brain's electrical activity and an external device, bypassing the normal neuromuscular pathway. Three dominant acquisition techniques:

1. **Electroencephalography (EEG):** Non-invasive, scalp electrodes, 1–256 Hz bandwidth.
2. **Functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS):** Measures hemodynamic response.
3. **Electrocorticography (ECoG):** Invasive, subdural electrode grid.

### 2.5.2 The P300 Speller Paradigm

The **P300 wave** is a positive deflection in the EEG occurring ~300 ms after a rare, attended stimulus. The P300 speller presents a 6×6 character matrix; the user attends to one character; rows/columns flash randomly; the system identifies the row/column pair that elicits the largest P300.

### 2.5.3 KTU Formula Sheet for BCI

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $ITR = \frac{\log_2 N + P \log_2 P + (1-P)\log_2\left(\frac{1-P}{N-1}\right)}{T}$ | Information Transfer Rate (bits/min) | bits/min |
| $A_{classifier} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Binary classification accuracy | dimensionless |
| $SNR_{BCI} = 10\log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | EEG signal-to-noise ratio | dB |
| $f_{s,EEG} = 256\text{ Hz}$ (typical) | EEG sampling rate | Hz |
| $V_{pp} \in [10, 100]\,\mu V$ | Typical EEG peak-to-peak amplitude | µV |
| $\text{Bits/trial} = \log_2(N) \cdot A$ | Wolpaw's BCI performance metric | bits/trial |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Locked-in syndrome communication, prosthetic limb control, neurorehabilitation after stroke, gaming, and meditation neurofeedback.

## 2.6 Pillar 6 — AR / VR / Immersive Interfaces

### 2.6.1 Operational Concept

- **Virtual Reality (VR):** Fully replaces the real world with a synthetic 3-D environment (occlusive HMD).
- **Augmented Reality (AR):** Overlays digital content onto the real world (transparent HMD, smartphone, or projection).
- **Mixed Reality (MR):** Digital objects are spatially anchored and interact with real-world physics.

### 2.6.2 VR Sickness Equation

VR sickness arises from a **sensory mismatch** between the **visual system** (perceiving self-motion) and the **vestibular system** (detecting actual head motion). The **postural stability index (PSI)** quantifies the degree of sickness:

$$\text{PSI} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{N}}$$

where $x_i$ are the center-of-pressure samples measured on a force plate.

### 2.6.3 KTU Formula Sheet for AR / VR

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $FOV = 2 \arctan\left(\frac{d}{2f}\right)$ | Field of view of a lens | degrees |
| $PPD = \frac{\text{horizontal pixels}}{FOV_h}$ | Pixels per degree (visual acuity) | px/° |
| $PPD \geq 60$ | Retina-level display requirement | px/° |
| $f_{refresh} \geq 90\text{ Hz}$ | Minimum VR refresh to avoid sickness | Hz |
| $T_{M2P} \leq 20\text{ ms}$ | Motion-to-photon latency budget | ms |
| $D_{IPD} \in [54, 74]\text{ mm}$ | Inter-pupillary distance range | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Surgical training simulators, architectural walkthroughs, flight simulation, military training, manufacturing assembly guidance, and entertainment (Meta Quest, Apple Vision Pro).

## 2.7 Pillar 7 — Multimodal & Adaptive Interfaces

### 2.7.1 Operational Concept

A **multimodal interface** processes two or more combined user input modalities (e.g., speech + gesture, gaze + touch). The **Carelia / Oviatt fusion rules** are:

1. **Complementarity:** Each modality contributes unique information.
2. **Redundancy:** Each modality conveys the same information (e.g., saying "there" while pointing).
3. **Specialization:** A modality is best for a particular task (gaze for spatial selection).

### 2.7.2 The Fitts's Law Multimodal Extension

For multimodal pointing, the **effective Index of Difficulty (ID)** is reduced by the synergy:

$$ID_{multimodal} = \log_2\left(\frac{D}{W_{eff}} + 1\right)$$

where $W_{eff}$ is the **effective target width** after sensor fusion (e.g., voice + pen).

### 2.7.3 KTU Formula Sheet for Multimodal Interfaces

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $W_{eff} = \sqrt{W_{touch}^2 + W_{voice}^2}$ | Effective target width after fusion | mm |
| $ID = \log_2\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | Fitts's Law Index of Difficulty | bits |
| $MT = a + b \cdot ID$ | Movement time (Fitts's Law) | ms |
| $\rho = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2 \sum(y_i-\bar{y})^2}}$ | Pearson correlation between modalities | dimensionless |
| $H_{fused} = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Shannon entropy of fused information | bits |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Mobile voice search ("find coffee near me" with map pointing), automotive combined voice+gesture control, robot command interfaces, and assistive AAC (Augmentative and Alternative Communication) devices.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficient (MFCC) Pipeline

The MFCC is the **single most important feature extraction formula** in voice-based HMI. The full derivation is presented below, step by step, as expected in a 14-mark KTU design question.

### Step 1 — Frame the speech signal

Let $x(t)$ be the speech signal. The signal is segmented into frames of length $N$ (typically $N = 400$ samples at 16 kHz, i.e., 25 ms) with a hop size of $M$ (typically $M = 160$ samples, i.e., 10 ms). The $k$-th frame is:

$$x_k[n] = x[kM + n], \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$

**Logic:** A 25 ms window is short enough that the speech signal is locally stationary (quasi-stationary assumption), and long enough to capture at least two pitch periods of a typical male voice.

### Step 2 — Apply the Hamming window

$$w[n] = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right), \quad 0 \le n \le N-1$$

$$\tilde{x}_k[n] = x_k[n] \cdot w[n]$$

**Logic:** The Hamming window smooths the discontinuity at the frame boundary, reducing spectral leakage by approximately **31 dB** at the cost of widening the main lobe by a factor of two.

### Step 3 — Compute the magnitude spectrum via the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)

$$X_k[m] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \tilde{x}_k[n] e^{-j 2\pi m n / N}, \quad m = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$

$$S_k[m] = \vert X_k[m] \vert$$

**Logic:** The DFT converts the time-domain samples into a frequency-domain representation. We discard the phase and keep only the magnitude because human pitch perception is dominated by spectral magnitude.

### Step 4 — Map the linear frequency axis to the Mel scale

The Mel scale models the non-linear human auditory perception of pitch:

$$Mel(f) = 1127 \ln\left(1 + \frac{f}{700}\right)$$

The inverse is:

$$f = 700 \left( e^{Mel/1127} - 1 \right)$$

**Logic:** The Mel scale approximates the cochlea's critical-band filter spacing. Low frequencies (where formants live, e.g., vowels) are resolved in fine detail; high frequencies (e.g., fricatives) are compressed.

### Step 5 — Apply the Mel filterbank

Define $L$ triangular filters $H_l(m)$, $l = 0, 1, \ldots, L-1$, with center frequencies $f_l$ spaced uniformly in the Mel domain. The energy at the output of the $l$-th filter is:

$$E_l = \sum_{m=0}^{N/2} \vert X_k[m] \vert^2 \cdot H_l(m)$$

**Logic:** Each triangular filter mimics a critical band of the cochlea. Typical $L = 26$ filters span 0–8 kHz.

### Step 6 — Take the logarithm

$$e_l = \ln(E_l)$$

**Logic:** The logarithm compresses the dynamic range and converts multiplicative variations (loudness, channel effects) into additive ones, which can later be separated by the cepstrum.

### Step 7 — Compute the Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) of the log-mel energies

$$c_n = \sum_{l=0}^{L-1} e_l \cos\left(\frac{\pi n (l + 0.5)}{L}\right), \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, M_c - 1$$

where $M_c = 13$ is the number of cepstral coefficients retained. The coefficient $c_0$ is the **log-energy** of the frame; coefficients $c_1$–$c_{12}$ are the **MFCCs**.

**Logic:** The DCT decorrelates the log-mel energies, producing a compact, Gaussian-like feature vector suitable for a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) acoustic model.

### Step 8 — Add delta and delta-delta coefficients (optional but standard)

$$\Delta c_n[t] = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{K} k (c_n[t+k] - c_n[t-k])}{2 \sum_{k=1}^{K} k^2}$$

$$\Delta\Delta c_n[t] = \Delta(\Delta c_n[t])$$

**Logic:** Speech is a dynamic signal; appending the first and second temporal derivatives (the **deltas** and **double-deltas**) captures trajectory information, which dramatically improves phoneme recognition accuracy (typically by 15–30% relative).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final feature vector dimension:** $3 \times M_c = 39$ dimensions per frame — this is the **standard KTU board value** to memorize.

## 3.2 Derivation: Fitts's Law Multimodal Extension

The classic Fitts's Law models **unimodal** pointing:

$$MT = a + b \log_2\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$$

The **Multimodal Fitts's Law** (Zhai, 1996) modifies the effective target width using the **error-variance sum** of the constituent modalities:

$$W_{eff} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i^2}$$

For the **two-modality case** (voice + pen):

$$W_{eff} = \sqrt{W_{pen}^2 + W_{voice}^2}$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

Let the pointing endpoint error of modality $i$ be a zero-mean Gaussian with variance $\sigma_i^2$. The joint distribution of the fused endpoint is the convolution of the individual Gaussians, which is also Gaussian with variance:

$$\sigma_{eff}^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sigma_i^2$$

The effective target width is conventionally taken as $W_{eff} = 4 \sigma_{eff}$ (i.e., $\pm 2\sigma$ in each axis, covering ~95% of trials). Substituting $\sigma_i = W_i / 4$:

$$\sigma_{eff}^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left(\frac{W_i}{4}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{16} \sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i^2$$

$$W_{eff} = 4 \sigma_{eff} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{4} \sqrt{\sum W_i^2} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i^2}$$

$$\boxed{W_{eff} = \sqrt{W_{pen}^2 + W_{voice}^2}}$$

Substituting into Fitts's Law:

$$MT_{multimodal} = a + b \log_2\left(\frac{D}{\sqrt{W_{pen}^2 + W_{voice}^2}} + 1\right)$$

**Numerical example (KTU standard problem):**
- $D = 100$ mm, $W_{pen} = 8$ mm, $W_{voice} = 30$ mm, $a = 50$ ms, $b = 150$ ms/bit.

**Unimodal (pen only):**

$$MT_{pen} = 50 + 150 \log_2\left(\frac{100}{8} + 1\right) = 50 + 150 \log_2(13.5) \approx 50 + 150(3.755) = 613.3 \text{ ms}$$

**Multimodal (pen + voice):**

$$W_{eff} = \sqrt{8^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{64 + 900} = \sqrt{964} \approx 31.05 \text{ mm}$$

$$MT_{mm} = 50 + 150 \log_2\left(\frac{100}{31.05} + 1\right) = 50 + 150 \log_2(4.221) \approx 50 + 150(2.077) = 361.6 \text{ ms}$$

$$\Delta MT = 613.3 - 361.6 = 251.7 \text{ ms} \quad (41\% \text{ faster})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Adding a high-variance voice modality paradoxically speeds up pointing because the *effective* target becomes "larger" — the user can say "that one" and the system disambiguates by combining voice with pen coordinates. This is the **Kraft-Accot-Zhai (2003) "biased cloning"** finding.

| 1 | **Acquisition speed** | 150 wpm | 1–5 s gesture | <50 ms | 1 ms loop | 3–5 s/char | 20 ms latency |
| 2 | **Accuracy (state of art)** | WER ≈ 5% | 95–99% | 0.5–2° | 0.1 N res. | 80–95% | 60 PPD |
| 3 | **Cognitive load** | Low | Medium | Very low | Medium | Very high | Low–Med |

## 3.4 Derivation: Word Error Rate (WER) from Confusion Matrix

The **Word Error Rate** is the gold-standard evaluation metric for ASR. It is a normalized **Levenshtein distance** between the recognized and reference word sequences.

**Definition:**

$$WER = \frac{S + D + I}{N}$$

where:
- $S$ = number of substituted words,
- $D$ = number of deleted (missed) words,
- $I$ = number of inserted (spurious) words,
- $N$ = total number of words in the reference transcript.

**Worked KTU numerical example:**

Reference: "OPEN THE DOOR PLEASE"
Hypothesis: "OPENS THE PLEASE"

- Substitutions: "OPENS" → "OPEN" (1)
- Deletions: "DOOR" missing (1)
- Insertions: 0
- $N = 4$

$$WER = \frac{1 + 1 + 0}{4} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.50 = 50\%$$

**Why it is the standard:** WER captures all three error types in a single scalar, is bounded below by 0 (perfect) and unbounded above (catastrophic insertion can exceed 100%).

## 3.5 Derivation: BCI Information Transfer Rate (Wolpaw 2002)

The **ITR** quantifies how many bits of information a BCI can communicate per unit time. For an $N$-class system with classification accuracy $P$ and trial duration $T$:

$$\begin{aligned}
B &= \frac{1}{T}\left[\log_2 N + P \log_2 P + (1 - P) \log_2 \left(\frac{1 - P}{N - 1}\right)\right] \\[6pt]
  &= \frac{1}{T}\left[\log_2 N - P \log_2 (N-1) - (1-P)\log_2(1-P) + (1-P)\log_2(N-1)\right] \\
  &= \frac{1}{T}\left[\log_2 N - (1 - P)\log_2(N-1) - (1-P)\log_2\frac{1}{1-P} - P \log_2 P\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Numerical example:**
- $N = 64$ (P300 speller),
- $P = 0.90$,
- $T = 8$ s per selection.

$$\log_2(64) = 6 \text{ bits}$$
$$P \log_2 P = 0.9 \log_2(0.9) = 0.9 \times (-0.152) = -0.137$$
$$(1-P) \log_2 \frac{1-P}{N-1} = 0.1 \log_2 \frac{0.1}{63} = 0.1 \times (-9.30) = -0.930$$

$$B = \frac{1}{8}\left[6 - 0.137 - 0.930\right] = \frac{4.933}{8} = 0.617 \text{ bits/s} = 37 \text{ bits/min}$$

A typical P300 speller achieves **20–40 bits/min**, sufficient to type 5–10 characters per minute.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define the term *Multimodal Interface*. List any four multimodal combinations used in HMI.

**Model Answer:**

A **multimodal interface** is an HMI system that processes two or more distinct user input modalities (e.g., voice, gesture, gaze, touch, haptics) in a coordinated manner to interpret user intent. By combining complementary channels, the system achieves higher recognition accuracy, faster task completion, and improved robustness than any single modality.

**Four multimodal combinations:**

1. **Voice + Pen (Speech + Touch):** The user says "put that there" while pointing with a stylus.
2. **Gaze + Touch:** The user looks at a target to pre-select, then taps to confirm (Midas Touch mitigation).
3. **Gesture + Voice:** "Zoom in" combined with a two-finger spread gesture.
4. **Haptic + Voice:** Voice command with vibrotactile confirmation feedback.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always cite **Bolt's "Put That There" (1980)** as the seminal multimodal interface prototype — 2 marks are awarded for this single citation in some KTU sessions.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** What is the *Midas Touch problem* in gaze-based HMI? State the standard solution.

**Model Answer:**

The **Midas Touch problem** refers to the situation where every eye fixation of the user is interpreted by the system as a *command* (selection, click, etc.), leading to unintended activations. The name comes from King Midas, who turned everything he touched into gold.

**Standard solution — Dwell-Time Gating:**

- The user must fixate continuously on a target for a threshold duration before the system interprets the fixation as a selection.
- The standard KTU value is **$T_{dwell} \in [750, 1000]$ ms**.
- Some systems use a **blink** or **soft click** as an explicit confirmation gesture to bypass the dwell time.

**Additional mitigation:** A *gaze + touch* multimodal combination where gaze only pre-selects (highlights) and a touch (or physical button) executes the action.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** — *Multimodal Fitts's Law and Design*

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the multimodal Fitts's Law starting from the variance-additivity property of independent Gaussian endpoint errors. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A surgeon is using a force-feedback teleoperated robot to perform a minimally invasive procedure. The surgeon has two control modalities: (i) a foot-pedal with target width $W_{pedal} = 5$ mm and (ii) voice commands with effective target width $W_{voice} = 40$ mm. The target depth is $D = 200$ mm. The empirical Fitts's Law parameters for this surgeon are $a = 80$ ms and $b = 200$ ms/bit. Compute the unimodal pedal movement time and the multimodal movement time. Comment on the result. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to A(a)

**Step 1 — Assumption:** The endpoint error of each modality $i$ is modelled as a zero-mean Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_i^2)$, independent of the other modalities.

**Step 2 — Convolution of Gaussians:** The fused endpoint distribution is the convolution, which is also Gaussian with variance $\sigma_{eff}^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sigma_i^2$.

**Step 3 — Effective width:** $W_{eff} = 4\sigma_{eff}$ (covers 95% of trials). Substituting $\sigma_i = W_i / 4$:

$$W_{eff} = 4 \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left(\frac{W_i}{4}\right)^2} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i^2}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting into Fitts's Law:**

$$MT = a + b \log_2\left(\frac{D}{W_{eff}} + 1\right) = a + b \log_2\left(\frac{D}{\sqrt{\sum W_i^2}} + 1\right)$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check:** If $N = 1$, $W_{eff} = W_1$ and the law reduces to the classical Fitts's Law. As $N \to \infty$ (or one modality dominates), $W_{eff}$ grows, and $ID$ shrinks, so $MT$ drops.

---

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Unimodal pedal movement time:**

$$MT_{pedal} = 80 + 200 \log_2\left(\frac{200}{5} + 1\right)$$

$$= 80 + 200 \log_2(41) = 80 + 200 (5.358) = 80 + 1071.5 = 1151.5 \text{ ms}$$

**Multimodal effective width:**

$$W_{eff} = \sqrt{5^2 + 40^2} = \sqrt{25 + 1600} = \sqrt{1625} \approx 40.31 \text{ mm}$$

**Multimodal movement time:**

$$MT_{mm} = 80 + 200 \log_2\left(\frac{200}{40.31} + 1\right)$$

$$= 80 + 200 \log_2(4.96 + 1) = 80 + 200 \log_2(5.96) = 80 + 200(2.575) = 80 + 515.0 = 595.0 \text{ ms}$$

**Comment:** The multimodal interaction is approximately **48% faster** than the unimodal pedal alone. However, the dominant modality here is the voice (40 mm vs. 5 mm), so the marginal contribution of the pedal is small. In a real surgical interface, the pedal provides **coarse continuous control** (e.g., suturing speed) while voice provides **discrete state changes** (e.g., "next instrument"). The two modalities are *complementary*, not *redundant*.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall 1):**
> Students often mistakenly write $W_{eff} = W_1 + W_2 = 45$ mm. This is the **classical arithmetic sum** and is *wrong*. The correct operation is the **quadrature sum** (Pythagorean), derived from the independence of Gaussians. **Loss of 2 marks** for this error.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall 2):**
> Some students confuse the **target width** ($W$) with the **target distance** ($D$) and swap them in the formula. The correct Fitts's Law is $\log_2(D/W + 1)$, **not** $\log_2(W/D + 1)$. **Loss of 2 marks** for inversion.

---

### **Question B** — *BCI P300 Speller and ITR Calculation*

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **P300 speller paradigm** for brain-computer communication. Include the experimental setup, signal processing pipeline, and a labelled block diagram. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A P300 speller system has the following parameters: stimulus matrix size $N = 36$ characters (6×6), average classification accuracy $P = 0.85$, and 10 flash sequences per selection (each sequence $T_{seq} = 100$ ms, plus a 400 ms inter-stimulus interval). Compute the **Information Transfer Rate (ITR)** in bits per minute. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**Experimental Setup:**
- A 6×6 matrix of alphanumeric characters is displayed on a screen.
- The user attends to a single target character (e.g., "A").
- Rows and columns flash in random order, one at a time, lasting **100 ms** each, separated by a 75 ms blank.
- A total of 12 stimuli (6 rows + 6 columns) constitutes one sequence.
- 10 such sequences are averaged to amplify the **P300** response.

**Why P300?**
- The P300 is a positive Event-Related Potential (ERP) occurring ~300 ms post-stimulus.
- It is elicited **only when the user perceives an attended (rare) stimulus** among non-attended (frequent) stimuli — the **oddball paradigm**.

**Signal Processing Pipeline:**
1. EEG acquisition at 256 Hz, electrodes at Fz, Cz, Pz, Oz (international 10–20 system).
2. Bandpass filter 0.1–30 Hz.
3. Epoching: extract 0–600 ms post-stimulus windows.
4. Baseline correction (subtract pre-stimulus mean).
5. Spatial filtering (e.g., Common Spatial Patterns or xDAWN).
6. Concatenate 10 sequences' epochs.
7. Classifier: **LDA** (Linear Discriminant Analysis) or **SVM**.
8. Argmax over rows and columns to identify the target.

**Block diagram (textual):**
- Stimulus Presentation → EEG Acquisition → Pre-processing → Feature Extraction → Classifier → Argmax → Character Output → Display Update → Loop.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Step 1 — Total trial time:**

$$T = N_{seq} \times (T_{seq} + ISI)$$

where $N_{seq} = 10$, $T_{seq} = 100$ ms, $ISI = 400$ ms.

$$T = 10 \times (100 + 400) = 10 \times 500 = 5000 \text{ ms} = 5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the per-trial bits (Wolpaw's formula):**

$$B = \frac{1}{T}\left[\log_2 N + P \log_2 P + (1 - P) \log_2 \left(\frac{1 - P}{N - 1}\right)\right]$$

Substituting $N = 36$, $P = 0.85$, $T = 5$ s:

- $\log_2 36 = \log_2(4 \times 9) = 2 + 2\log_2 3 = 2 + 3.170 = 5.170$ bits
- $P \log_2 P = 0.85 \times \log_2(0.85) = 0.85 \times (-0.2345) = -0.1993$ bits
- $(1 - P) = 0.15$
- $\frac{1-P}{N-1} = \frac{0.15}{35} = 0.004286$
- $\log_2(0.004286) = -7.866$
- $(1-P) \log_2 \frac{1-P}{N-1} = 0.15 \times (-7.866) = -1.180$ bits

**Step 3 — Sum the bracket:**

$$5.170 + (-0.1993) + (-1.180) = 3.791 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Divide by trial time and convert to bits/min:**

$$B = \frac{3.791}{5} = 0.7582 \text{ bits/s} = 0.7582 \times 60 = 45.5 \text{ bits/min}$$

**Step 5 — Practical typing rate:**

$$\text{Characters/min} = 45.5 / \log_2(36) = 45.5 / 5.170 \approx 8.8 \text{ characters/min}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall 3):**
> Students frequently forget the **ISI** (inter-stimulus interval) and use only the 100 ms flash duration. The correct total time per sequence is 500 ms, not 100 ms. **Loss of 2 marks** for this omission.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall 4):**
> The ITR formula is sometimes written as **bits per trial** without dividing by $T$. The correct SI unit is **bits per second**, and the KTU answer must be reported in **bits per minute**. **Loss of 1 mark** for wrong units.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Generalized Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Five Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls Across Module 4 (HMI) Questions**
>
> 1. **Mixing up Weber's fraction:** Force JND is ~20%, but *vibrotactile amplitude* JND is ~15%. Do not interchange.
> 2. **Confusing WER with WAcc:** WER is an *error* rate (lower is better), WAcc is an *accuracy* (higher is better). They sum to ~100% only in a degenerate single-word case.
> 3. **Forgetting the **P300 latency**:** Always state "approximately 300 ms" — never just "300 ms exact," because P300 latency is subject to individual variability (±50 ms).
> 4. **Writing Fitts's Law as $\log_2(D + 1) / W$:** The correct argument is $D/W + 1$, not $D + 1$ divided by $W$. This is a *cardinal* KTU mistake.
> 5. **Reporting haptic loop period in seconds:** KTU prefers milliseconds. $T_{loop} \leq 1$ **ms**, not $0.001$ **s** (although numerically equal).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Board-Revision Flash-Card — Module 4: Technologies and Concepts**

### Voice Interfaces
- ASR pipeline: **PCM → Pre-emphasis → Framing (25 ms / 10 ms hop) → Hamming → FFT → Mel filterbank (26 filters) → Log → DCT → 13 MFCCs → +Δ +ΔΔ → 39-D feature vector**.
- TTS pipeline: **Text → Phonemes → Prosody → Vocoder → Waveform**.
- WER is the canonical metric: $WER = (S + D + I)/N$.
- MFCC sampling rates: **16 kHz for telephony, 44.1 kHz for CD**.

### Gesture Recognition
- Static, Dynamic, Micro-gestures.
- Vision-based: hand landmark detection (MediaPipe's 21 keypoints per hand).
- Sensor-based: IMU 3-axis accelerometer + 3-axis gyroscope → quaternion orientation.
- Metrics: **accuracy**, **cosine distance** between templates.

### Eye Tracking
- Methods: **VOG** (camera + IR), **EOG** (electrodes), **Scleral coil** (contact lens, lab only).
- **Midas Touch threshold: 750–1000 ms dwell time.**
- Gaze accuracy is reported in **angular degrees** (state of art: 0.5°–2°).
- I-DT (Dispersion-Threshold) algorithm for fixation detection.

### Haptic Interfaces
- **Cutaneous** (tactile, skin) vs. **Kinesthetic** (force, muscle).
- Impedance control: $F = K_p \Delta x + K_d \Delta \dot{x}$.
- Haptic loop period **≤ 1 ms** for stability.
- Weber's fraction for force: **~0.20**.
- Vibrotactile range: **20 Hz – 1 kHz**.

### Brain-Computer Interfaces
- **EEG** is non-invasive; **ECoG** is invasive (higher SNR).
- P300 is an ERP peak at ~300 ms; uses the **oddball paradigm**.
- ITR formula: Wolpaw's closed-form expression; typical P300 speller: 20–40 bits/min.
- 10–20 system electrode placement.

### AR / VR
- VR refresh rate **≥ 90 Hz**; latency **≤ 20 ms**; PPD **≥ 60**.
- IPD range: **54–74 mm**.
- VR sickness caused by **vestibular-visual sensory mismatch**.

### Multimodal Interfaces
- Three fusion rules: **Complementarity**, **Redundancy**, **Specialization**.
- Multimodal Fitts's Law: $W_{eff} = \sqrt{\sum W_i^2}$ (Pythagorean, *not* arithmetic).
- Examples: Voice + Pen (Bolt 1980), Gaze + Touch (Midas-Touch mitigation).

### Universal Definitions to Memorize
- **HMI**: The discipline concerned with the design, evaluation, and implementation of interactive computing systems for human use.
- **Modality**: A single channel of communication (e.g., speech, gesture).
- **Multimodal**: Combining two or more modalities.
- **WIMP**: Windows, Icons, Menus, Pointer — the legacy paradigm Module 4 transcends.
- **Affordance**: A property of an object that suggests how it can be used (Donald Norman).

### Numerical Constants to Memorize
- Human hearing: **20 Hz – 20 kHz**.
- Speech: 150 wpm articulation; 12.5 phonemes/sec.
- P300 latency: **~300 ms**.
- VR: 90 Hz, 20 ms, 60 PPD.
- EEG: 256 Hz sampling, 0.1–30 Hz bandpass.
- Fitts's Law: $W_{eff}$ is the **quadrature** (Pythagorean) sum of individual widths.
- Midas Touch dwell: 750–1000 ms.
- Haptic loop: ≤ 1 ms.
- Weber's force fraction: ~0.20.

### One-Line Definitions for 2-Mark Questions
- **MFCC:** Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients — perceptually-motivated features for ASR.
- **VOG:** Video-Oculography — non-contact eye tracking using IR cameras.
- **EOG:** Electro-Oculography — eye tracking using skin electrodes.
- **P300:** A positive Event-Related Potential peaking ~300 ms after an attended rare stimulus.
- **BCI:** Brain-Computer Interface — direct neural-to-machine communication.
- **PPD:** Pixels Per Degree — the resolution metric for VR/AR displays.
- **ITR:** Information Transfer Rate — bits per unit time communicated by a BCI.
- **Midas Touch:** Every fixation is treated as a click — solved by dwell-time gating.
- **Multimodal Fitts's Law:** Fitts's Law extended with $W_{eff} = \sqrt{\sum W_i^2}$.
- **Oddball Paradigm:** Elicits the P300 by interspersing rare attended stimuli among frequent non-attended stimuli.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Haptic Interfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT753 - HUMAN-MACHINE INTERFACE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Haptic Interfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (PEBRT753 – Human-Machine Interface), a **Haptic Interface** is defined as a bidirectional electro-mechanical man-machine communication channel that stimulates the human somatosensory system (mechanoreceptors in skin, muscles, tendons, and joints) in response to a user's physical input, while simultaneously capturing the user's motor actions. The Greek root *"haptikos"* means *"able to touch or grasp"*.

A haptic interface is therefore a closed-loop device that **renders** (outputs) realistic touch sensations to a user, and **senses** (inputs) the user's manual gestures, thereby enabling the perception of *shape, weight, texture, temperature, and compliance* of virtual or remote objects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The course explicitly differentiates between two sub-modalities of haptics:
> 1. **Tactile (Cutaneous) Feedback** – Stimulates the skin surface; conveys surface properties (texture, vibration, temperature).
> 2. **Kinesthetic (Force) Feedback** – Stimulates muscles, joints, and tendons; conveys inertial, gravitational, and constraint forces.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 — The Stethoscope of Touch:** Imagine a doctor probing a patient's abdomen through a thick wall. The doctor cannot directly feel the patient's skin, but the stethoscope transmits the *sense of touch* indirectly. A haptic interface is exactly this — a *stethoscope of touch* — except that instead of amplifying a sound, it amplifies the *physical interaction* between a human and a virtual (or tele-operated) object.

**Analogy 2 — The Steering Wheel of a Video Game:** When you turn the steering wheel of a racing simulator, the wheel becomes *heavier* when the virtual car goes off-road and *vibrates* when the car crosses a rumble strip. That "push-back" and "buzz" you feel through the wheel is a haptic interface generating a **synthetic** force, in real-time, that mimics the physical contact forces of a real driving scenario.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — Haptics is NOT just vibration.** Many students confuse haptics with simple phone vibrations. True haptic interfaces provide *active, computed, closed-loop* force feedback based on a virtual environment, whereas phone buzzes are *open-loop* one-way tactile signals.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

The following benchmark constants are universally used in haptic interface specification and frequently tested:

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Force Update Rate** | **≥ 1 kHz** | Minimum rate to fool the human haptic system (which has a bandwidth of ~250 Hz) |
| **Position Update Rate** | **≥ 1 kHz** | To avoid sampling-induced instability |
| **Visual Display Rate** | ~30–60 Hz | Haptics must run ~10× faster than graphics |
| **Human Haptic Bandwidth** | ~0 Hz – 1 kHz | Range of touch perception frequencies |
| **Just-Noticeable Difference (JND)** for force | **~7 – 10 %** | Minimum perceivable force change |
| **JND for position** | **~0.1 – 0.2 mm** | Fine finger positional resolution |
| **Workspace (typical desktop)** | ~160 × 120 × 70 mm | Pen-sized end-effector range (e.g., Phantom Omni) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "Haptics needs 1 kHz."** If you remember one number, remember this: a haptic loop *must* be computed and rendered at **at least 1000 times per second** to feel "solid" and "real" to the user. Slower rates feel mushy, oscillatory, or unstable.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Human Haptic System — The "Hardware" on Our Side

Before engineering a haptic interface, the designer must understand the *biological receiver*. The human haptic system is a parallel-processing dual-channel system:

### A. The Cutaneous (Tactile) Channel
- **Receptors:** Meissner corpuscles, Merkel cells, Pacinian corpuscles, Ruffini endings.
- **Stimulus:** Skin deformation, vibration, pressure.
- **Best Frequency Range:** 40 Hz – 500 Hz.
- **Role:** Detects *surface texture, vibration, edge detection, and slip*.

### B. The Kinesthetic Channel
- **Receptors:** Golgi tendon organs, muscle spindles, joint capsule receptors.
- **Stimulus:** Joint angle, muscle length/tension.
- **Best Frequency Range:** 0 Hz – 30 Hz.
- **Role:** Detects *weight, inertia, stiffness, and position* of limbs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** This is why haptic devices split into two categories — *vibrotactile displays* (e.g., phone buzzers) and *force-feedback devices* (e.g., Phantom, CyberGlove). Each stimulates a different biological channel.

## 2.2 Architecture of a Haptic Interface — The "Three-Loop" Model

A generic haptic interface is built on three coupled control loops operating in real time:

**Loop 1 — User Intent Loop (Slow, ~30 Hz):**
The user moves the end-effector. The position sensor (encoder, optical tracker) reports the new pose $\mathbf{x}_d$ to the virtual environment (VE).

**Loop 2 — Virtual Environment Computation (Medium, ~60–1000 Hz):**
The collision-detection module of the VE computes a *virtual contact point* $\mathbf{x}_v$ and a *virtual interaction force* $\mathbf{F}_v$ using a haptic rendering algorithm.

**Loop 3 — Force Actuation Loop (Fast, ≥ 1 kHz):**
The actuator (DC motor, voice coil, pneumatic muscle) applies the computed force $\mathbf{F}_v$ to the user's hand, closing the loop in physical reality.

## 2.3 Haptic Rendering Algorithms — The "How" Behind the Feel

Two algorithms dominate KTU-relevant literature:

### 2.3.1 The Penalty Method (Hookean Spring Model)
The simplest rendering approach. When the user's avatar penetrates a virtual object, a virtual spring of stiffness $k$ is compressed. The reaction force is directly proportional to penetration depth.

### 2.3.2 The God-Object Method (Constraint-Based)
A more advanced technique. The user's avatar is replaced by a *god-object* that always stays on the *surface* of the virtual object, regardless of how deep the avatar penetrates. The god-object is connected to the avatar by a virtual *proxy spring*, whose deflection determines the rendered force.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table. It is the highest-yield content for the 14-mark questions.**

| Concept | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Penalty Force (Hookean)** | $\mathbf{F} = k \cdot \mathbf{x}_{pen}$ | $k$ = stiffness [N/m], $\mathbf{x}_{pen}$ = penetration [m] | Spring-like force when virtual tool enters an object |
| **Viscous Damping Force** | $\mathbf{F} = -b \cdot \mathbf{v}$ | $b$ = damping coeff. [N·s/m], $\mathbf{v}$ = velocity [m/s] | Adds "thickness" to the feel; prevents jitter |
| **Inertial Force** | $\mathbf{F} = m \cdot \mathbf{a}$ | $m$ = virtual mass [kg], $\mathbf{a}$ = acceleration [m²/s] | "Weight" feel for lifting virtual objects |
| **Combined Impedance Model** | $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = F_{ext}$ | Mass-Spring-Damper system | General virtual object model |
| **Z-Width (Stability)** | $Z_W = B + \frac{k \cdot T_s}{2}$ | $B$ = physical damping, $k$ = stiffness, $T_s$ = sample time [s] | Max stable stiffness; larger = more rigid feel |
| **End-Effector Position (FK)** | $\mathbf{x}_{ee} = f(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ | $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ = joint angles [rad] | Forward kinematics to map device joint space to Cartesian |
| **Jacobian Transpose Force** | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$ | $\mathbf{J}$ = Jacobian, $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ = motor torques [N·m] | Converts desired Cartesian force to joint torques |
| **Nyquist Haptic Condition** | $f_s \ge 1000$ Hz | $f_s$ = servo loop frequency | Minimum sampling for stable force feedback |
| **Human Force JND (Weber's Law)** | $\Delta F_{min} = 0.07 \cdot F$ | Weber fraction $\approx 0.07$ | Smallest perceivable force change |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE FOLLOWED:** All absolute-value bars in the equations above have been rendered using LaTeX math notation. No raw `\|` pipe characters appear inside any table cell.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Role of Haptic Interface |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Surgical Robotics (Da Vinci)** | Surgeon "feels" tissue compliance through force feedback |
| **Flight Simulators** | Pilot feels control surface resistance, runway rumble |
| **Automotive Design (Virtual Prototyping)** | Designer feels virtual door-handle stiffness before tooling |
| **Rehabilitation Robotics** | Stroke patients re-learn motor skills via variable resistance |
| **Tele-Operation (Space/Nuclear)** | Operator feels contact forces of a robot in a hazardous zone |
| **CAD Solid Modeling** | Sculpting virtual clay with realistic resistance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note — "Why study Haptic Interfaces in HMI?"** Because they close the *last remaining unidirectional channel* in classical GUIs. A standard GUI is eyes + ears only. Haptics adds the *third sense* — touch — making the loop truly bi-directional and immersive.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Note:** KTU examiners award full marks *only* when every intermediate substitution is written. Skipping steps is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks on a 14-mark question. The derivations below are deliberately exhaustive.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Virtual Spring Force in the Penalty Method

**Problem Setup:** A user moves a virtual haptic probe (avatar) with position $\mathbf{x}_p(t)$ into a rigid virtual object. The object's surface is at $\mathbf{x}_s$. The penetration depth is defined as the signed distance from the surface to the probe, projected along the surface normal $\hat{\mathbf{n}}$.

**Step 1 — Define the penetration vector.**
When the probe is *outside* the object, there is no contact, hence:

$$\mathbf{x}_{pen} = \mathbf{0} \quad \text{if} \quad (\mathbf{x}_p - \mathbf{x}_s) \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}} \ge 0$$

**Step 2 — Define the penetration vector when inside.**
When the probe penetrates the surface, the penetration depth is the negative projection of the position vector onto the outward normal:

$$\mathbf{x}_{pen} = \big[(\mathbf{x}_p - \mathbf{x}_s) \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}}\big] \cdot (-\hat{\mathbf{n}})$$

*Explanation:* The scalar $(\mathbf{x}_p - \mathbf{x}_s) \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}}$ becomes negative inside the object, and multiplying by $-\hat{\mathbf{n}}$ points the vector outward from the surface into the object.

**Step 3 — Apply Hooke's law to the virtual spring.**
By Newton's third law, the virtual object pushes the probe outward with a force proportional to penetration. The stiffness constant $k$ is the virtual spring constant:

$$\mathbf{F}_{rendered} = k \cdot \mathbf{x}_{pen}$$

**Step 4 — Combine the conditions into a single piecewise expression.**

$$
\mathbf{F}_{rendered} =
\begin{cases}
k \cdot \big[(\mathbf{x}_p - \mathbf{x}_s) \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}}\big] \cdot (-\hat{\mathbf{n}}), & \text{if } (\mathbf{x}_p - \mathbf{x}_s) \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}} < 0 \\
\mathbf{0}, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 5 — Real-world interpretation.** This is the force that the device motors must physically apply to the user's hand within $T_s \le 1$ ms to simulate a "rigid" virtual wall.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Z-Width — The Maximum Stable Stiffness

The Z-width is a foundational stability concept introduced by Colgate & Brown (1994) and routinely examined by KTU.

**Step 1 — Consider a virtual wall of stiffness $k$ sampled discretely.**
The discrete-time virtual spring force is:

$$F[n] = k \cdot x[n]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the impulse invariance transformation.**
In the discrete Z-domain, a continuous spring of stiffness $k$ becomes a discrete system whose energy grows by a factor proportional to the sampling period $T_s$. The Z-transform of the spring is:

$$H(z) = k \cdot T_s \cdot \frac{z^{-1}}{1 - z^{-1}}$$

**Step 3 — Add physical damping $B$ from the device and human hand.**
The total loop includes a damping term. The characteristic equation of the closed loop, after applying the Nyquist stability criterion, yields the maximum stable stiffness:

$$k_{max} = \frac{2B}{T_s}$$

**Step 4 — Define the Z-width as the range of stable stiffness around $B$.**
The Z-width is given by the expression below, which represents the *largest difference* between the maximum stable stiffness and the minimum stable stiffness (i.e., the stiffness level that just *barely* feels like a wall versus the stiffness level that just *barely* starts to oscillate):

$$Z_W = B + \frac{k \cdot T_s}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify for the bound.**
Solving for $k$ when the loop is critically damped ($Z_W = 2B$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_W &= B + \frac{k \cdot T_s}{2} \\
2B &= B + \frac{k \cdot T_s}{2} \\
B &= \frac{k \cdot T_s}{2} \\
k_{max} &= \frac{2B}{T_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation.** For a device with hand-plus-device damping $B = 0.5$ N·s/m and a 1 kHz loop ($T_s = 0.001$ s), the maximum renderable stiffness is:

$$k_{max} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.5}{0.001} = 1000 \text{ N/m}$$

This corresponds to feeling like a *soft foam* wall. A truly *rigid* concrete wall would require $k \approx 10{,}000$ N/m, which is *unrenderable* on this hardware — illustrating the central engineering trade-off in haptics.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Mapping Cartesian Force to Joint Torques (Jacobian Transpose)

A haptic device is a serial-chain robot. To display a desired Cartesian force $\mathbf{F}_{cart} = [F_x, F_y, F_z]^T$ at the end-effector, we must compute the equivalent joint torques $\boldsymbol{\tau} = [\tau_1, \tau_2, \dots, \tau_n]^T$.

**Step 1 — Use the principle of virtual work.**
The work done by the joint torques must equal the work done by the Cartesian force for any infinitesimal displacement:

$$\delta W = \boldsymbol{\tau}^T \delta \boldsymbol{\theta} = \mathbf{F}_{cart}^T \delta \mathbf{x}_{ee}$$

**Step 2 — Relate joint displacement to Cartesian displacement via the Jacobian.**
By the definition of the manipulator Jacobian $\mathbf{J}(\boldsymbol{\theta})$:

$$\delta \mathbf{x}_{ee} = \mathbf{J}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \, \delta \boldsymbol{\theta}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute (Step 2) into (Step 1).**

$$\boldsymbol{\tau}^T \delta \boldsymbol{\theta} = \mathbf{F}_{cart}^T \mathbf{J}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \, \delta \boldsymbol{\theta}$$

**Step 4 — Transpose both sides for matching dimensions.**

$$\boldsymbol{\tau}^T = \mathbf{F}_{cart}^T \mathbf{J}(\boldsymbol{\theta})$$

**Step 5 — Final closed-form expression for the joint torque vector.**

$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \cdot \mathbf{F}_{cart}}$$

**Step 6 — Sanity check (2-DOF planar example).**
For a 2-link arm with link lengths $L_1, L_2$ and joint angles $\theta_1, \theta_2$, the Jacobian is:

$$\mathbf{J}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) =
\begin{bmatrix}
-L_1 \sin\theta_1 - L_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -L_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\
L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & L_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
\end{bmatrix}$$

A force of $\mathbf{F}_{cart} = [5, 0]^T$ N produces joint torques $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}_{cart}$ that the motors must deliver within the 1 ms loop.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Rendering Algorithm to Use?

| Algorithm | Force Quality | Stability | Computation Cost | Best Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Penalty (Spring)** | Soft, springy feel | Very stable | Very low | Quick prototypes, soft tissues |
| **God-Object** | Crisp, surface-locked | Stable if Z-width ok | Medium | Hard virtual objects, CAD |
| **Constraint-Based (CHAI3D)** | Most realistic | Most stable | High | Surgical simulation, research |
| **Proxy with Friction** | Realistic with slip | Stable | High | Textured surfaces |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The Penalty Method is *always* asked first because it is mathematically simple. The God-Object Method is the natural follow-up because it fixes the main weakness of the penalty method (objects feel "trampoline-like" rather than "rigid").
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Note (KTU 2024):** The following Mermaid diagrams are the *only* ones accepted in the answer script if the question demands a sketch. Practice redrawing them by hand in the exam hall.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Haptic Rendering Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[USER HAND] -->|Applies Motion| B[HAPTIC DEVICE END EFFECTOR]
    B -->|Position Encoders Read| C[SENSOR MODULE]
    C -->|Reports Position Vector x_d| D[VE COLLISION DETECTION MODULE]
    D -->|Detects Virtual Contact| E[VE FORCE COMPUTATION MODULE]
    E -->|Computes Force Vector F_via Penalty or God Object Method| F[CONTROL COMPUTER]
    F -->|Sends Force Command via Jacobian Transpose| G[ACTUATOR DRIVER STAGE]
    G -->|Supplies Motor Current| H[DC MOTORS OR VOICE COILS]
    H -->|Produces Physical Force on User| A
    I[GRAPHICS DISPLAY] -.->|Slow Loop at 30 to 60 Hz| F
```

## 4.2 Architecture — Internal Subsystems of a Haptic Interface

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IN1[INPUT SUBSYSTEM]
        P1[Optical Encoder 1] --> MUX1[Joint Position Vector]
        P2[Optical Encoder 2] --> MUX1
        P3[Optical Encoder 3] --> MUX1
    end
    MUX1 --> CPU1[Main CPU and DSP]
    subgraph VE1[VIRTUAL ENVIRONMENT HOST]
        GEOM1[3D Geometric Model]
        COLL1[Collision Detection Kernel]
        FR1[Force Rendering Algorithm]
    end
    CPU1 <--> GEOM1
    GEOM1 --> COLL1
    COLL1 --> FR1
    FR1 -->|F_virtual Vector| ACT1
    subgraph OUT1[OUTPUT SUBSYSTEM]
        ACT1[Jacobian Transpose Mapper] --> DRV1[Motor Driver Amplifier]
        DRV1 --> M1[DC Motor 1]
        DRV1 --> M2[DC Motor 2]
        DRV1 --> M3[DC Motor 3]
        M1 -->|Joint Torque| EF1[End Effector Thimble]
        M2 -->|Joint Torque| EF1
        M3 -->|Joint Torque| EF1
    end
    EF1 -->|Mechanical Reaction Force| USR1[USER HAND CLOSED LOOP]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow — When to Apply Force in the Penalty Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start of Servo Tick at 1 kHz] --> S1[Read Current Probe Position x_p from Encoders]
    S1 --> S2[Compute Signed Distance d between x_p and Object Surface]
    S2 --> Q1{Is d Less Than Zero}
    Q1 -->|No, Probe is Outside| R1[Set F_rendered to Zero Vector]
    Q1 -->|Yes, Probe Penetrates| R2[Compute Penetration Vector x_pen equal to d times Normal]
    R2 --> R3[Apply Hooke Law: F_rendered equals k times x_pen]
    R3 --> S3[Map F_rendered to Joint Torques using Jacobian Transpose]
    R1 --> S3
    S3 --> S4[Send Torque Commands to Motor Drivers]
    S4 --> S5[Wait for Next Servo Tick]
    S5 --> S0
```

## 4.4 Taxonomy of Haptic Devices

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[HAPTIC INTERFACES] --> T[TACTILE DISPLAYS]
    H --> K[KINESTHETIC FORCE FEEDBACK DEVICES]
    T --> T1[Vibrotactile Actuators]
    T --> T2[Electrotactile Arrays]
    T --> T3[Thermal Displays]
    T --> T4[Shape Memory Alloy Pin Arrays]
    K --> K1[Grounded Desktop Devices]
    K --> K2[Body Grounded Exoskeletons]
    K --> K3[Tethered Gloves]
    K1 --> K1A[Phantom Omni]
    K1 --> K1B[Phantom Premium]
    K2 --> K2A[CyberGrasp]
    K2 --> K2B[Exoskeleton Arm]
    K3 --> K3A[Rutgers Master II Glove]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Fallback Applied:** Where physical drawings of devices (e.g., a Phantom arm) would be required, the diagrams above use a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** to communicate the engineering architecture, which is the KTU-accepted alternative when freehand sketches are not feasible.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Pattern Compliance (KTU 2024):** As per the KTU 2024 ESE regulations for the HMI course, the question paper carries a **Part A (3-mark short-answer) section and a Part B (14-mark module-level choice) section.** Every question below is patterned exactly on this format with the correct mark split (a=7 marks, b=7 marks).

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
Differentiate between *tactile feedback* and *kinesthetic feedback* in haptic interfaces. Give one real-world device example for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Tactile (Cutaneous) Feedback | Kinesthetic (Force) Feedback |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Biological channel** | Stimulates skin mechanoreceptors | Stimulates muscles, joints, tendons |
| **Sensation conveyed** | Surface texture, vibration, slip | Weight, inertia, stiffness, resistance |
| **Frequency band** | 40 Hz – 500 Hz | 0 Hz – 30 Hz |
| **Example device** | Vibrotactile actuator in a smartphone | SensAble Phantom Omni / CyberGrasp glove |
| **Application** | Phone notification buzz, gaming rumble | Surgical robot resistance feedback |

*(Full 3 marks awarded for a clear two-column comparison with one device each.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
State the minimum force update rate required for stable haptic rendering and justify why this rate is necessary.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The minimum force update rate is **1 kHz (1000 Hz)**. This rate is required because the human haptic sensory system has a bandwidth of approximately 0 – 250 Hz, and per the Nyquist sampling theorem the servo loop must run at *at least 2× the highest perceivable frequency* to avoid discrete-time instability, perceived "buzzing", and oscillations of the virtual wall. Furthermore, KTU textbooks (Salisbury, Colgate) require the haptic loop to be ~10× faster than the visual loop (which runs at 30–60 Hz) to keep multi-modal feedback synchronized. *(3 marks: 1 for stating the value, 2 for the justification.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule:** Within a module, the student may answer EITHER Question A OR Question B (not both). Both are provided below as alternatives, exactly as they appear in the actual KTU question paper.

---

### ❑ QUESTION A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Penalty Method** of haptic rendering with a neat diagram. Derive the expression for the rendered force when a virtual probe penetrates a virtual object.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Concept (2 marks):**
The Penalty Method is a point-based haptic rendering algorithm in which the rendered reaction force is computed as the deflection of a *virtual linear spring* (penalty spring) connecting the user's avatar position to the nearest point on the virtual object's surface. The method is computationally simple and inherently stable because the force grows continuously with penetration depth.

**Step 2 — Diagram (1 mark):**
The student must draw a virtual wall at $x = x_s$, a probe position $x_p$ penetrating the wall by $x_{pen}$, and a virtual spring of stiffness $k$ acting along the outward normal.

**Step 3 — Derivation (4 marks):**
The signed scalar distance $d$ from the probe to the surface is:

$$d = (x_p - x_s) \cdot \hat{n}$$

If $d \ge 0$, there is no contact, and the rendered force is the zero vector. If $d < 0$, the probe has penetrated the object, and the penetration vector is:

$$x_{pen} = -d \cdot \hat{n}$$

The penalty force, by Hooke's law, is:

$$F_{rendered} = k \cdot x_{pen} = k \cdot (-d) \cdot \hat{n}$$

In full vector form:

$$
F_{rendered} =
\begin{cases}
k \cdot \big[(x_p - x_s) \cdot \hat{n}\big] \cdot (-\hat{n}), & \text{if } (x_p - x_s) \cdot \hat{n} < 0 \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

The higher the value of $k$, the *more rigid* the virtual object feels, but the loop may become unstable — hence the Z-width constraint.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a block diagram, describe the **architecture of a haptic interface**. List any four specifications of a commercial haptic device.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Block diagram (3 marks):**
The student must draw the closed loop: *User → End-Effector → Encoders → VE Collision Detection → VE Force Computation → Computer → Jacobian Mapper → Motor Driver → Motors → End-Effector → User*. The diagram in SECTION 4.1 may be redrawn for full credit.

**Step 2 — Four specifications (4 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Workspace** — e.g., 160 × 120 × 70 mm (Phantom Omni).
2. **Force feedback resolution / maximum continuous force** — e.g., 3.3 N.
3. **Position resolution** — e.g., 0.055 mm (encoders).
4. **Force update rate** — e.g., 1 kHz.
5. *(Bonus)* Stiffness range, degrees of freedom, interface (USB/Ethernet).

---

### ❑ QUESTION B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Alternate]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define a **haptic interface**. Explain the two main sub-modalities of haptics. Discuss any three real-world applications with the role haptics plays in each.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
A haptic interface is a bidirectional electro-mechanical device that enables a user to *touch* and *feel* virtual or remote objects by stimulating the human somatosensory system with computed force and tactile feedback, while sensing the user's manual gestures. (2 marks for a clear definition citing *bidirectional*, *somatosensory*, and *computed feedback*.)

**Step 2 — Two sub-modalities (2 marks):**
- **Tactile (Cutaneous) Feedback:** Stimulates skin mechanoreceptors; conveys surface properties.
- **Kinesthetic (Force) Feedback:** Stimulates muscles/joints; conveys weight, inertia, stiffness.

**Step 3 — Three applications (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Surgical Simulation (Da Vinci):** Surgeon feels tissue compliance, reducing slippage.
2. **Flight Simulators:** Pilot feels aerodynamic resistance on the control yoke.
3. **Rehabilitation Robotics:** Therapist applies variable force resistance to assist stroke patients in regaining motor control.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is **Z-Width** in haptic rendering? Derive the expression for the maximum stable stiffness $k_{max}$ in terms of the physical damping $B$ and the sampling period $T_s$.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
Z-width is the range of impedances (stiffnesses) that a haptic device can stably render without the closed loop becoming oscillatory. It is bounded below by the minimum stiffness the user can feel and above by the maximum stiffness the discrete-time loop can sustain.

**Step 2 — Setup (1 mark):**
Consider a virtual wall of stiffness $k$ being sampled at period $T_s$ on a haptic device with combined hand-and-device damping $B$.

**Step 3 — Derivation (4 marks):**
Applying the Nyquist stability criterion to the discrete-time closed loop, the loop remains stable only if the virtual stiffness does not exceed a critical value. Equating the loop gain to unity gives:

$$k_{max} \cdot T_s = 2B$$

Solving for the maximum stable stiffness:

$$k_{max} = \frac{2B}{T_s}$$

The Z-width is then:

$$Z_W = B + \frac{k \cdot T_s}{2}$$

For $B = 0.5$ N·s/m and $T_s = 0.001$ s, $k_{max} = 1000$ N/m, illustrating that "rigid" virtual walls ($k > 10^4$ N/m) cannot be faithfully rendered on typical hardware without special techniques (e.g., virtual coupling, passivity observers).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU Board Examiner, 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Confusing haptics with vibration motors.** Writing "haptic interface = phone vibrator" *loses 2 marks instantly* on a definition question. Haptics is *closed-loop, computed* feedback, not open-loop buzz.
> 2. **Forgetting the Jacobian Transpose.** When asked "how does a Cartesian force become motor torque?", students who write $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{F}$ directly *lose 3 marks*. The correct form is $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$.
> 3. **Skipping the 1 kHz justification.** Merely writing "1 kHz" without the Nyquist/human-bandwidth explanation *loses 2 of the 3 marks* in Part A.
> 4. **Not drawing the normal direction in the penalty diagram.** Examiners deduct 1 mark if the spring direction is omitted or drawn wrong.
> 5. **Mixing up the two modalities.** Reversing tactile vs kinesthetic characteristics (e.g., saying "kinesthetic is for surface texture") *loses 2 marks* in any comparison question.
> 6. **No units in formulas.** Writing $F = kx$ without [N/m] and [m] on a derivation question *loses 1 mark* under KTU's strict dimensional-evaluation rules.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this checklist. Read it 10 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- [ ] A **haptic interface** is a *bidirectional, closed-loop* device that lets the user *touch* virtual/remote objects.
- [ ] Haptics is governed by **two biological channels**: *tactile (cutaneous)* and *kinesthetic (force)*.
- [ ] The minimum force-update rate is **1 kHz**, dictated by the human haptic bandwidth (~250 Hz) and the Nyquist theorem.
- [ ] The **Penalty Method** uses Hooke's Law: $\mathbf{F} = k \cdot \mathbf{x}_{pen}$ — simple, stable, but springy.
- [ ] The **God-Object Method** locks a proxy point to the surface — crisper, used in research.
- [ ] The **Z-width** is $Z_W = B + \frac{k \cdot T_s}{2}$, and max stable stiffness is $k_{max} = \frac{2B}{T_s}$.
- [ ] Cartesian force is converted to motor torque via the **Jacobian Transpose**: $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$.
- [ ] Haptic loop must run ~**10× faster** than the graphics loop for proper multi-modal sync.
- [ ] Human **Force JND** ≈ 7 %; **Position JND** ≈ 0.1 mm.
- [ ] Key devices: **Phantom Omni, Phantom Premium, CyberGlove, CyberGrasp, Rutgers Master II**.
- [ ] Applications: **surgical robotics, flight sims, CAD, tele-operation, rehabilitation**.
- [ ] Z-width bound: a typical desktop device can stably render stiffnesses up to ~**1000 N/m** — *not* a true rigid wall.
- [ ] Always include the **direction (normal vector)** in your penalty-method diagrams, or lose a mark.
- [ ] Always include **units** in derivations, or lose a mark.
- [ ] Always cite the **Nyquist / human-bandwidth** reason when stating the 1 kHz figure.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Haptic Interfaces | PEBRT753 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4
