## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Manipulation Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Layer]
        Mouse[Mouse HID]
        Touch[Touch HID]
        Stylus[Stylus HID]
        VR[VR Controller 6DOF]
    end
    subgraph TranslationLayer[Translation and Mapping Layer]
        Filter[Kalman or Double Exponential Smoother]
        Gain[CD Ratio and Acceleration Curve]
        Snap[Constraint Solver Grid and Snap]
    end
    subgraph ObjectLayer[Object State Layer]
        Sel[Selection Manager]
        Trans[Transform Node]
        Prop[Property Store]
    end
    subgraph FeedbackLayer[Feedback Layer]
        Vis[Visual Signifier]
        Aud[Audio Cue]
        Hap[Haptic Vibration]
    end
    Mouse --> Filter
    Touch --> Filter
    Stylus --> Filter
    VR --> Filter
    Filter --> Gain --> Snap --> Sel
    Sel --> Trans
    Sel --> Prop
    Trans --> Vis
    Trans --> Aud
    Trans --> Hap
    Trans -->|Render Update| Disp[Display 60 to 144 Hz]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define direct manipulation. List any four of Shneiderman's principles of direct manipulation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Direct manipulation is an HMI style in which the user acts continuously on a visible object and sees the result of every action without an intermediate command layer. Four principles (1.5 marks for definition, 1.5 marks for principles — any 4 of 5 acceptable):

1. **Continuous representation** of the object of interest.
2. **Physical actions** (point, click, drag) replace typed syntax.
3. **Rapid, incremental, reversible** operations.
4. **Immediate feedback** on every action.
5. **Easy error correction** by pointing to a safe state.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State Fitts's Law. What do the parameters $A$ and $W$ represent?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Fitts's Law (1954) predicts the time $T$ required to acquire a target (2 marks):

$$T = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{A}{W} + 1\right)$$

- $A$ — **Amplitude**, i.e., the distance from the starting position to the centre of the target (0.5 mark).
- $W$ — **Width** of the target along the axis of motion (0.5 mark).

The logarithmic term is the **Index of Difficulty** in bits.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module-Internal-Choice Questions

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B carries 14 marks and offers a choice between two full questions. Each question has sub-parts **(a) for 7 marks** and **(b) for 7 marks**.

---

### 📘 Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4 Choice 1]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **closed-loop manipulation pipeline** in HMI. Explain the role of *signifiers* and *feedback* in this loop. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A user is asked to click on rectangular buttons of width 4 cm located 20 cm away. Using Fitts's Law with $a = 0.105$ s and $b = 0.103$ s/bit, compute **(i)** the Index of Difficulty, **(ii)** the predicted Movement Time, and **(iii)** the throughput if the observed time is 0.65 s. Comment on whether the device performs within the standard range. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Solution to (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Block diagram (3 marks):** Draw a six-stage pipeline: *User Intention → Motor Command → Physical Device → Driver/Event → Object State → Display → Perception → Comparison with Goal*. A clear arrow returning to *User Intention* completes the closed loop.
2. **Signifier (2 marks):** A *signifier* (Norman, 1988) is a visible cue — e.g., a drag-handle icon — that communicates *what* can be manipulated and *how*. Without a signifier, the affordance is invisible, the Gulf of Execution widens, and the user hesitates.
3. **Feedback (2 marks):** *Feedback* is the system's response shown to the user within < 100 ms. It can be visual (the object moves), audio (a click), or haptic (vibration). Feedback closes the Gulf of Evaluation and stabilises the loop.

**Solution to (b) — 7 marks:**

Given: $A = 20$ cm, $W = 4$ cm, $a = 0.105$ s, $b = 0.103$ s/bit, $T_{obs} = 0.65$ s.

**(i) Index of Difficulty (2 marks):**

$$ID = \log_2\!\left(\frac{A}{W} + 1\right) = \log_2\!\left(\frac{20}{4} + 1\right) = \log_2(6) = 2.585 \text{ bits}$$

**(ii) Predicted Movement Time (2 marks):**

$$T_{pred} = 0.105 + 0.103 \times 2.585 = 0.105 + 0.2663 = 0.3713 \text{ s}$$

**(iii) Throughput and comment (3 marks):**

$$TP = \frac{ID}{T_{obs}} = \frac{2.585}{0.65} \approx 3.98 \text{ bits/s}$$

**Comment (1 mark):** Throughput ≈ **3.98 bits/s** lies inside the standard healthy range of **3 – 8 bits/s**, so the device–task combination is performing acceptably.

---

### 📗 Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 Choice 2]**

**(a)** Compare **direct and indirect manipulation** with two advantages and two disadvantages each. Explain Norman's *Gulf of Execution* and *Gulf of Evaluation* in this context. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive Fitts's Law from Shannon's information theory. A designer increases the button width from 2 cm to 4 cm on a 16 cm reach. What is the **percentage reduction** in Index of Difficulty? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Solution to (a) — 7 marks:**

| Aspect | Direct Manipulation | Indirect Manipulation |
|--------|---------------------|------------------------|
| **Definition (1 mark each)** | User acts on the object itself | User acts through a proxy/mediator |
| **Advantage 1** | Immediate visual feedback | Scales to many parameters and hidden states |
| **Advantage 2** | Easy error correction by pointing away | Reduces screen clutter for precise numeric input |
| **Disadvantage 1** | Limited to visible/small-scale operations | Higher cognitive load and training time |
| **Disadvantage 2** | Consumes screen space for handles | Steeper Gulf of Execution — user must learn widget semantics |

**Norman's Gulfs (2 marks):** The *Gulf of Execution* is the gap between what the user *wants to do* and the actions the system *allows*. Direct manipulation narrows it because the action is spatially isomorphic with the goal. The *Gulf of Evaluation* is the gap between the system's *actual* state and the user's *perceived* state. Direct manipulation narrows it through immediate rendering of the result.

**Solution to (b) — 7 marks:**

**Derivation of Fitts's Law (4 marks):**
1. Shannon's channel capacity $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — replace $S$ with movement amplitude $A$ and $N$ with target width $W$ to obtain the **Index of Difficulty** $ID = \log_2(A/W + 1)$.
2. Movement time $T$ is proportional to information transmitted: $T = a + b \cdot ID$.
3. Combining: $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(A/W + 1)$.

**Numerical part (3 marks):**
- $ID_1 = \log_2(16/2 + 1) = \log_2(9) = 3.170$ bits.
- $ID_2 = \log_2(16/4 + 1) = \log_2(5) = 2.322$ bits.
- Reduction $= (3.170 - 2.322) / 3.170 \times 100\% \approx 26.75\%$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students mistakenly compute $W$ as the diagonal of the target or forget to add 1 inside the logarithm. Marks are deducted for:
> 1. **Forgetting the +1** in $\log_2(A/W + 1)$ — Fitts added it to avoid the singularity when $A \to 0$. (–0.5 mark)
> 2. **Using $T_{pred}$ instead of $T_{obs}$** when computing throughput. (–0.5 mark)
> 3. **Not converting units consistently** — if $A$ is in cm and $W$ in mm, normalise to a single unit *before* taking the ratio. (–0.5 mark)
> 4. **Quoting Fitts's Law without explaining the role of the logarithm** — the examiner expects at least one sentence on *why* it is logarithmic (the Weber-Fechner law analogue). (–1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Manipulation of Digital Objects**

- 🔑 **Direct manipulation** = continuous + physical + reversible + immediate feedback + easy error correction (Shneiderman, 1983).
- 🔑 **Indirect manipulation** uses a proxy widget such as a dialog box; needed for hidden or numerical state.
- 🔑 **Norman’s Action Cycle** has 7 stages split by the *Gulf of Execution* and *Gulf of Evaluation*.
- 🔑 **Fitts's Law:** $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(A/W + 1)$. $A$ = amplitude, $W$ = width. Unit of ID is **bits**.
- 🔑 **Throughput** = ID / T, expressed in **bits/second**. Healthy range **3 – 8 bits/s** per ISO 9241-9.
- 🔑 **Steering Law** $T = a + b \cdot (L/W)$ applies to sliders, scrollbars, and 2D drag-through-tunnel tasks.
- 🔑 **DOF** — 2D tasks have 2 DOF (x, y); 3D rigid bodies have **6 DOF** (3 translation + 3 rotation).
- 🔑 **Latency budget** for direct manipulation: feedback must appear within **< 100 ms**; ideally **< 16 ms** for 60 Hz.
- 🔑 **Affordance vs Signifier** — affordance is *perceived* possibility; signifier is the *visible* indicator. (Norman.)
- 🔑 **CD ratio** = cursor movement ÷ device movement; values 0.5–4 are typical; high CD can break Fitts compliance.
- 🔑 **Common widgets for manipulation** — drag-handles, rotate rings, corner-resize grips, slider, scrollbar, color picker, property sheet.
- 🔑 **Touchscreen specifics** — pinch = 2-DOF scale; two-finger rotate = 1-DOF rotation; 3D Touch / Force Touch adds a *pressure* DOF.
- 🔑 **3D manipulation** — Virtual trackball / Arcball, widgets-on-rails (Bier’s), and HOMER (Hand-centered Object Manipulation Extending Ray-casting) are common techniques.
- 🔑 **Constraint snap** reduces Fitts ID by *enlarging the effective target width* when the user is near a snap point (Photoshop guides, AutoCAD grid).
- 🔑 **Error rate model:** $P_e = 1 - \exp(-W^2 / 8\sigma^2)$ — Gaussian endpoint spread assumption.
- 🔑 **KTU favourite topics:** *Shneiderman's principles*, *Norman’s gulfs*, *Fitts's Law derivation + numerical*, *Steering Law*, *direct vs indirect comparison*, *widget examples* — be ready with a 2-mark, a 3-mark, a 7-mark, and a 14-mark answer on each.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Displays with Rear Projection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT753 - HUMAN-MACHINE INTERFACE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Displays with Rear Projection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Rear Projection Display (RPD)** — also called a **Rear Projection Television (RPTV)** — is a large-format electronic display technology in which the image-forming light source, modulation engine, and projection optics are physically housed in a **sealed enclosure behind a translucent viewing screen**. The viewer observes the *rear* face of the screen, while the optical projection occurs on the *opposite* (back) face of the same screen.

The optical engine projects the modulated light beam onto a **diffusing transmission screen** (typically a **Fresnel lens** + **lenticular lens** stack, or a **diffusive bead screen**), which redistributes the light toward the viewer's eyes with controlled gain and viewing angle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> *Rear projection displays position the projector and screen in a single integrated unit. Light from the image source is projected onto the back surface of a diffusing screen; the viewer sees the image from the front. The optical path is folded using mirrors to keep the cabinet depth short relative to screen diagonal.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are inside a **cinema auditorium** but you swap places with the projector operator:

- **Front projection (normal cinema):** The projector shoots light from *behind the audience* onto a *reflective* screen. The screen bounces light back to your eyes.
- **Rear projection:** The projector is *between you and the screen*, hidden inside a black box. It shoots light **away from you** onto the back of a *translucent* screen, and the image is transmitted *through* the screen to reach your eyes.

A perfect real-world analogy is a **slide projector** (old-style, with transparent slides). When you place the slide glass between a lamp and a sheet of tracing paper, the picture "appears" on the paper because the light passes *through* the slide. A rear projection TV does exactly this — but electronically, with LCD/DLP chips instead of glass slides, and a special optical screen instead of tracing paper.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use a translucent screen instead of a wall?**
> Because a normal wall would scatter light in all directions (loss of brightness, color shift, and no focus). A rear-projection screen uses micro-optical elements (**Fresnel lenses + lenticular lenses**) to **collimate the incoming projection light** and then **redirect it forward into a controlled viewing cone**, giving high brightness and wide viewing angles simultaneously.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics

The following metrics are **standardized industry parameters** for any rear projection system, and appear regularly in KTU 2024 ESE questions:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Throw Distance** | $D$ | 0.5 m – 3.0 m | Distance from projection lens to screen |
| **Throw Ratio** | $TR$ | 0.25 – 1.5 | Ratio of throw distance to screen diagonal |
| **Screen Diagonal** | $d$ | 42" – 80"+ | Diagonal measurement of viewing screen |
| **Screen Gain** | $G$ | 1.0 – 2.5 | Luminance amplification factor of the screen |
| **Half-Gain Angle** | $\theta_{HG}$ | 25° – 45° | Viewing angle at which gain drops to $G/2$ |
| **Luminous Flux** | $\Phi_v$ | 500 – 2000 lm | Total light output of the projection lamp |
| **ANSI Lumens** | $\Phi_{ANSI}$ | 500 – 3000 lm | Standardized brightness measurement |
| **Contrast Ratio** | $CR$ | 1000:1 – 10000:1 | Luminance ratio of white-to-black pixels |
| **Resolution** | $N$ | XGA, 1080p, 4K | Native pixel count of image source |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optical Constant — Speed of Light:**
> All optical projection obeys the universal relation $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. Although rear projection does not involve relativistic effects, the **imaging geometry** (lens equation) is the same as for cameras and telescopes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Subsystem Breakdown

A complete rear projection system is a **chain of six subsystems** working in optical cascade:

### 2.1.1 Light Source (Illumination Engine)
- Historically: **UHP (Ultra-High Performance) mercury arc lamps** (100 – 250 W, lifetime ~ 6000 h).
- Modern: **LED arrays** (RGB) or **Laser phosphor** sources.
- Function: Provides a uniform, intense, **white** (or sequenced RGB) light beam.
- Key spec: **luminous flux** $\Phi_v$ measured in **lumens (lm)**.

### 2.1.2 Modulator (Image Generator)
The modulated light forms the image. Three principal technologies:

**(a) CRT-based (legacy, 1970s – 2000s):**
- Three separate cathode ray tubes (R, G, B) project converged beams through projection lenses.
- Excellent blacks, but bulky, heavy, and dim (200 – 400 lm).

**(b) LCD (Liquid Crystal Display) Transmissive Light Valve:**
- Single white lamp → dichroic mirrors split into R, G, B → three transmissive LCD panels → dichroic prism re-combines → projection lens → screen.
- Each LCD pixel rotates polarization; polarizers block light at "off" pixels.

**(c) DLP (Digital Light Processing) — Texas Instruments technology:**
- Uses a **Digital Micromirror Device (DMD)** — a chip with millions of microscopic mirrors that tilt ±10°–12° between "on" and "off" positions thousands of times per second.
- Single-chip designs use a **color wheel** (RGB or RGBRGB); three-chip designs split light into R, G, B paths and use three DMDs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why mirrors tilt?** A DMD mirror in the "on" position directs light *through* the projection lens onto the screen. In the "off" position, it deflects light to a **light absorber (heat sink)**. Pulse-width modulation of the tilt duration produces **grayscale**.

### 2.1.3 Projection Optics
- **Projection lens** assembly: multi-element, aspherical, fixed or short-throw.
- Often combined with **fold mirrors** to bend the optical path 90° or more.
- This folding is what makes a 60" rear projection TV only ~50 cm deep.

### 2.1.4 Rear Projection Screen
Two-layer micro-optical construction:
- **Fresnel lens layer** (rear) — collimates diverging projection light into parallel rays (mimics an infinite-distance source).
- **Lenticular lens layer** (front) — vertical cylindrical micro-lenticules redirect light horizontally toward the viewer with controlled gain.

### 2.1.5 Electronics / Video Processing
- Video decoder → scaler → deinterlacer → frame rate converter → image enhancer.
- Provides aspect-ratio control, gamma correction, color temperature adjustment.

### 2.1.6 Enclosure / Cabinet
- Light-sealed housing.
- Folded mirror path keeps depth $< d/2$ for most designs.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Optical Folding

> **The "depth problem":** For a 50" screen, an unobstructed projection throw would require $\approx 2.5$ m. By inserting a **fold mirror**, the optical path travels: *lens → up → mirror → forward → screen*, achieving the same projection distance in a cabinet of depth $< 0.5$ m.

This is analogous to a **periscope** in a submarine: you cannot push the projection beam straight, so you redirect it using plane mirrors, preserving image orientation.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following table. These are the most-tested formulas for rear projection topics in KTU ESE.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $TR = \dfrac{D}{d}$ | Throw Ratio definition (distance to screen / screen diagonal) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $D = TR \times d$ | Rearranged throw distance | m or inches |
| 3 | $W_{img} = \dfrac{D \times W_{mod}}{f}$ | Image width on screen (lens equation, $D \gg f$) | m |
| 4 | $h_{img} = \dfrac{D \times h_{mod}}{f}$ | Image height on screen | m |
| 5 | $M = \dfrac{h_{img}}{h_{mod}} = \dfrac{D}{f}$ | Magnification of the projection lens | dimensionless |
| 6 | $G = \dfrac{L_{screen,\theta = 0}}{L_{Lambertian}}$ | Screen gain (peak luminance vs Lambertian reference) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $L_{viewed} = \dfrac{\Phi_v \cdot G \cdot \eta_{opt}}{A_{screen} \cdot \pi}$ | Approx. on-axis luminance (Lambertian approx.) | cd/m² |
| 8 | $\theta_{HG} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{p_{lens}}{2f_{lentic}}\right)$ | Approx. half-gain angle of lenticular screen | rad / ° |
| 9 | $CR = \dfrac{L_{white}}{L_{black}}$ | Contrast ratio of the display | dimensionless |
| 10 | $f_{update} = \dfrac{N_{color} \times 60}{1}$ | DMD color wheel frequency (single-chip, 60 Hz) | Hz |
| 11 | $N_{mirror} = 1920 \times 1080$ (or $3840 \times 2160$) | Native mirror count = native resolution | pixels |

> **Note on notation:** $f$ = projection lens focal length; $W_{mod}, h_{mod}$ = width and height of modulator (DMD or LCD); $D$ = throw distance; $A_{screen}$ = screen area; $\eta_{opt}$ = optical throughput efficiency (typically 0.05 – 0.20).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Rear projection technology — though largely superseded by flat-panel LCD/OLED in living rooms — is **still mission-critical** in the following domains:

- **Control rooms** (air traffic control, power grid SCADA, military command centers): very large diagonals (up to 200"), serviceable light engines, 24/7 operation.
- **Simulation and training** (flight simulators, driving simulators): enormous immersive screens, often with multiple RPTV cubes edge-blended.
- **Digital signage** (stadiums, airports, public squares): weather-resistant when cabinet-sealed.
- **Medical imaging review** (radiology halls): high-resolution cubes, calibrated DICOM grayscale.
- **Rear-projection **video walls** in boardrooms and broadcast studios: modular, hot-swappable engines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why didn't RPTV survive in homes?** Three reasons: (1) cabinet depth was unacceptable for wall mounting; (2) UHP lamps required expensive replacement every ~3 years; (3) flat-panel LCD prices fell below RPTV prices by ~2008. The KTU 2024 syllabus still covers RPD because the **optical principles** underpin DLP classroom projectors, cinema projectors, and pico-projectors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Throw Distance from Lens Equation

**Starting principle:** The thin-lens equation governs all refractive imaging optics, including projection.

$$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{s_o} + \frac{1}{s_i}$$

where:
- $f$ = focal length of the projection lens
- $s_o$ = object distance (from modulator to lens)
- $s_i$ = image distance (from lens to screen) = $D$

**Step 1 — Recognize the projection geometry.**

In rear projection, the modulator (LCD/DMD) is physically small (diagonal $\approx 0.5" - 1.3"$), and the screen diagonal $d$ is large (50" – 80"). The magnification is therefore large, meaning the modulator sits **just outside the focal point** of the lens.

**Step 2 — Use the projection approximation $D \gg s_o$.**

The image distance $s_i = D$ is much greater than the object distance $s_o$. Therefore:

$$s_o \approx f \quad \text{(modulator positioned one focal length behind the lens)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the lens equation with this approximation.**

$$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{f} + \frac{1}{D}$$

The leading $\frac{1}{f}$ terms cancel, leaving $\frac{1}{D} \approx 0$. This is the classic *infinity-focus* limit; in real projection designs, $s_o$ is slightly larger than $f$, producing a finite $D$.

**Step 4 — Solve for the exact case where $s_o$ is not equal to $f$.**

Let $s_o = f + \delta$, where $\delta$ is a small offset. Then:

$$\frac{1}{D} = \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{f + \delta} = \frac{(f + \delta) - f}{f(f + \delta)} = \frac{\delta}{f(f + \delta)}$$

**Step 5 — Invert to get throw distance.**

$$D = \frac{f(f + \delta)}{\delta} = \frac{f^2}{\delta} + f \approx \frac{f^2}{\delta} \quad (\text{since } \delta \ll f)$$

**Step 6 — Express as a function of the modulator diagonal $h_{mod}$ and the desired image diagonal $d$.**

Using $M = D/f = d/h_{mod}$:

$$D = \frac{f \times d}{h_{mod}}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to throw ratio form.**

$$TR = \frac{D}{d} = \frac{f}{h_{mod}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key engineering insight:** The throw ratio of a projection lens is essentially the **ratio of its focal length to the modulator diagonal**. A short-focal-length (short-throw) lens has $TR \ll 1$, allowing a large image from a short distance.

## 3.2 Derivation: Screen Gain and Luminance

**Starting principle:** A perfectly **Lambertian** surface scatters incoming light equally in all directions, producing a baseline luminance. A directional screen can exceed this baseline by a factor $G$ (gain) within a narrow cone.

**Step 1 — Define Lambertian reference luminance.**

For a Lambertian surface illuminated by a light source of luminous flux $\Phi_v$ with screen area $A$:

$$L_{Lambertian} = \frac{\Phi_v}{\pi A} \quad \text{[cd/m}^2\text{]}$$

The factor $\pi$ comes from the integration of Lambert's cosine law over a hemisphere: $E = \pi L$.

**Step 2 — Define screen gain.**

$$G = \frac{L_{screen}}{L_{Lambertian}} = \frac{\Phi_{forward}}{A} \times \frac{\pi A}{\Phi_v} = \frac{\pi \Phi_{forward}}{\Phi_v}$$

where $\Phi_{forward}$ is the luminous flux emitted by the screen in the forward viewing direction.

**Step 3 — Account for optical losses in the projection path.**

The light reaching the screen is not the full lamp flux. The actual on-screen flux is:

$$\Phi_{screen} = \Phi_v \times \eta_{opt} \times \eta_{screen\_abs}$$

where:
- $\eta_{opt} \approx 0.20$ — optical throughput of lamp → lens → modulator → mirror path
- $\eta_{screen\_abs} \approx 0.85$ — fraction absorbed by the screen (rest is transmitted or back-scattered)

**Step 4 — Compute perceived luminance with screen gain.**

$$L_{viewed} = \frac{\Phi_{screen} \times G}{\pi A_{screen}}$$

Substituting values for a typical RPTV ($\Phi_v = 1000$ lm, $\eta_{opt} = 0.20$, $A_{screen} = 0.5$ m², $G = 1.5$):

$$L_{viewed} = \frac{1000 \times 0.20 \times 0.85 \times 1.5}{\pi \times 0.5} = \frac{255}{1.571} \approx 162 \text{ cd/m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Verify against the KTU mark scheme expectation.**

A KTU 14-mark question on screen gain will require this 5-step derivation. Skipping Step 3 (optical losses) costs 2 marks; skipping Step 4 (final numeric) costs 2 marks.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Rear Projection vs Front Projection

| Parameter | Rear Projection | Front Projection |
|---|---|---|
| Ambient light immunity | **High** (screen absorbs stray light) | **Low** (image washed out) |
| Cabinet footprint | Deep, bulky | Thin, just a screen on wall |
| Lamp accessibility | Easy (rear panel) | Ceiling mount — difficult |
| Setup complexity | **None** (integrated) | High (alignment, focus) |
| Maximum screen size | Limited (~80") | **Unlimited** |
| Shadow on image | **Impossible** (viewer cannot block beam) | Possible |
| Cost for large screen | High (per inch) | Lower (per inch) |
| Hot-spot effect | Possible (gain artifacts) | N/A (matte screen) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Rear Projection Display

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Light Source UHP Lamp LED Laser] --> B[Color Separation Dichroic Mirrors]
    B --> C1[Red Light Path]
    B --> C2[Green Light Path]
    B --> C3[Blue Light Path]
    C1 --> D1[Red Modulator LCD or DMD]
    C2 --> D2[Green Modulator LCD or DMD]
    C3 --> D3[Blue Modulator LCD or DMD]
    D1 --> E[Recombination Prism X Cube Dichroic]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Projection Lens Assembly]
    F --> G[Fold Mirror 45 Degree]
    G --> H[Fresnel Lens Layer Rear of Screen]
    H --> I[Lenticular Lens Layer Front of Screen]
    I --> J[Viewer Eye Position]
    K[Video Processor Scaler] --> D1
    K --> D2
    K --> D3
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Light flows from the lamp (left) through color separation, gets modulated by three imaging chips, recombines in a dichroic X-cube prism, projects through a lens, bounces off a fold mirror, passes through the two-layer screen, and reaches the viewer.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Single-Chip DLP Rear Projection Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[White LED or Lamp] --> B[Condenser Optics]
    B --> C[Rotating Color Wheel RGB or RGBRGB]
    C --> D[Total Internal Reflection Prism TIR]
    D --> E[Digital Micromirror Device DMD]
    E --> F[Projection Lens]
    F --> G[Fold Mirror]
    G --> H[Rear Projection Screen]
    H --> I[Viewer]
    E -->|Off state light| J[Light Absorber Heat Sink]
    K[Video Signal] --> L[DMD Controller FPGA]
    L --> E
    M[Color Wheel Encoder] --> L
```

> **Key insight:** The color wheel spins synchronously with the DMD. As the red filter passes the light path, the DMD displays the red channel; the eye fuses the sequential R, G, B frames into a full-color image (a technique called **field-sequential color**).

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Rear Projection Screen Layers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Projection Beam Diverging from Lens] --> B[Fresnel Lens Layer Collimates Light]
    B --> C[Lenticular Lens Layer Vertical Cylinders Redirect Forward]
    C --> D[Diffuser Layer Optional Black Stripe]
    D --> E[Viewer Eye]
    F[Ambient Room Light] --> D
    D -->|Absorbed| G[Black Stripe Layer Rejects Ambient]
```

> **Why two layers?** The Fresnel lens converts diverging projection light into collimated light (mimicking an "infinity" light source). The lenticular lens then bends the collimated light toward the viewer's seating position. The optional black-stripe layer (visible as faint vertical lines on the screen) absorbs ambient room light coming from above, boosting contrast.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between front projection and rear projection displays. List any three advantages of rear projection systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Front Projection | Rear Projection |
|---|---|---|
| Projector location | In front of screen, with audience | Behind screen, hidden in cabinet |
| Image formation | Reflected off screen | Transmitted through screen |
| Ambient light | Severe washout | Good immunity |

**Three advantages of rear projection:**
1. **No shadow formation** — viewer cannot obstruct the projection beam since it is behind the screen. (1 mark)
2. **Better ambient light performance** — the screen's optical structure absorbs stray light from the room. (1 mark)
3. **Integrated, pre-aligned system** — no setup, no keystone correction needed; the optical geometry is factory-calibrated. (1 mark)

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is meant by the *throw ratio* of a projection system? If a projector has a throw ratio of 0.5 and the screen diagonal is 70", calculate the throw distance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Throw ratio $TR$ is the ratio of the throw distance $D$ (lens-to-screen distance) to the screen diagonal $d$. It is a dimensionless quantity that indicates whether a projector is short-throw, standard, or long-throw.
- **Formula (1 mark):**
$$TR = \frac{D}{d} \implies D = TR \times d$$
- **Substitution (1 mark):**
$$D = 0.5 \times 70" = 35" = 0.889 \text{ m}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Forgetting to mention **screen gain** in any answer on rear projection screens = −2 marks.
> - Confusing **Fresnel lens** (rear layer, collimates) with **lenticular lens** (front layer, redirects) = −1 mark.
> - Drawing the optical path with the projector *in front* of the screen = −3 marks (a fatal conceptual error).
> - Using wrong units in $D = TR \times d$ (mixing inches and metres) = −1 mark per occurrence.
> - Forgetting to state that RPTV has been **superseded by flat panels** in homes but is **still used in control rooms and simulation** = −1 mark if the question asks about "current relevance."

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a rear projection TV using DLP technology. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Introduction (1 mark):**
A DLP rear-projection TV uses a **Digital Micromirror Device (DMD)** — a semiconductor chip containing an array of microscopic aluminum mirrors, one per pixel — to modulate light from a high-intensity lamp.

**(ii) Light source and color wheel (2 marks):**
A UHP (Ultra-High Performance) mercury arc lamp or LED produces white light. A spinning **color wheel** with red, green, and blue (and sometimes clear / yellow / cyan) segments sequentially filters the white light into time-multiplexed primary colors. The wheel rotates at $6 \times 60 = 360$ Hz for a 60 Hz refresh with 6 segments.

**(iii) DMD operation (2 marks):**
The monochromatic light falls on the DMD. Each micromirror can tilt **+12° (ON state)** to direct light through the projection lens onto the screen, or **−12° (OFF state)** to deflect light into a heat-absorbing light dump. The mirror toggles thousands of times per frame, and the **duty cycle** of ON time produces grayscale. Three sequential R, G, B sub-frames are integrated by the viewer's **persistence of vision** to produce full color.

**(iv) Projection lens and folded optical path (1 mark):**
The modulated light passes through a multi-element projection lens, then reflects off a **45° fold mirror** that redirects the beam horizontally toward the rear of the screen, allowing a shallow cabinet.

**(v) Screen (1 mark):**
The rear-projection screen consists of a **Fresnel lens** (to collimate the diverging light) and a **lenticular lens** (to redirect light toward the viewer with gain > 1).

---

**(b) Explain the construction and function of a rear projection screen. Why are two optical layers (Fresnel + lenticular) used? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Two-layer construction (2 marks):**
A modern rear-projection screen is a **two-layer optical sandwich**:
- **Rear layer:** A **Fresnel lens** — a flat plastic sheet molded with concentric circular grooves acting as a thin, lightweight replacement for a bulky glass Fresnel lens.
- **Front layer:** A **lenticular lens** — a sheet with vertical cylindrical micro-lenses molded into its surface.

**(ii) Function of the Fresnel layer (2 marks):**
Light from a short-distance projector diverges strongly. The Fresnel lens **collimates** this diverging light, making it appear to come from a source at optical infinity. This means that subsequent optics (the lenticular layer) see a uniform, parallel light distribution — independent of the position within the screen.

**(iii) Function of the lenticular layer (2 marks):**
The vertical cylindrical micro-lenticules of the lenticular sheet **refract the collimated light horizontally toward the viewer's seating zone**. This gives the screen a **gain** greater than 1 (typically $G = 1.4 - 2.0$), meaning the on-axis image is brighter than a Lambertian reference.

**(iv) Why two layers are necessary (1 mark):**
- The Fresnel lens alone would not redirect light directionally (it only collimates).
- The lenticular lens alone would not correct the angle-of-incidence variation across the screen.
- **Together** they provide uniform brightness across the screen AND controlled viewing angle — a single layer cannot achieve both.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Compare CRT-based, LCD-based, and DLP-based rear projection technologies in terms of image quality, lifetime, and cost. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | CRT-based RPTV | LCD-based RPTV | DLP-based RPTV |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Image quality** | Excellent color, deep blacks, but limited resolution (480p – 1080i) | Good color, slightly soft, can suffer from "screen door" pixel grid | Sharp, high contrast, may show "rainbow artifacts" (color wheel) |
| **Luminance** | Low (200 – 400 lm) | Medium (500 – 1000 lm) | High (800 – 2000 lm) |
| **Lamp/light source lifetime** | No lamp (CRT phosphors, ~ 20000 h) | UHP lamp ~ 6000 h | UHP lamp ~ 6000 h; LED/laser ~ 20000 h |
| **Cabinet depth** | Very deep (3-cube geometry) | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Power consumption** | High (CRT heaters) | Medium | Medium |
| **Cost** | Highest (precision CRTs) | Lowest | Medium |
| **Maintenance** | Convergence re-alignment | Lamp replacement | Lamp + color wheel replacement |
| **Status (2024)** | Discontinued | Discontinued for RPTV; LCD still used in front projectors | Dominant in front projection; legacy in RPTV |

**(Mark distribution: 1 mark introduction + 6 rows × 1 mark each = 7 marks.)**

---

**(b) Define *screen gain* and *half-gain angle*. For a rear projection screen with gain $G = 1.8$ and half-gain angle $\theta_{HG} = 30°$, compute the off-axis luminance when the viewer sits at $45°$ from the screen normal. Assume a cosine-roll-off gain model. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Definition of screen gain (1 mark):**
**Screen gain $G$** is the ratio of the luminance of the screen in a given direction to the luminance of a perfectly Lambertian surface under identical illumination, measured at the screen normal:
$$G = \frac{L_{screen}(\theta = 0)}{L_{Lambertian}}$$

**(ii) Definition of half-gain angle (1 mark):**
The **half-gain angle $\theta_{HG}$** is the viewing angle (measured from screen normal) at which the screen's gain falls to half of its peak (on-axis) value, i.e., $G(\theta_{HG}) = G(0)/2$.

**(iii) Cosine-roll-off model (2 marks):**
For a lenticular screen, the gain as a function of viewing angle $\theta$ can be approximated as:
$$G(\theta) = G(0) \cos^k(\theta)$$
where $k$ is determined by the half-gain condition. At $\theta = \theta_{HG}$:
$$\frac{G(0)}{2} = G(0) \cos^k(\theta_{HG}) \implies \cos^k(\theta_{HG}) = 0.5$$

Solving for $k$:
$$k = \frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(\cos\theta_{HG})} = \frac{-0.693}{\ln(\cos 30°)} = \frac{-0.693}{-0.1431} \approx 4.84$$

**(iv) Compute off-axis gain at $\theta = 45°$ (2 marks):**
$$G(45°) = 1.8 \times \cos^{4.84}(45°)$$
$$\cos(45°) = 0.7071$$
$$\cos^{4.84}(45°) = (0.7071)^{4.84} = e^{4.84 \times \ln(0.7071)} = e^{4.84 \times (-0.3466)} = e^{-1.6775} \approx 0.1869$$
$$G(45°) = 1.8 \times 0.1869 \approx 0.336$$

**(v) Interpretation (1 mark):**
At $45°$ off-axis, the screen's effective gain drops to about **0.34** — i.e., the image appears **dimmer than a Lambertian surface** (gain < 1). This defines the practical **viewing cone** of the rear projection screen; viewers beyond $\theta_{HG}$ experience washed-out images.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Displays with Rear Projection**

- **Rear Projection Display (RPD):** Projector + screen in one unit; viewer sees image **transmitted** through a translucent screen.
- **Optical path is folded** using plane (fold) mirrors to keep cabinet depth < 50 cm for a 60" screen.
- **Three modulator technologies:** CRT (legacy), LCD (transmissive panels + dichroic prism), DLP (DMD with tilting micromirrors).
- **DMD operation:** Each mirror tilts $\pm 12°$; "on" = light to screen, "off" = light to absorber; pulse-width modulation creates grayscale.
- **Color wheel** spins at $N \times 60$ Hz to sequence R, G, B for single-chip DLP; uses **field-sequential color**.
- **Rear projection screen = Fresnel lens + Lenticular lens.**
  - **Fresnel lens:** collimates diverging projection light.
  - **Lenticular lens:** redirects collimated light into a forward viewing cone.
- **Screen gain $G$** = directional luminance amplification (1.0 = Lambertian, > 1 = brighter on-axis, < 1 = dimmer).
- **Half-gain angle $\theta_{HG}$:** angle at which gain = $G/2$; defines viewing cone.
- **Throw Ratio $TR = D/d$**; typical RPTV $TR = 0.25 - 1.5$.
- **Throw distance $D = TR \times d$**; in metres if $d$ in metres, in inches if $d$ in inches.
- **Lens equation** gives magnification $M = D/f = d/h_{mod}$.
- **Luminance** $L \approx \Phi_v \eta_{opt} G / (\pi A_{screen})$ — must include optical efficiency $\eta_{opt} \approx 0.20$.
- **Contrast ratio** $CR = L_{white} / L_{black}$; DLP has highest CR; CRT has deepest blacks.
- **Ambient light rejection:** RPTV is much better than front projection because the screen's black-stripe layer absorbs off-axis light.
- **Shadow-free:** Viewer cannot cast a shadow on the image because the beam is behind the screen.
- **Current relevance:** RPTV is **discontinued for home use** (replaced by flat panels) but **still used in control rooms, simulation, and large video walls**.
- **Common pitfalls:** Confusing Fresnel and lenticular; omitting optical efficiency in luminance calculations; mixing units in throw distance; stating RPTV is "still mainstream for homes" (it is not, post-2010).
- **Numerical constants to memorize:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s; DMD tilt $\pm 10°$–$12°$; UHP lamp lifetime $\approx 6000$ h; LED/laser lifetime $\approx 20000$ h; 60 Hz refresh with 6-segment color wheel = 360 Hz.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sound Interaction** in Human-Machine Interface (HMI) refers to the systematic design, processing, and utilization of auditory signals as a primary or secondary modality for communication between a user and a computational system. It encompasses three principal sub-domains:

1. **Speech-Based Interaction** — bidirectional exchange involving automatic speech recognition (ASR) and text-to-speech synthesis (TTS).
2. **Non-Speech Audio** — the use of abstract tones, earcons, auditory icons, and sonification to convey information, status, or alerts.
3. **Auditory Display Engineering** — the application of psychoacoustic principles to optimize the perceptual clarity of system feedback.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Paraphrase):**
> Sound Interaction is the engineering discipline concerned with capturing, processing, presenting, and perceiving audio signals within a human-machine loop, treating the human auditory system as a high-bandwidth, parallel-processing perceptual channel.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Cockpit of a Commercial Aircraft**
> 
> Imagine a pilot flying a Boeing 737 in dense fog. The pilot cannot see the runway, but a voice in the headset calmly announces *"Glide Slope, One Thousand"* while a soft repeating *ding-ding* indicates terrain proximity. The pilot does not need to look at a screen — the **ears** are doing the work. This is exactly what Sound Interaction does in HMI: it converts machine state (altitude, error, success) into **audible signals** that the human brain processes in parallel with vision, freeing the eyes for other tasks.

### 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants and Perceptual Metrics

| Constant / Metric | Standard Value | Context |
|---|---|---|
| **Threshold of Hearing (I₀)** | **10⁻¹² W/m²** | Quietest sound a young ear can detect at 1 kHz |
| **Threshold of Pain** | **10 W/m²** | Upper limit of auditory comfort |
| **Dynamic Range of Human Ear** | **120 dB** | From silence to pain |
| **Audible Frequency Range** | **20 Hz – 20,000 Hz** | Human hearing bandwidth |
| **Speed of Sound in Air (20°C)** | **v = 343 m/s** | Reference value for delay/arrival-time calculations |
| **Speech Bandwidth** | **300 Hz – 3,400 Hz** | Telephony-quality voice range |
| **Preferred Speech Range** | **500 Hz – 2,000 Hz** | Maximum intelligibility band |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The human ear is **not linear** — it is logarithmic. A sound that is *ten times* more intense is perceived as only *twice as loud*. This is why we use the **decibel (dB)** scale, not raw intensity, to describe sound.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics of Sound in HMI Context

A sound wave is a **longitudinal mechanical pressure wave** propagating through a medium (typically air). For HMI engineers, three properties matter most:

#### 2.1.1 Frequency (f)
- Measured in **Hertz (Hz)**.
- Determines **pitch** (perceived highness/lowness).
- Human voice fundamental frequency: ~85 Hz (male) to ~255 Hz (female).
- Critical for **earcon** design and **alert tone** recognition.

#### 2.1.2 Amplitude (A)
- Determines **loudness** (perceived intensity).
- Subjectively, doubling the loudness requires roughly **a 10 dB increase**.

#### 2.1.3 Wavelength (λ)
- The spatial period of one full cycle of the wave.
- Governs diffraction and directionality — critical for **spatial audio** in VR/AR interfaces.

#### 2.1.4 Timbre (Sound Quality)
- Determined by the **harmonic spectrum** above the fundamental frequency.
- Distinguishes the letter *"S"* from *"F"* in speech.

### 2.2 Psychoacoustics — The Bridge Between Physics and Perception

**Psychoacoustics** is the scientific study of how the human brain interprets physical sound waves. Key phenomena exploited in HMI:

- **Masking**: A loud sound renders a quiet, similar-frequency sound inaudible. *Used in MP3 compression.*
- **Critical Bands**: The ear behaves as a bank of ~24 bandpass filters (Bark scale). *Used in equalizer design.*
- **Localization**: Interaural Time Difference (ITD) and Interaural Level Difference (ILD) enable 3D sound placement. *Used in 3D audio games.*
- **Loudness Recruitment**: Hearing-impaired users perceive disproportionate loudness jumps. *Critical for accessibility design.*

### 2.3 The Two Pillars of Sound-Based HMI

> [!NOTE]
> **Pillar 1 — Speech Recognition (Machine Ears)**
> The system listens, decodes acoustic patterns into phonemes, and converts to text/intent.
> 
> **Pillar 2 — Speech Synthesis (Machine Mouth)**
> The system generates human-like speech from text using formant synthesis or neural models.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` to avoid breaking markdown tables.**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use in HMI |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v = f \cdot \lambda$ | Wave equation: speed = frequency × wavelength | Microphone placement, audio delay |
| 2 | $L_d = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | Sound intensity level in decibels | Volume UI, OSHA safety design |
| 3 | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)$ | Sound pressure level (SPL) in dB | Microphone calibration |
| 4 | $I_0 = 10^{-12} \ \text{W/m}^2$ | Reference intensity (threshold of hearing) | Base for dB calculations |
| 5 | $P_0 = 20 \ \mu\text{Pa}$ | Reference pressure (threshold of hearing) | Base for SPL calculations |
| 6 | $f_0 = \dfrac{v}{2L}$ | Fundamental frequency of a tube (open-closed) | Speech formant modeling |
| 7 | $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | Period of a wave | Tone duration design |
| 8 | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | Speech recognizer accuracy |
| 9 | $W_{D} = \int_{f_1}^{f_2} \vert H(f) \vert^2 \, df$ | Power within a frequency band | Spectral feature extraction |
| 10 | $v_{air} = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T$ | Speed of sound in air at temperature T (°C) | Outdoor echo/alert timing |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive HMI**: Auditory warnings for lane-departure must be **localizable** and **distinct from music** → uses ITD and timbre contrast.
- **Medical Devices**: Defibrillators use **paced beeps** whose tempo matches recommended CPR compression rate (110 BPM).
- **Aviation**: The Ground Proximity Warning System (GPWS) uses the spoken phrase *"TERRAIN, TERRAIN"* — chosen because spoken words survive cockpit noise better than tones.
- **Accessibility**: Screen readers (JAWS, NVDA) are pure-TTS HMI systems for visually impaired users.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why the Decibel Scale is Logarithmic

**Problem Statement:** Show that the decibel (dB) formulation emerges naturally from the human ear's logarithmic loudness response, and derive the relationship between intensity ratio and perceived loudness.

**Step 1 — Empirical Fact from Psychophysics (Stevens' Power Law):**
The perceived loudness *L* of a tone is proportional to the intensity *I* raised to a small power:

$$L \propto I^{0.3}$$

**Step 2 — Define a Linear Sensation Scale:**
We wish to construct a sensation scale *S* such that equal increments in *S* correspond to equal perceived increments. By definition of logarithm:

$$S = k \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)$$

where *I₀* is the reference threshold of hearing and *k* is a scaling constant.

**Step 3 — Choose *k = 10* (the Bel → DeciBel convention):**

$$S = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) \quad \text{[decibels]}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the Range:**
- At threshold: $I = I_0 \Rightarrow S = 10 \log_{10}(1) = 0$ dB.
- At pain threshold: $I = 10 \text{ W/m}^2 \Rightarrow S = 10 \log_{10}(10^{13}) = 130$ dB.

**Result:** The audible range of human hearing is mapped to a convenient **0–130 dB** scale.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion to Pressure Form (Step 5):**
> Because acoustic intensity is proportional to the *square* of pressure ($I \propto P^2$):
> 
> $$\frac{I}{I_0} = \left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)^2$$
> 
> Substituting into the dB formula:
> 
> $$L_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)^2\right] = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)$$
> 
> This explains the **factor of 20** used in Sound Pressure Level (SPL) formulas — it is *not* arbitrary; it is a direct consequence of $I \propto P^2$.

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Computing Perceived Loudness of a HMI Alert Tone

**Given:**
- Alert tone intensity: $I = 10^{-4}$ W/m².
- Reference intensity: $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m².
- Frequency: $f = 1000$ Hz (where the ear is most sensitive).

**Find:** Sound intensity level in dB.

**Step 1 — Substitute into the dB equation:**

$$L_{dB} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10^{-4}}{10^{-12}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the fraction inside the log:**

$$\frac{10^{-4}}{10^{-12}} = 10^{(-4) - (-12)} = 10^{8}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithm:**

$$L_{dB} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(10^{8}) = 10 \times 8 = 80 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result (Engineering):**
An 80 dB tone is **loud** (city traffic level) and is well above the 60 dB conversational level. This is appropriate for a **collision-warning** alert in an automotive HMI because it must be impossible to miss.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Perceptual Rule of Thumb:** A **+10 dB** change is perceived as *"twice as loud"* by the average listener. Designers must use this rule carefully — increasing alert volume by 10 dB may make it *annoying* rather than *informative*.

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — Wavelength of a Spoken Vowel

**Given:** Average speed of sound $v = 343$ m/s. The vowel "ah" /ɑ/ has a fundamental frequency of approximately 110 Hz.

**Find:** The wavelength of the fundamental tone.

**Step 1 — Apply the wave equation:**

$$v = f \cdot \lambda \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac{v}{f}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\lambda = \frac{343 \ \text{m/s}}{110 \ \text{Hz}} = 3.118 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Interpretation:**
The wavelength (~3.1 m) is larger than a human head (~0.2 m). This is why low-frequency voice components **diffract around the head** and cannot be easily localized. In contrast, consonants at 4 kHz have $\lambda \approx 0.086$ m — *smaller than the head* — which is why they are responsible for **speech intelligibility** and **localization cues**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sound Interaction Subsystems

| Subsystem | Input | Output | Core Algorithm / Model | HMI Role |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR)** | Acoustic waveform | Text / Intent | HMM, DNN, Whisper, RNN-T | Voice command |
| **Text-to-Speech (TTS)** | Text | Synthesized speech | Concatenative, Formant, Tacotron, WaveNet | Voice prompt |
| **Speaker Identification** | Voice sample | Speaker ID | GMM-UBM, i-Vector, x-Vector | Biometric login |
| **Sound Source Localization** | Multi-mic array | 3D position | GCC-PHAT, SRP-PHAT | Robot audition |
| **Auditory Scene Analysis** | Mixed audio | Event labels | NMF, Deep Clustering | Context-aware HMI |
| **Sonification Engine** | Data stream | Parameterized audio | Mapping function $D \rightarrow A$ | Data exploration |
| **Earcon Generator** | Symbolic event | Tonal motif | MIDI-based synthesizer | Discrete alerts |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Sound Interaction Pipeline in HMI

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    userA[User Voice] --> micA[Microphone Array]
    micA --> preA[Pre-processing: Noise Gate and AGC]
    preA --> featA[Feature Extraction: MFCC or Mel Spectrogram]
    featA --> asrA[ASR Engine: Acoustic and Language Model]
    asrA --> nluA[NLU: Intent and Entity Parser]
    nluA --> dlgA[Dialogue Manager: State Machine]
    dlgA --> nlgA[NLG: Response Generator]
    nlgA --> ttsA[TTS Engine: Neural Vocoder]
    ttsA --> spkA[Speaker Output]
    spkA --> userA

    sysB[System Event or Sensor Data] --> sonB[Sonification Mapper]
    sonB --> audB[Audio Renderer: 3D Spatial Engine]
    audB --> earB[User Ears]
    earB --> percB[Human Auditory Cortex]
    percB --> cogrB[Cognitive Interpretation]
    cogrB --> actB[User Action or Response]

```

### 4.2 Speech Recognition vs. TTS — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph recBlock [Speech Recognition Pipeline]
        r1[Raw Audio Signal] --> r2[Analog to Digital Conversion]
        r2 --> r3[Pre-emphasis Filter]
        r3 --> r4[Framing and Windowing]
        r4 --> r5[FFT: Frequency Domain]
        r5 --> r6[Mel Filterbank]
        r6 --> r7[MFCC Features]
        r7 --> r8[Acoustic Model: DNN]
        r8 --> r9[Decoder with Language Model]
        r9 --> r10[Text Output]
    end

    subgraph synBlock [Text to Speech Pipeline]
        s1[Input Text] --> s2[Text Normalization]
        s2 --> s3[Phoneme Conversion]
        s3 --> s4[Prosody Prediction]

        s5 --> s6[Vocoder: WaveNet or HiFi GAN]

    end

```

### 4.3 Psychoacoustic Model — Masking Thresholds

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input Sound Spectrum X of f] --> B[Apply Critical Band Filter Bank of 24 Bark Bands]
    B --> C[Compute Energy in Each Band]
    C --> D[Apply Spreading Function]
    D --> E[Compute Masking Threshold Curve]
    E --> F[Compare Threshold vs Original Spectrum]
    F --> G[Identify Audible Components Above Masking Floor]
    G --> H[Output: Perceptually Relevant Features]

```

### 4.4 Earcon Design Decision Tree

```mermaid

    branchA --> a1[Choose Family: Familiar Motif]

    a3 --> a4[Validate with Users]

    branchB --> b1[Source Real World Recording]
    branchB --> b2[Ensure 1 to 1 Metaphor Match]

    b3 --> b4[Validate with Users]

    branchC --> c1[Combine Motifs Sequentially]

    c3 --> c4[Validate with Users]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
> Define the term **earcon** and explain how it differs from an **auditory icon**. Provide one HMI use-case for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Earcon (1 mark):** A *Earcon* is a short, abstract, synthetic musical motif used to convey information about a system event. It has no inherent semantic connection to the event it represents — meaning is learned through consistent use. *Example:* The distinctive two-tone "ding-ding" of a microwave indicating cooking completion.
- **Auditory Icon (1 mark):** An *Auditory Icon* is a natural, everyday sound recorded from the real world and used metaphorically to represent a corresponding computer event. The mapping is intuitive because the sound *resembles* the action. *Example:* The sound of paper crumpling played when the user "deletes" a file.
- **HMI Use-Case (1 mark):** Earcons for hierarchical menu navigation in a car's infotainment system; auditory icons for drag-and-drop or trash-can operations in a desktop GUI.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
> State the **wave equation** relating frequency, wavelength, and velocity. If a speech fundamental tone has a frequency of 150 Hz and the speed of sound is 340 m/s, calculate its wavelength.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Wave Equation (1 mark):** $v = f \cdot \lambda$
- **Rearrangement (1 mark):** $\lambda = \dfrac{v}{f}$
- **Substitution and Solution (1 mark):**
  $$\lambda = \frac{340 \ \text{m/s}}{150 \ \text{Hz}} = 2.267 \ \text{m}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Module 4, Model]**
> 
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of an **Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR)** system. Explain the role of the acoustic model, language model, and decoder. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Define the **decibel (dB)** scale. Derive the relationship between **sound intensity level** and **sound pressure level**. An industrial HMI alarm produces a sound of intensity $10^{-2}$ W/m². Calculate its intensity level in dB. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

The architecture of a modern ASR system consists of the following sequential stages:

1. **Signal Acquisition (1 mark):** A microphone captures the analog speech waveform, which is digitized using a 16 kHz, 16-bit Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC).

2. **Feature Extraction (1 mark):** The waveform is converted into a sequence of acoustic feature vectors — typically **Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCCs)** or **filterbank energies** — at 10 ms frame intervals. The Mel scale models the ear's nonlinear frequency resolution.

3. **Acoustic Model (AM) (2 marks):** Maps acoustic features to **phonemes** (basic sound units). Modern systems use **Deep Neural Networks (DNNs)** trained on thousands of hours of transcribed speech. Older systems used **Hidden Markov Models (HMMs)** to model temporal variation.

4. **Language Model (LM) (1 mark):** A statistical or neural model (e.g., n-gram, Transformer) that assigns probabilities to word sequences. It ensures the recognizer prefers *"recognize speech"* over *"wreck a nice beach"*.

5. **Decoder (1 mark):** A search algorithm (e.g., Viterbi, beam search) that combines the AM and LM to find the **most probable word sequence** given the audio. It also handles the pronunciation lexicon (word → phoneme mapping).

6. **Output (1 mark):** The recognized text, optionally with confidence scores, is forwarded to a Natural Language Understanding (NLU) module for intent extraction.

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The decibel is a logarithmic unit that expresses the ratio of a measured sound quantity to a reference quantity. It is used because the human ear responds logarithmically to sound intensity.

**Derivation (3 marks):** Acoustic intensity $I$ is proportional to the square of the RMS sound pressure $P$:

$$I \propto P^2$$

The intensity level in dB is defined as:

$$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)$$

Substituting $I/I_0 = (P/P_0)^2$:

$$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)^2\right] = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)$$

This is the **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)** formula. The factor **20** (instead of 10) arises directly from the square-law relationship between pressure and intensity.

**Numerical Solution (2 marks):**
$$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10^{-2}}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(10^{10}) = 100 \text{ dB}$$

This level (100 dB) corresponds to a **subway train** — extremely loud and appropriate only for a critical industrial safety alarm.

---

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam – Module 4, Model — Alternative Choice]**
> 
> **(a)** Explain the **psychoacoustic concepts of masking and critical bands**. How are these principles used in **MP3 audio compression**? **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Compare and contrast **Text-to-Speech (TTS)** synthesis approaches: *formant synthesis*, *concatenative synthesis*, and *neural synthesis*. State one advantage and one limitation of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

**Masking (2 marks):** Auditory masking is the phenomenon in which the perception of one sound (the *maskee*) is reduced or eliminated by the presence of another sound (the *masker*). Masking is strongest when the two sounds are close in frequency. Two types exist: *frequency masking* (simultaneous) and *temporal masking* (a loud sound masks a quieter one just before or after it).

**Critical Bands (2 marks):** The cochlea behaves as a bank of overlapping bandpass filters. Each filter has a bandwidth known as a **critical band** (measured on the **Bark scale**). There are approximately 24 critical bands covering the audible range. Within a critical band, the ear integrates sound energy — a louder sound at one frequency will mask quieter sounds within the same band.

**Application to MP3 (3 marks):**
1. The audio is decomposed into frequency components using a Modified Discrete Cosine Transform (MDCT).
2. Energy is grouped into **critical-band bins** to model the ear's frequency resolution.
3. A **psychoacoustic model** computes the masking threshold curve — the level below which a component is inaudible and can be discarded.
4. Bit allocation assigns more bits to frequencies **above** the masking threshold (audible) and fewer bits to those **below** (inaudible).
5. This **perceptual coding** achieves 10:1 to 12:1 compression with no perceptible loss in quality.

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

| Approach | Mechanism (1 mark each) | Advantage (1 mark each) | Limitation (1 mark each) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Formant Synthesis** | Models the vocal tract as a set of resonant filters (formants) driven by a glottal source. | Highly compact; fully parametric; easy to modify pitch/rate. | Robotic, "buzzy" quality; poor naturalness. |
| **Concatenative Synthesis** | Stitches together short pre-recorded speech units (diphones, half-phones). | High intelligibility; natural prosody within units. | Discontinuities at unit boundaries; large database needed; limited expressivity. |
| **Neural Synthesis** | End-to-end deep learning (e.g., Tacotron + WaveNet, VITS, FastSpeech). | Near-human naturalness; flexible voice cloning. | Computationally expensive; requires large training data; "black box" control. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Sound Interaction Questions:**
> 
> 1. **dB formula confusion (–2 marks risk):** Students frequently write $10 \log_{10}(I/I_0)$ for *pressure* dB. Remember: intensity uses **10**, pressure uses **20**. The derivation explicitly explains why.
> 
> 2. **Earcon vs. Auditory Icon mix-up (–1 mark risk):** Earcons are *abstract/synthetic*; auditory icons are *natural/recorded*. The metaphorical relationship is the key discriminator.
> 
> 3. **Missing units in numerical answers (–1 mark risk):** Always state units (dB, Hz, m, W/m²). KTU examiners frequently deduct marks for "Hz-less" answers.
> 
> 4. **ASR pipeline missing the Language Model (–2 marks risk):** Many students describe only the acoustic model. The Language Model is *essential* — without it, the decoder cannot choose between acoustically similar words.
> 
> 5. **Confusing speed of sound with speed of light (–1 mark risk):** Always use $v_{sound} \approx 343$ m/s, not $3 \times 10^8$ m/s, for audio problems.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Sound Interaction in HMI**

- **Sound** is a longitudinal pressure wave; in HMI, it serves as a *parallel perceptual channel* to vision.
- **Frequency** → pitch perception; **Amplitude** → loudness perception; **Timbre** → sound quality.
- **Speed of sound** in air at 20°C: **v = 343 m/s**; wave equation: $v = f\lambda$.
- The **decibel scale** is logarithmic because the human ear is logarithmic:
  - Intensity: $L = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_0)$
  - Pressure: $L = 20 \log_{10}(P/P_0)$
- **Threshold of hearing:** $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m² → corresponds to **0 dB**.
- **Audible range:** 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz; **speech range:** 300 Hz to 3,400 Hz.
- **Psychoacoustics** principles: masking, critical bands (Bark scale), ITD/ILD for localization, Stevens' power law.
- **Earcons** = abstract, learned, musical motifs. **Auditory icons** = real-world sounds with intuitive mapping.
- **Sonification** = mapping *data → audio parameters* for non-speech data exploration.
- **ASR pipeline:** ADC → Pre-emphasis → Framing → FFT → Mel filterbank → MFCC → Acoustic Model (DNN) + Language Model → Decoder → Text.
- **TTS pipeline:** Text → Normalization → Phonemes → Prosody → Acoustic Model → Vocoder → Waveform.
- **Three TTS approaches:** Formant (compact/robotic), Concatenative (natural/intrusive), Neural (best quality/heavy compute).
- **+10 dB** is perceived as **twice as loud** — the golden rule of audio UI volume changes.
- **MP3 compression** exploits auditory masking to discard inaudible frequency components, achieving ~10:1 compression with no perceptual loss.
- For HMI design, audio is best for: **alerts, status, continuous data, accessibility, hands-busy/eyes-busy situations**.
- For HMI design, audio is *worst* for: **precise numeric values, long messages, secret information** (overheard).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Acoustics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT753 - HUMAN-MACHINE INTERFACE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basics of Acoustics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Acoustics?

**Acoustics** is the interdisciplinary branch of physics and engineering that deals with the study of mechanical waves in gases, liquids, and solids, including topics such as vibration, sound, ultrasound, and infrasound. In the context of **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)**, acoustics is the foundational science governing the design of auditory displays, voice-controlled interfaces, audio feedback systems, warning sirens, and sonification of data.

**Sound** is specifically defined as a *mechanical longitudinal wave* that propagates through a compressible medium (such as air, water, or solids) by means of pressure variations (compressions and rarefactions) within an *audible frequency range* — typically **20 Hz to 20,000 Hz** for the average young human ear.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEBRT753 / Module 4):**
> The course treats acoustics not merely as a physics topic but as a **design science for HMI**. The student must master (a) the physical properties of sound, (b) the logarithmic decibel scale used in audio engineering, (c) the human auditory perception range, and (d) the interaction of sound with enclosed spaces (reverberation, absorption, reflection) since most HMIs operate indoors.

## 1.2 The Three Quantities that Fully Define a Sound Wave

| Physical Quantity | Symbol | Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency | $f$ | **Hertz (Hz)** | Number of pressure oscillations per second |
| Wavelength | $\lambda$ | **meter (m)** | Spatial distance of one complete cycle |
| Amplitude / Sound Pressure | $p$ | **Pascal (Pa)** | Magnitude of pressure deviation from atmospheric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Audible Frequency Bands (Standard ISO/R IEC Frequencies):**
> - Infrasound: $f < 20$ Hz (inaudible to humans)
> - Audible range: $20$ Hz $\le f \le 20{,}000$ Hz
> - Ultrasound: $f > 20$ kHz (used in medical/industrial HMI)
> - Human speech dominant band: **300 Hz to 3,400 Hz** (telephony / voice HMI bandwidth)

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pebble in a Pond"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — Pebble in a Pond:**
> Imagine tossing a pebble into a still pond. Concentric circular ripples travel outward from the impact point. The **frequency** is how fast the ripples oscillate; the **wavelength** is the distance between two adjacent crests; the **amplitude** is how tall each ripple is. Sound behaves identically — except instead of water-surface displacement, the "ripple" is a *longitudinal pressure wave* travelling through air molecules. Drop the pebble in air, and your eardrum is the "floating leaf" that vibrates sympathetically with the wave — converting pressure oscillations into neural signals (i.e., the perception of sound).

In HMI terms: every time a smartphone plays a notification chime, the speaker acts as the "pebble," and your eardrum is the "leaf." The engineer who designed the chime had to balance **loudness** (amplitude / SPL), **pitch** (frequency), and **timbre** (harmonic content) to make the sound both pleasant and informative — a quintessentially acoustic HMI problem.

## 1.4 Why Acoustics Matters in HMI

In modern Human-Machine Interfaces, acoustic feedback is critical because:

1. **Auditory warnings** in aircraft cockpits, ICUs, and nuclear control rooms convey danger faster than any visual display.
2. **Voice input/output** (ASR + TTS) is now the dominant mode for in-car HMIs, smart speakers, and assistive technologies.
3. **Sonification** — converting data streams into sound — assists visually impaired operators.
4. **Noise control** in operator cabins (tractors, submarines, manufacturing) directly affects cognitive workload and human error rates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reference Standard Sound Pressures (for HMI design benchmarks):**
> - Threshold of human hearing: $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa
> - Normal conversation at 1 m: $\approx 0.02$ Pa
> - Jet engine at 1 m: $\approx 200$ Pa
> - **Pain threshold / immediate ear damage: $\approx 20$ Pa (= 120 dB SPL)**

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Sound Generation and Propagation

Sound is a **longitudinal mechanical wave** that requires a material medium for propagation. Unlike electromagnetic waves, sound **cannot travel through a vacuum**.

**Generation Sequence** (HMI Speaker Example):

1. An electrical audio signal drives a voice coil inside a loudspeaker.
2. The coil moves a diaphragm; the diaphragm compresses adjacent air molecules (creating a **compression**).
3. As the diaphragm reverses, it rarefies the air (creating a **rarefaction**).
4. These alternating high- and low-pressure regions propagate outward at the **speed of sound**.
5. Upon reaching the listener's eardrum, the pressure oscillations are converted to neural impulses by the cochlea.

## 2.2 Core Theoretical Principles

### Principle 1 — Wave Equation in a Fluid Medium

For a one-dimensional plane sound wave propagating in the $+x$ direction through a homogeneous fluid, the **acoustic wave equation** is:

$$\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2} = \frac{1}{c^2} \cdot \frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2}$$

where $p$ is the instantaneous acoustic pressure, $t$ is time, and $c$ is the **speed of sound** in the medium.

### Principle 2 — Speed of Sound

The speed of sound in air depends on temperature. The standard KTU/KTU-exam formula is:

$$c = c_0 \sqrt{\frac{T}{T_0}}$$

where $c_0 = 331.4$ m/s at $T_0 = 273$ K, and $T$ is the absolute temperature in Kelvin. A useful linearised engineering approximation is:

$$c \approx 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot \theta \quad \text{(m/s, where } \theta \text{ is in } ^\circ\text{C)}$$

**Why this matters in HMI:** Outdoor voice-announcement systems (railway platforms, airports) must compensate temperature-dependent speed of sound when beam-forming directional speakers over long distances.

### Principle 3 — Acoustic Impedance

The **specific acoustic impedance** $Z$ (in Rayls, $\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^{-2}\text{s}^{-1}$) is the ratio of acoustic pressure to particle velocity:

$$Z = \frac{p}{u} = \rho_0 \cdot c$$

For air at 20 °C, $\rho_0 \approx 1.204$ kg/m³ and $c \approx 343$ m/s, so $Z_{\text{air}} \approx 413$ Rayls. For water, $Z_{\text{water}} \approx 1.48 \times 10^6$ Rayls — about 3,500× higher. This huge mismatch is why underwater acoustic transducers (hydrophones) need entirely different design from air transducers (microphones).

### Principle 4 — The Decibel Scale (Logarithmic Perception Matching)

The human ear responds **logarithmically** to sound intensity, not linearly. A 10× increase in acoustic power is perceived as roughly *twice as loud*. To match this, engineers use the **decibel (dB)** — a logarithmic unit.

**Sound Pressure Level (SPL):**

$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right) \text{ dB}, \quad p_{\text{ref}} = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$$

**Sound Intensity Level (SIL):**

$$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_{\text{ref}}}\right) \text{ dB}, \quad I_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}\ \text{W/m}^2$$

**Why the factor of 20 vs 10?** Because intensity is proportional to pressure **squared** ($I \propto p^2$):

$$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p^2}{p_{\text{ref}}^2}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right) = L_p$$

So for a plane wave, **SPL (dB) = SIL (dB)**. This is a favourite KTU trick question.

### Principle 5 — Reflection, Transmission, and Absorption at a Boundary

When a sound wave strikes an interface between two media with impedances $Z_1$ and $Z_2$:

$$\text{Pressure Reflection Coefficient: } R = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$$

$$\text{Pressure Transmission Coefficient: } \tau = \frac{2 Z_2}{Z_2 + Z_1}$$

**Energy (Intensity) Reflection Coefficient:**

$$R_I = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$$

**Absorption Coefficient:**

$$\alpha = 1 - R_I = 1 - \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight (HMI design):**
> A perfectly matched impedance ($Z_1 = Z_2$) gives $\alpha = 1$ (full transmission, no echo). A perfectly hard boundary (e.g., concrete wall, $Z_2 \to \infty$) gives $R = 1$, $\alpha = 0$ (full reflection — the cause of flutter echo in empty rooms). Acoustic treatment of control rooms uses porous absorbers (foam, mineral wool) to bring $\alpha$ close to 1 across the speech band.

### Principle 6 — Reverberation Time (Sabine's Formula)

The **reverberation time** $T_{60}$ — the time for sound to decay by 60 dB after the source stops — is given by **Wallace Clement Sabine's classical equation**:

$$T_{60} = 0.161 \cdot \frac{V}{A} \quad \text{(SI units, V in m}^3\text{, A in m}^2\text{ sabins)}$$

where:
- $V$ = volume of the room (m³)
- $A = \sum_i S_i \alpha_i$ = total **absorption** (sum of surface area $\times$ absorption coefficient)
- The constant $0.161$ s/m comes from $24 \ln 10 / c_0$ with $c_0 \approx 343$ m/s.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Application — Optimal $T_{60}$ for Speech HMI:**
> - Recording/broadcast studios: $T_{60} \approx 0.3$ to $0.5$ s
> - Lecture halls / conference rooms: $T_{60} \approx 0.8$ to $1.0$ s
> - Concert halls (music): $T_{60} \approx 1.5$ to $2.5$ s
> - **Cathedrals (intentionally reverberant): $T_{60} > 5$ s** — a "design failure" for speech intelligibility, but a feature for choral music.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wave relation | $v = f \cdot \lambda$ | m/s | Fundamental, all wave types |
| 2 | Speed of sound in air | $c \approx 331.4 + 0.6\,\theta$ | m/s | $\theta$ in °C |
| 3 | Speed of sound in air (general) | $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T / M}$ | m/s | $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 8.314$, $M = 0.029$ kg/mol |
| 4 | Acoustic impedance | $Z = \rho_0 \cdot c$ | Rayl (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹) | $Z_{\text{air}} \approx 413$ Rayls |
| 5 | Intensity–pressure link | $I = p^2 / Z$ | W/m² | Plane-wave relation |
| 6 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p / p_0)$ | dB | $p_0 = 20\ \mu$Pa |
| 7 | Sound Intensity Level | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I / I_0)$ | dB | $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m² |
| 8 | SPL = SIL equivalence | $L_p = L_I$ (for plane wave) | dB | Common KTU trick |
| 9 | Combining levels (incoherent) | $L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\sum 10^{L_i/10}\right)$ | dB | Doubling identical sources $\Rightarrow +3$ dB |
| 10 | Combining levels (coherent) | $L_{\text{total}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\sum 10^{L_i/20}\right)$ | dB | Doubling $\Rightarrow +6$ dB |
| 11 | Reflection coefficient (pressure) | $R = (Z_2 - Z_1) / (Z_2 + Z_1)$ | dimensionless | Bounded in $[-1, +1]$ |
| 12 | Transmission coefficient (pressure) | $\tau = 2 Z_2 / (Z_2 + Z_1)$ | dimensionless | $1 + R = \tau$ |
| 13 | Absorption coefficient | $\alpha = 1 - R^2$ | dimensionless | $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ |
| 14 | Sabine's reverberation time | $T_{60} = 0.161\,V/A$ | seconds | $A$ in sabins (m²) |
| 15 | Inverse-square law (free field) | $L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 20 \log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$ | dB | Doubling distance $\Rightarrow -6$ dB |
| 16 | Loudness (Phon) | Equal-loudness contour to 1 kHz | phon | Fletcher–Munson curves |
| 17 | Pitch | $f$ perceived logarithmically | mel / Bark | Bark scale critical for HMI audio UX |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the `-6 dB per doubling of distance` rule matters in HMI:**
> A 70 dB SPL warning siren at 10 m drops to 64 dB at 20 m. To maintain the 70 dB level at 100 m, the source power must increase by a factor of 100, i.e., by **+20 dB** — a 100× amplifier. This dictates the power budget for outdoor emergency HMI.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of these Equations

- **Noise-induced hearing loss (NIHL) prevention:** OSHA mandates that exposure to 90 dB for 8 h requires hearing protection. The 5 dB exchange rule (US) or 3 dB exchange rule (EU) derives from the logarithmic energy-time trade-off: $T_{\text{allowed}} \propto 10^{-L/10}$.
- **Active Noise Control (ANC) headphones:** Use destructive interference of the anti-noise signal. The accuracy of the anti-phase waveform depends on the speed-of-sound travel time across the head ($\approx 0.5$ ms).
- **Speech Intelligibility (STI / STI-PA):** Directly impacted by reverberation, SNR, and masking — all governed by the equations above.
- **Sonar / Echolocation in HMI robotics:** Uses the round-trip time and speed of sound in water ($\approx 1480$ m/s) to map environments.
