# 🎯 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the Fourier Transform. State and explain the Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem as applied to HMI audio systems.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Fourier Transform** is a mathematical operator that converts a time-domain signal $x(t)$ into its frequency-domain representation $X(f)$, decomposing it into constituent sinusoids:

$$
X(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) \, e^{-j 2\pi f t} \, dt
$$

The inverse transform reconstructs $x(t)$ from $X(f)$.

**Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem** states: *A band-limited signal with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be perfectly reconstructed from its samples if and only if the sampling rate $f_s$ satisfies*:

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{max}
$$

The minimum rate $2f_{max}$ is the **Nyquist rate**; half this rate is the **Nyquist frequency**. If violated, **aliasing** occurs — high-frequency components fold back into the lower spectrum, causing irreversible distortion.

**HMI Application:** Speech occupies 300 Hz – 3.4 kHz, so $f_{max} = 3.4$ kHz, requiring $f_s \geq 6.8$ kHz. The standard telephony rate of 8 kHz adds a safety margin. (3 marks)

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Additive Synthesis and FM Synthesis. Give one HMI application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Additive Synthesis | FM Synthesis |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Sum of sine partials with controlled amplitudes | Modulate one sine with another |
| Math | $s(t) = \sum A_n \sin(2\pi f_n t + \phi_n)$ | $s(t) = A_c \sin(2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin 2\pi f_m t)$ |
| Parameters | $A_n, f_n, \phi_n$ per partial | Carrier $f_c$, modulator $f_m$, index $\beta$ |
| Computational cost | High (many oscillators) | Low (two oscillators) |
| Timbral control | Direct, intuitive | Indirect (Bessel-weighted sidebands) |

**HMI Applications:**
- **Additive:** Realistic engine sound synthesis in electric vehicles (summing 20+ engine harmonics).
- **FM:** Compact, expressive alert tones in mobile phones and medical devices (Yamaha DX7-style). (3 marks)

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **🅰️ QUESTION A** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**(a) Derive the trigonometric Fourier series for a symmetric square wave of amplitude $A$ and period $T_0$. Show that only odd harmonics are present and sketch the magnitude spectrum for the first five harmonics.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the signal:**

$$
x(t) =
\begin{cases}
+A, & 0 \leq t \leq T_0/2 \\
-A, & T_0/2 \leq t \leq T_0
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $a_0$:**

$$
a_0 = \frac{2}{T_0}\!\int_{0}^{T_0} x(t)\,dt = \frac{2}{T_0}\!\left[A\frac{T_0}{2} - A\frac{T_0}{2}\right] = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $a_n$ (cosine coefficients):** By symmetry of the square wave about its mean, the function is odd after subtracting the DC, hence $a_n = 0$ for all $n \geq 1$. (Full integral evaluation given in §3.1 confirms this.)

**Step 4 — Compute $b_n$ (sine coefficients):**

$$
b_n = \frac{2}{T_0}\!\int_{0}^{T_0}\!x(t)\sin(n\omega_0 t)\,dt = \frac{2A}{T_0}\!\left[\int_{0}^{T_0/2}\!\sin(n\omega_0 t)\,dt - \int_{T_0/2}^{T_0}\!\sin(n\omega_0 t)\,dt\right]
$$

$$
= \frac{2A}{n\omega_0 T_0}\left[-\cos(n\omega_0 t)\right]_{0}^{T_0/2} - \frac{2A}{n\omega_0 T_0}\left[-\cos(n\omega_0 t)\right]_{T_0/2}^{T_0}
$$

$$
= \frac{2A}{n\omega_0 T_0}\!\left[1 - \cos(n\pi) - \cos(2n\pi) + \cos(n\pi)\right] = \frac{2A}{n\pi}\!\left[1 - (-1)^n\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Final form:**

$$
b_n = \begin{cases} \dfrac{4A}{n\pi}, & n = 1, 3, 5, \ldots \\ 0, & n = 2, 4, 6, \ldots \end{cases}
$$

$$
\therefore \; x(t) = \frac{4A}{\pi}\!\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{\sin\!\left(2\pi(2k+1)f_0 t\right)}{2k+1}
$$

**Step 6 — Magnitude spectrum sketch (first five harmonics):**

| $n$ | $f_n$ | $C_n = \vert b_n \vert$ | $C_n / C_1$ (dB) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_0$ | $4A/\pi$ | 0 dB |
| 2 | $2f_0$ | 0 | $-\infty$ |
| 3 | $3f_0$ | $4A/(3\pi)$ | $-9.54$ dB |
| 4 | $4f_0$ | 0 | $-\infty$ |
| 5 | $5f_0$ | $4A/(5\pi)$ | $-13.98$ dB |

**Step 7 — Conclusion:** Only odd harmonics exist; amplitude decays as $1/n$. (7 marks)

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**(b) Explain the STFT (Short-Time Fourier Transform) and its role in HMI speech recognition front-ends. State the Gabor limit.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept:** The **STFT** applies a sliding window $w(t-\tau)$ of finite duration to a non-stationary signal, then performs FFT on each windowed segment. Mathematically:

$$
X(\tau, f) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) \, w(t - \tau) \, e^{-j 2\pi f t} \, dt
$$

The output $X(\tau, f)$ is a 2D complex function of both time-shift $\tau$ and frequency $f$. The squared magnitude $\vert X(\tau, f) \vert^2$ is the **spectrogram**.

**Step 2 — Window Selection:** Common choices include **Hamming**, **Hann**, and **Blackman** windows. Each trades main-lobe width (frequency resolution) against side-lobe level (spectral leakage).

**Step 3 — HMI Speech Front-End Role:**
1. **Framing:** Speech is sliced into 20–30 ms frames (~160–240 samples at 8 kHz). The duration balances time and frequency resolution.
2. **STFT Computation:** Each frame is windowed and FFT'd.
3. **Feature Extraction:** The power spectrum is converted to **Mel-frequency cepstral coefficients (MFCCs)** — perceptually scaled features mimicking cochlear filtering.
4. **Classification:** MFCCs feed HMMs, GMMs, or neural networks (RNN/Transformer) for phoneme/word recognition.
5. **Keyword Spotting:** In "Hey Siri" or "OK Google," the STFT-MFCC pipeline runs continuously at low power, detecting wake-words.

**Step 4 — Gabor (Heisenberg) Uncertainty Limit:** A signal cannot be localized simultaneously in time and frequency. The product of standard deviations is bounded:

$$
\sigma_t \cdot \sigma_f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}
$$

A 25 ms window gives $\sigma_f \geq 1/(4\pi \cdot 0.025) \approx 3.18$ Hz, while a 5 ms window yields $\sigma_f \geq 15.9$ Hz but $\sigma_t = 5$ ms. The window length is chosen to suit the application: **phoneme transitions** demand short windows; **vowel formants** benefit from long windows. (7 marks)

---

### **🅱️ QUESTION B** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model] *(Alternative Choice)*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the Additive Synthesis technique. A musical note has fundamental $f_0 = 440$ Hz and harmonics with amplitudes $A_1 = 1, A_2 = 0.5, A_3 = 0.3, A_4 = 0.15$ (all others negligible). Write the synthesis equation and compute the instantaneous pressure for $t = 1$ ms if $A_n$ are pressure amplitudes in Pa.**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (textual):**

```
[Harmonic 1: f0, A1] ─┐
[Harmonic 2: 2f0, A2]─┤
[Harmonic 3: 3f0, A3]─┼──► [Summing Junction] ──► [Output s(t)]
[Harmonic 4: 4f0, A4]─┤
[ADSR Envelope] ──────┘ (modulates A_n)
```

**Synthesis Equation:**

$$
s(t) = \sum_{n=1}^{4} A_n \sin(2\pi n f_0 t)
$$

$$
s(t) = 1 \sin(2\pi \cdot 440 t) + 0.5 \sin(2\pi \cdot 880 t) + 0.3 \sin(2\pi \cdot 1320 t) + 0.15 \sin(2\pi \cdot 1760 t)
$$

**Evaluation at $t = 0.001$ s:**

Compute each term:

- $2\pi \cdot 440 \cdot 0.001 = 0.88\pi = 2.7646$ rad → $\sin(2.7646) = 0.3681$
- $2\pi \cdot 880 \cdot 0.001 = 1.76\pi = 5.5292$ rad → $\sin(5.5292) = -0.7063$
- $2\pi \cdot 1320 \cdot 0.001 = 2.64\pi = 8.2938$ rad → $\sin(8.2938) = 0.9252$
- $2\pi \cdot 1760 \cdot 0.001 = 3.52\pi = 11.0584$ rad → $\sin(11.0584) = -0.9975$

$$
s(0.001) = 1(0.3681) + 0.5(-0.7063) + 0.3(0.9252) + 0.15(-0.9975)
$$

$$
= 0.3681 - 0.3532 + 0.2776 - 0.1496 = 0.1429 \text{ Pa}
$$

(7 marks)

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**(b) Describe Frequency Modulation (FM) Synthesis with its mathematical model. Using Carson's rule, estimate the bandwidth for $f_c = 500$ Hz, $f_m = 200$ Hz, modulation index $\beta = 3$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mathematical Model:** FM synthesis modulates the *frequency* of a carrier sine wave with another sine (the modulator):

$$
s(t) = A_c \sin\!\left( 2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t) \right)
$$

- $A_c$ = carrier amplitude
- $f_c$ = carrier frequency (perceived pitch)
- $f_m$ = modulator frequency
- $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m}$ = **modulation index** (where $\Delta f$ is peak frequency deviation)
- $\beta$ controls *timbre*; $f_m$ controls spectral density.

**Step 2 — Spectrum:** The output spectrum contains sidebands at $f_c \pm n f_m$ with amplitudes $J_n(\beta)$ (Bessel functions of the first kind, $n$-th order). For large $\beta$, the number of significant sidebands grows, producing a brighter, more complex timbre.

**Step 3 — Carson's Rule for Bandwidth:**

$$
BW \approx 2(\beta + 1) f_m = 2(\Delta f + f_m)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values:**

$$
BW = 2(3 + 1) \times 200 = 2 \times 4 \times 200 = 1600 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** The FM tone has significant spectral energy from $f_c - 8 f_m = 500 - 1600 = -1100$ Hz (i.e., 1100 Hz) to $f_c + 8 f_m = 500 + 1600 = 2100$ Hz. The tone will sound rich and bell-like, suitable for an HMI confirmation chime.

(7 marks)

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students most commonly lose marks in "Analysis and Synthesis of Sound" questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the DC term** ($a_0/2$) when computing Fourier series — the examiner explicitly looks for it.
> 2. **Units confusion:** Amplitude in Pa vs. normalized — always state units.
> 3. **Sign error in phase:** $\phi_n = \tan^{-1}(-b_n/a_n)$, **not** $b_n/a_n$. The negative sign is critical.
> 4. **Nyquist mis-statement:** Writing $f_s = 2 f_{max}$ instead of $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — the inequality matters.
> 5. **STFT confusion:** Forgetting that STFT produces a *time-frequency* (2D) representation, not a pure spectrum.
> 6. **FM bandwidth:** Using $2 \beta f_m$ instead of $2(\beta+1) f_m$ — the "1" is the carrier itself, easily missed.
> 7. **Confusing Parseval's theorem with the Fourier series formula** — they are related but distinct identities.
> 8. **No sketch in spectrum questions:** KTU examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for the magnitude spectrum sketch. **Always draw it.**

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Analysis = Decomposition**; **Synthesis = Reconstruction** — the inverse pair.
- ✅ Any periodic signal = sum of sines + cosines (Fourier Series). Any signal = integral of complex exponentials (Fourier Transform).
- ✅ Fourier coefficients: $a_n, b_n$ via integration over one period; $C_n = \sqrt{a_n^2 + b_n^2}$.
- ✅ DFT for digital signals; FFT = $O(N \log_2 N)$ algorithm.
- ✅ STFT = sliding window + FFT → spectrogram. Governed by the Gabor limit $\sigma_t \sigma_f \geq 1/(4\pi)$.
- ✅ Nyquist rate $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$; telephone = 8 kHz; CD audio = 44.1 kHz.
- ✅ Five synthesis families: **A**dditive, **S**ubtractive, **F**M, **W**avetable, **P**hysical modeling.
- ✅ Additive: $s(t) = \sum A_n(t) \sin(2\pi f_n t + \phi_n(t))$.
- ✅ FM: $s(t) = A_c \sin(2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin 2\pi f_m t)$; sidebands at $f_c \pm n f_m$.
- ✅ Carson's rule: $BW = 2(\beta+1) f_m$.
- ✅ ADSR envelope: Attack → Decay → Sustain → Release.
- ✅ HMI warning tones typically use **2 kHz – 4 kHz** band (ear's most sensitive region).
- ✅ Speed of sound in air (20°C): $v = 343$ m/s.
- ✅ Hearing range: **20 Hz – 20 kHz**; Speech: 300 Hz – 3.4 kHz.
- ✅ Sound Pressure Level: $L_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$, $p_0 = 20 \mu$Pa.
- ✅ Parseval's theorem: total power in time domain = total power in frequency domain.
- ✅ HMI applications: medical alarms, automotive alerts, voice assistants, screen readers, engine sound synthesis, fault diagnosis.
